1990.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 1.8% of GDP. Manageable. New Order Indonesia maintained fiscal discipline. Oil revenues important for fiscal balance but declining as share of revenue. Suharto government prioritized balanced budgets. Public debt moderate at ~50-60% of GDP. Development budget financed partly by aid and loans.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Public Expenditure Review 1990.

1991.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 1.5% of GDP. Small and manageable. Development finance (APBN) budget discipline maintained. Suharto's technocrats (Berkeley Mafia economists) enforced conservative fiscal policy. Oil revenues declining but tax revenues growing with economic expansion.

1992.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 0.9% of GDP. Near balance. Strong revenue growth from expanding economy. New Order fiscal conservatism preventing deficit accumulation. Spending on education and infrastructure managed within means.

1993.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 0.5% of GDP. Nearly balanced. Strong GDP growth expanding tax base. Oil/gas revenues contributing. Development spending managed. Indonesia maintaining reputation for fiscal prudence. IMF consultations consistently positive.

1994.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 0.2% of GDP. Nearly balanced budget. Strong economy, rising tax revenues. Capital market development allowing non-oil financing. Privatization proceeds supplementing revenues. Low public debt.

1995.assessment: Fiscal surplus approximately 0.3% of GDP. Rare surplus. Strong revenue. Tax reforms (VAT expansion) successful. Oil/gas revenues still significant. Fiscal position praised by IMF. Public debt declining as share of GDP. Off-budget items (Pertamina, state enterprises) not fully captured — actual position may be more constrained.

1996.assessment: Fiscal surplus approximately 0.2% of GDP. Balanced/near-surplus. Strong growth boosting revenues. Pre-crisis appearance of fiscal health. However, off-balance-sheet liabilities (state bank NPLs, BPPN future costs) not recognized. Banking sector accumulating contingent liabilities that would materialize in crisis.

sources.citation: Kenward, L. Indonesia: From Crisis to Recovery. 2000.

1997.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 0.3% of GDP — pre-crisis still manageable. Crisis began H2 1997; initial fiscal costs limited. Bank Indonesia guarantees being issued (Bantuan Likuiditas Bank Indonesia — BLBI). Habibie pressures for subsidies mounting. Suharto still in power; crisis not yet reflected in full-year accounts.

sources.citation: Rosser, A. The Politics of Economic Liberalisation in Indonesia. 2002.

1998.assessment: Fiscal deficit surged to approximately 5.2% of GDP. Crisis-year fiscal collapse. Bank bailout (BLBI — Bantuan Likuiditas Bank Indonesia) transferred enormous costs to government: ~Rp 144 trillion (~$20B at crisis exchange rates). GDP denominator collapsed 56% in USD terms inflating deficit ratio. Suharto fell; Habibie emergency spending. IMF program fiscal conditions negotiated. Public debt exploded from bank recapitalization.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Crisis and Fiscal Costs 1998.
Djiwandono, J.S. Bank Indonesia and the Crisis. 2005.

1999.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 6.4% of GDP — deepest in crisis. Bank recapitalization costs continuing. IBRA managing failed bank assets. Social safety net (JPS — Jaring Pengaman Sosial) emergency spending. Habibie then Wahid government. IMF-supported fiscal program providing financing. Public debt approaching 100% of GDP from crisis costs.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Crisis Fiscal Assessment 1999.

2000.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 4.8% of GDP. Declining from peak but still large. Recovery beginning; tax revenues recovering. Fuel subsidies very large (~4% of GDP). IBRA divesting bank assets slowly. Decentralization fiscal transfers beginning. Wahid government unstable; fiscal discipline constrained. Public debt ~80% of GDP.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Public Expenditure Analysis 2001.

2001.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 3.4% of GDP. Declining. Megawati assumed presidency July 2001; IMF program restored. Fiscal consolidation underway. Fuel subsidies reduced. Bank privatization proceeds. Decentralization transfers implemented (DAU, DAK). Public debt declining from peak.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Decentralization Fiscal Review 2002.

2002.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 2.2% of GDP. Continuing consolidation. IMF program last year (concluded 2003). Fuel subsidies still large. Tax revenues growing. IBRA recovering assets. Public debt declining. Bali bombings limited fiscal impact. Megawati maintaining discipline.

2003.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 1.8% of GDP. IMF program concluded; Indonesia managing independently. Tax revenues improving. Fuel subsidies still large but managed. KPK (anti-corruption) operational reducing leakage. Public debt now ~55-60% of GDP. Fiscal position increasingly sustainable.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Public Expenditure Review 2003.

2004.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 1.3% of GDP. Small and manageable. SBY elected; committed to fiscal prudence. Revenue improving with commodity prices. Fuel subsidies rising with oil prices (partially offset). New State Budget Law (2003) requiring parliamentary approval improving accountability. Debt declining toward 50% of GDP.

2005.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 0.8% of GDP. Small. Fuel subsidy reform (Oct 2005) savings ~1.5% GDP. Indonesia repaid IMF early. Tsunami reconstruction spending offset by subsidy savings. Revenue buoyant with commodity boom. Public debt now ~42% of GDP — declining rapidly.

2006.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 1.0% of GDP. Small. Fuel subsidies re-expanded as oil prices rose. Revenue strong from commodity boom. Lapindo mudflow government response costs limited. Infrastructure investment beginning to grow. Public debt now ~35% of GDP — approaching sustainable threshold.

2007.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 1.3% of GDP. Small. Strong commodity revenues. Fuel subsidies growing with oil prices. Infrastructure investment (RPJM) beginning. Education budget (20% of national budget mandate) being met. Public debt now ~30% of GDP. Positive fiscal trajectory.

sources.citation: Constitution Amendment requiring 20% education budget allocation.

2008.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 0.1% of GDP — near balance. H1 oil price spike ($147/barrel) cost the fuel subsidy budget massively (~4% GDP in subsidies). H2 commodity collapse reduced revenues but also reduced subsidy costs. G-20 fiscal crisis response coordination. Public debt now ~27% of GDP.

2009.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 1.6% of GDP. Small. GFC fiscal stimulus deployed. Revenue somewhat reduced from commodity price collapse. Social protection spending increased. GFC response managed without large fiscal deterioration. Public debt ~26-28% of GDP. Indonesia avoided severe fiscal stress.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Fiscal Stimulus Assessment 2009.

2010.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 0.7% of GDP. Small. Strong recovery; revenue buoyant. Infrastructure spending increasing. Fuel subsidy remaining large but managed. Public debt now ~25% of GDP — among lowest in G-20. Indonesia in strong fiscal position.

2011.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 1.1% of GDP. Small. Fuel subsidy growing with oil prices. Infrastructure budget increasing. Education budget at 20% constitutional requirement. Health budget growing. Public debt stabilizing ~24% of GDP. Revenue buoyant from commodity super-cycle.

2012.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 1.9% of GDP. Widening. Fuel subsidy costs exploded: Rp 245 trillion (~2.5% GDP) as oil prices high and subsidy unchanged. Failed attempt to raise fuel prices (April 2012 — withdrew bill). Current account shifting to deficit. Infrastructure budget underspent despite allocation.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Fiscal Risks 2012 (fuel subsidy analysis).

2013.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 2.3% of GDP. Largest in SBY era. Fuel subsidy partially reduced (June 2013) saving ~0.5% GDP but full costs still very high. Election-year spending pressures. Taper tantrum affected rupiah and fiscal calculations. Revenue growth slowing with commodity prices. Debt ~24% of GDP.

2014.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 2.1% of GDP. Managed within 3% legal ceiling. Jokowi elected; December 2014 fuel subsidy reform transformational — freed ~1.5-2% GDP for reallocation to infrastructure. Deficit declined despite election-year spending. Public debt ~24% GDP.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Fiscal Policy Transformation 2015 (fuel subsidy reform).

2015.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 2.6% of GDP. Large but managed. Revenue shortfall from commodity price collapse. Fuel subsidy savings offset by infrastructure investment increase. Revenue target missed. Tax ratio declining. Infrastructure budget growing (Jokowi's flagship). Debt ~26% GDP.

2016.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 2.5% of GDP. Managed. Tax amnesty (July 2016) yielded Rp 135 trillion revenue; largest globally. Mid-year budget revision required as revenue fell short. Automatic budget adjustment mechanism triggered. Debt ~27% GDP. Infrastructure spending high.

sources.citation: Tax amnesty program results: $9.7 billion revenue, Rp 4,881 trillion repatriated assets declared.

2017.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 2.5% of GDP. Stable within legal ceiling. Revenue growing from post-amnesty expansion. Infrastructure spending record high. Education 20% maintained. Social protection (PKH conditional cash transfer) expanding. Debt ~27% GDP. Managed within legal 3% ceiling.

2018.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 1.8% of GDP. Improving. Revenue buoyant from commodity price recovery. Tax compliance improving. VAT revenue strong. Fuel subsidy costs contained with partial managed price approach. Debt ~29% of GDP — still low.

2019.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 2.2% of GDP. Small. Pre-COVID fiscal position sound. Revenue growth from digital economy taxation (OTT tax). Infrastructure budget high. Nusantara capital relocation announced; early spending beginning. Public debt ~30% of GDP. Social protection expanded.

2020.assessment: Fiscal deficit 6.1% of GDP — breaching 3% legal ceiling. Parliament suspended ceiling for COVID emergency. PEN (National Economic Recovery) program: Rp 695 trillion ($47B) stimulus. Healthcare, social protection, economic recovery spending. Revenue collapsed from GDP decline. Public debt ~38% of GDP — still manageable but rising.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia COVID Fiscal Response 2020.

2021.assessment: Fiscal deficit 4.6% of GDP. Declining from 2020 peak. Revenue recovering from commodity boom. PEN program continuing. Vaccination spending large. Delta wave required additional health spending. Government committed to return to 3% ceiling by 2023. Public debt ~40% GDP — elevated but manageable.

sources.citation: Ministry of Finance Indonesia. APBN 2021 realization.

2022.assessment: Fiscal deficit 2.4% of GDP. Returned below 3% legal ceiling one year ahead of schedule. Revenue windfall from commodity super-cycle (coal, nickel, palm oil export taxes). G-20 presidency year. Fuel subsidy costs re-expanded with energy price spike; government maintained cap requiring large subsidy budget. Public debt declining: ~38% GDP.

sources.citation: Ministry of Finance Indonesia. APBN 2022 realization.

2023.assessment: Fiscal deficit 1.7% of GDP. Well within legal ceiling. Revenue strong from commodity/mining; coal windfall partially persisting. Infrastructure spending high. Health and social protection maintained. Pre-election (2024) fiscal expansion managed within rules. Public debt ~37% GDP. Strong fiscal position.

sources.citation: Ministry of Finance Indonesia. APBN 2023 realization.

2024.assessment: Fiscal deficit approximately 2.7% of GDP. Within legal ceiling. Prabowo government (from October 2024) started free meals program with fiscal cost ~0.5-0.8% GDP. Revenue stable. Infrastructure spending continued. Public debt estimated ~38-40% GDP. Manageable but program costs monitoring required.

sources.citation: Ministry of Finance Indonesia. APBN 2024 realization.

2025.assessment: Fiscal deficit projected 2.5-2.9% of GDP. Within legal ceiling. Free meals program full-year cost ~Rp 71 trillion (~0.5% GDP). Infrastructure continuing. Health and social protection. Revenue from commodity sector moderating. Debt projected ~39-41% GDP. IMF recommends fiscal vigilance as Prabowo programs expand spending.

1990.assessment: GDP per capita $622 USD. Indonesia in mid-Suharto New Order era. Strong economic growth averaging ~7% annually through the late 1980s. Oil revenue declining as share of economy; manufacturing exports growing. Population ~182 million. Poverty declining. Economic baseline well above regional peers but still low-middle income.

sources.citation: Booth, A. The Indonesian Economy in the Nineteenth and Twentieth Centuries. 1998.
Hill, H. The Indonesian Economy. 2nd ed. Cambridge University Press. 2000.

1991.assessment: GDP per capita $689. Continued strong growth under Suharto New Order. Manufacturing sector expanding; export-led growth. Financial sector deregulation (PAKTO 1988) driving credit expansion. Regional development improving. Poverty rate declining from ~40% in 1976 to ~15% by 1990.

1992.assessment: GDP per capita $744. Continued economic expansion. Conglomerate sector (Salim Group, Astra etc.) dominant. Indonesia graduating from IDA-only borrower. Corruption and cronyism entrenched but growth sustained. Investment climate positive; FDI growing.

sources.citation: Schwarz, A. A Nation in Waiting. 1994.

1993.assessment: GDP per capita $838. Indonesia meeting World Bank middle-income threshold. Repelita VI (sixth five-year plan) emphasizing industrial deepening. Telecommunications and aviation sectors privatized partially. Suharto regime at political apex. APEC membership; regional integration deepening.

1994.assessment: GDP per capita $908. Indonesia approaching $1,000 per capita. Financial sector expansion; credit growing rapidly — seeds of future vulnerability. Capital account liberalization facilitating short-term borrowing. Labour-intensive manufacturing exports growing. Dili Santa Cruz massacre (1991) international criticism of regime.

sources.citation: Robison, R. and Hadiz, V. Reorganising Power in Indonesia. 2004.

1995.assessment: GDP per capita $1,035. Crossed $1,000 threshold. IMF Article IV consultations positive. Regional boom; investment inflows strong. Short-term external debt building rapidly. Conglomerate sector borrowing heavily offshore. Political succession concerns beginning as Suharto aged. Indonesia praised as 'Asian Tiger cub'.

sources.citation: World Bank. The East Asian Miracle. 1993.

1996.assessment: GDP per capita $1,154. Near pre-crisis peak. Financial sector overextension. Current account deficit widening. PDI (Megawati's party) office stormed July 1996 — political tensions rising. Conglomerate debt-to-equity ratios dangerously high. Thailand baht cracking; regional vulnerability unrecognized by most forecasters.

sources.citation: Radelet, S. and Sachs, J. The East Asian Financial Crisis. NBER. 1998.

1997.assessment: GDP per capita $1,045. Asian Financial Crisis hit July 1997; rupiah devalued. By year-end rupiah had fallen from Rp 2,400/$ to ~Rp 5,000/$. IMF program negotiated October 1997. Annual average still $1,045 but sharp Q4 deterioration. Suharto refused IMF conditions initially.

sources.citation: Radelet, S. and Sachs, J. The East Asian Financial Crisis. NBER. 1998.

1998.assessment: GDP per capita collapsed to $455 — a fall of 56.5% from 1997 in USD terms (partly currency depreciation). Rupiah at Rp 14,000-17,000/$. Real GDP contracted -13.6%. Suharto resigned May 21, 1998 after 32 years. Habibie assumed presidency. May 1998 riots killed ~1,000. IMF structural adjustment program imposed. Poverty rose sharply — tens of millions fell into poverty.

sources.citation: IMF. Indonesia: The Crisis and Adjustment. 1999.
Hill, H. The Indonesian Economy in Crisis. 1999.
World Bank. Indonesia in Crisis: A Macroeconomic Update. 1998.

1999.assessment: GDP per capita $700. Partial recovery as rupiah stabilized. Real GDP contracted further (-0.6% in 1999). Habibie government; East Timor referendum August 1999 (independence); Wahid elected President October 1999. Democratic transition underway. IBRA managing failed banks. Economy stabilizing but fragile. Reformasi era beginning.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia: From Crisis to Opportunity. 1999.

2000.assessment: GDP per capita $790. Real GDP grew 4.9%. Wahid presidency; democratic consolidation. Sectarian violence in Maluku and Kalimantan ongoing. Decentralization laws implemented. IMF program continuing. External debt restructuring. Banking sector recapitalization. Political instability under Wahid government constraining recovery.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia: Country Assistance Strategy 2001.

2001.assessment: GDP per capita $835. Real GDP grew 3.6%. Wahid impeached July 2001; Megawati Sukarnoputri became President. Political transition managed constitutionally — landmark for democracy. Continued sectarian violence in outer islands. Decentralization deepening. 9/11 global shock limited impact on Indonesia. Rupiah volatile.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Indonesia: Ending Repression in Irian Jaya. 2001.

2002.assessment: GDP per capita $887. Real GDP grew 4.5%. Megawati presidency. Bali bombings October 2002 — JI/al-Qaeda attack killing 202; severe blow to tourism and investor confidence. Aceh conflict intensifying; martial law declared 2003. East Timor independence recognized; Indonesia adjusting to new neighbour. KPK (anti-corruption) legislation passed.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Indonesia Backgrounder: How the Jemaah Islamiyah Terrorist Network Operates. 2002.

2003.assessment: GDP per capita $1,016. Crossed $1,000 again. Real GDP grew 4.8%. Jakarta Marriott bombing August 2003. Aceh martial law declared May 2003. Economic recovery consolidating. Commodity prices beginning to rise. 2004 elections approaching. IMF program concluded. Indonesia beginning to stand on own feet economically.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia: Maintaining Stability, Deepening Reforms. 2003.

2004.assessment: GDP per capita $1,134. Real GDP grew 5.0%. First direct presidential election September 2004; Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono (SBY) won. Democratic consolidation milestone. December 26 tsunami devastating Aceh and North Sumatra (230,000+ deaths). Commodities boom beginning to boost revenues.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Aceh: A New Chance for Peace. 2005.

2005.assessment: GDP per capita $1,263. Real GDP grew 5.7%. Helsinki MoU signed August 2005 ending 30-year Aceh conflict. Tsunami reconstruction boosting investment in Aceh. Fuel subsidy reform (partial). SBY government consolidating. KPK (Corruption Eradication Commission) becoming operational. Commodity prices continuing to rise. Indonesia repaid IMF debt early.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia: Economic and Social Update 2005.

2006.assessment: GDP per capita $1,589. Real GDP grew 5.5%. Commodities boom boosting coal and palm oil exports. Papua conflict continuing at low level. Lapindo mudflow disaster in East Java (May 2006). Rupiah stable; fiscal consolidation continuing. Investment climate improving. G-20 membership important for status.

2007.assessment: GDP per capita $1,872. Real GDP grew 6.3%. Indonesia began sustained 6%+ growth phase. Commodity boom (coal, palm oil, nickel) driving revenues. Global financial crisis early signals; Indonesia's limited financial integration provided protection. Elections 2009 approaching; political stability high.

2008.assessment: GDP per capita $2,165. Crossed lower-middle-income threshold. Real GDP grew 6.0% despite global financial crisis. Indonesia's banking sector relatively isolated from subprime exposure. Commodity price spike (coal, palm oil) boosted revenues. Rupiah depreciated in Q4 but recovered quickly. G-20 member in global crisis response.

2009.assessment: GDP per capita $2,264. One of only 3 G-20 economies to maintain positive growth in 2009 global crisis (4.6% real). SBY re-elected in first-round victory July 2009. Fiscal stimulus deployed. External debt declining. Democracy widely praised as stable. Domestic consumption-driven growth resilient.

2010.assessment: GDP per capita $3,122. Rapid USD increase partly from rupiah appreciation. Real GDP grew 6.2%. Indonesia increasingly recognized as BRICS-equivalent emerging market. Commodity super-cycle benefiting coal, palm oil, rubber exports. Strong FDI inflows. Tax revenue improving. Middle class expanding rapidly.

sources.citation: McKinsey Global Institute. The Archipelago Economy: Unleashing Indonesia's Potential. 2012.

2011.assessment: GDP per capita $3,689. Commodity super-cycle near peak. Real GDP grew 6.2%. Indonesia became 16th largest economy globally. Rupiah strong. Investment grade credit rating restored by Fitch and Moody's after 14 years. Foreign reserves at record levels. SBY second term governance improving.

sources.citation: Fitch Ratings/Moody's. Indonesia investment grade restoration 2011.

2012.assessment: GDP per capita $3,704. Growth slowing slightly (6.0% real). Commodity prices beginning to ease. Current account deficit re-emerging. Infrastructure bottlenecks constraining growth. Corruption cases increasing under KPK. Labour unrest over wages. Political jockeying for 2014 elections beginning.

2013.assessment: GDP per capita $3,624. Taper tantrum (May 2013): Fed tightening signal caused rupiah and markets to plunge. Real GDP 5.6%. Fuel subsidy reform attempted; partial implementation. Current account deficit widened. Bank Indonesia raised rates. Growing infrastructure deficit. KPK Corruption cases involving prominent politicians accelerating.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Economic Quarterly 2013 Q3 (Taper Tantrum).

2014.assessment: GDP per capita $3,492. Commodity price decline accelerating. Real GDP 5.0%. Joko Widodo (Jokowi) elected President July 2014 in competitive election. SBY era ended; new populist-technocrat leadership. Fuel subsidy reform finally implemented December 2014 (80% reduction). Rupiah declining. EITI compliance awarded.

2015.assessment: GDP per capita $3,333. Commodity cycle downturn deepening. Coal and palm oil prices fell sharply. Rupiah hit 14,000/USD — approaching 1998 crisis levels nominally. Real GDP 4.9% — slowest since crisis era. Jokowi infrastructure push beginning. China slowdown affecting exports. Tax amnesty discussed.

2016.assessment: GDP per capita $3,564. Recovery; real GDP 5.0%. Tax amnesty program yielded $9.7B (largest globally). Capital repatriation boosted financial sector. Jakarta gubernatorial election controversy (Ahok/Basuki Tjahaja Purnama — Chinese-Christian — charged with blasphemy following Islamist mobilization). Political populism rising.

2017.assessment: GDP per capita $3,847. Real GDP 5.1%. Commodity prices recovering. Infrastructure investment increasing. Jokowi One-Stop Service reform improving business environment. Ahok convicted May 2017; sectarian politics deepened. 2019 election campaign beginning. Nickel and coal exports strong.

2018.assessment: GDP per capita $3,894. Real GDP 5.2%. Fed tightening caused rupiah depreciation to 15,000/$. Current account deficit widened. Bank Indonesia rate hikes. Lombok and Palu earthquakes (August-October 2018) — significant human cost. Trade tensions (US-China) affecting Indonesia through commodity channels.

2019.assessment: GDP per capita $4,135 — historical peak for Indonesia in current USD terms. Real GDP 5.0%. Jokowi re-elected April 2019 in close, polarized contest against Prabowo. Capital city relocation to Nusantara (East Kalimantan) announced. Commodity prices stable. Investment reaching record levels. Population 270 million — 4th largest globally.

2020.assessment: GDP per capita $3,870. COVID-19 pandemic; real GDP -2.1% — first negative growth since 1998 crisis. Stimulus packages; social protection expanded. Healthcare system strained. Commodity exports partially maintained. Rupiah depreciated; BI cut rates. PSBB (large-scale social restrictions) mobility restrictions. Indonesia avoided worst scenarios.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Economic Quarterly COVID Special 2020.

2021.assessment: GDP per capita $4,350. Above pre-COVID peak. Real GDP +3.7%; recovery driven by commodity price spike (nickel, coal, palm oil). Vaccination program scaled. Delta wave severe (July 2021). Nickel export ban driving battery supply chain investment. G-20 presidency announced for 2022.

2022.assessment: GDP per capita $4,784. New high. Real GDP +5.3%. Commodity super-cycle (Russia-Ukraine war) drove coal and palm oil prices to records. Indonesia hosted G-20 Presidency. Coal export revenues record-high. Nickel investment boom (EV battery supply chains). Jokowi economic nationalism policies (downstream processing mandates).

2023.assessment: GDP per capita $4,919. New historical high. Real GDP +5.0%. Commodity prices moderating from 2022 peaks. Prabowo Subianto won February 2024 presidential election (result known December 2023). Nusantara new capital construction continuing. Nickel downstream processing driving FDI. Inflation under control. Election campaign period.

2024.assessment: GDP per capita estimated ~$5,020 (World Bank estimate). Real GDP approximately 5.0%. Prabowo Subianto inaugurated October 2024 — democratic transfer from Jokowi. Free school meals program (major Prabowo promise) implementation begun. Commodity revenues moderating. Nusantara capital construction continuing. Approaching upper-middle-income threshold ($4,466 World Bank).

2025.assessment: GDP per capita projected ~$5,100-5,200. Real GDP growth approximately 4.8-5.2%. Prabowo government first full year. Free meals program fiscal impact. Commodity revenues moderating. Nickel-EV supply chain investment continuing. Inflation manageable. Indonesia on track toward upper-middle-income status. Estimated above 2019 pre-COVID peak.

1990.assessment: Inflation 7.83%. Moderate inflation under Suharto New Order. Managed exchange rate (crawling peg against USD) kept inflation in check. Food price stability an explicit New Order policy goal. Structurally moderate inflation in the 7-10% range through much of this decade.

sources.citation: Hill, H. The Indonesian Economy. Cambridge University Press. 2000.

1991.assessment: Inflation 9.42%. Upper range of moderate. Credit expansion following PAKTO 1988 financial deregulation fueling domestic demand. Bank Indonesia tightening. Still within policy tolerance. Food prices primary driver.

1992.assessment: Inflation 7.53%. Moderate, declining from 1991 peak. Monetary tightening effective. Exchange rate crawling peg maintaining stability. Indonesia widely praised for macroeconomic stability in early 1990s.

1993.assessment: Inflation 9.74%. Near double-digits; food price pressures. El Nino impacts on agricultural production. Monetary expansion supporting growth. Still managed within broad stability parameters.

1994.assessment: Inflation 8.53%. Moderate. Continued strong growth with managed inflation. Rice price stability maintained through BULOG (state logistics agency) interventions. Strong investment inflows partly offset by sterilization.

1995.assessment: Inflation 9.42%. Near double digits again; credit boom fueling demand. Capital inflows increasing money supply. Asset prices (land, equities) rising rapidly. Short-term offshore borrowing by conglomerates building currency mismatch risk.

1996.assessment: Inflation 7.97%. Moderate. Pre-crisis last 'normal' year. Bank Indonesia managing monetary aggregates. Crawling peg maintained. Food prices stable from good harvest. Financial sector continuing to expand with significant offshore borrowing building.

1997.assessment: Annual average inflation 6.23% — deceptively low given H2 crisis. Crisis began July 1997 with rupiah attack; Bank Indonesia defended until August then floated. By December rupiah at Rp 5,000/$ (from 2,400). Annual CPI average still moderate as crisis concentrated in Q4. Inflation surge came in 1998.

1998.assessment: Inflation 58.45% — worst since 1960s. Rupiah collapsed to Rp 16,000-17,000/$. Import prices surged. Food security crisis; panic buying. BULOG unable to hold rice prices. Suharto resigned May 21, 1998. Bank runs, capital flight. IMF structural adjustment program with painful price liberalization. Fuel prices raised 71% in May 1998. Poverty increased dramatically.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia in Crisis: A Macroeconomic Update. 1998.

1999.assessment: Inflation 20.49%. Declining from 1998 crisis peak. Rupiah stabilizing around Rp 7,000-8,000/$. IMF program stabilizing. Habibie government; democratic transition. Food prices elevated but declining. Bank Indonesia gaining independence (Law 23/1999 establishing BI independence).

2000.assessment: Inflation 3.72%. Dramatic disinflation; back to low range. Rupiah stabilizing; monetary policy normalizing. Bank Indonesia independent, tightening. International commodity prices low. Food supplies normalizing. Wahid government managing economic recovery.

2001.assessment: Inflation 11.50%. Spike from political uncertainty (Wahid impeachment July 2001). Rupiah depreciated under Wahid's erratic governance. 9/11 global uncertainty added to pressure. Bank Indonesia raised rates. Megawati government restored stability by year-end. Fuel price increases passed through to CPI.

2002.assessment: Inflation 11.87%. Elevated. Bali bombings (October 2002) added uncertainty. Fuel price adjustments. Decentralization fiscal transfers creating local price pressures. Megawati government managed stability. Bank Indonesia raised SBI rates. Gradual trend toward price stability.

2003.assessment: Inflation 6.77%. Back to moderate range. Bank Indonesia inflation targeting framework being developed. Rupiah stable. IMF program concluded 2003. Macroeconomic stability consolidating. Food prices moderate. Pre-election (2004) stability maintained.

2004.assessment: Inflation 6.24%. Moderate. First direct presidential election year; SBY elected. Bank Indonesia adopting inflation targeting formally. December tsunami (Aceh) limited inflation impact.

2005.assessment: Inflation 10.45%. Spike from fuel subsidy reform. SBY government reduced fuel subsidies in October 2005 — fuel prices rose ~120%. CPI surged. Bank Indonesia raised interest rates aggressively. Temporary but significant inflationary pressure. Inflation targeting credibility tested but maintained.

2006.assessment: Inflation 13.11%. Lagged effects of 2005 fuel price increases. Bank Indonesia tightening throughout year. Food prices elevated. Lapindo mudflow disaster (East Java) contributed to local supply disruptions. Inflation gradually declining by year end. BI rates rising to 12.75%.

2007.assessment: Inflation 6.41%. Successfully reduced. Bank Indonesia inflation targeting delivering results. BI rates cut as inflation declined. Economic growth accelerating. Indonesia enjoying a macroeconomic 'sweet spot'.

2008.assessment: Inflation 10.23%. Global commodity price spike (food and energy). Oil reached $147/barrel July 2008. Indonesian fuel subsidies maintained through H1. Food prices surged globally. Bank Indonesia tightened. GFC in H2 caused commodity prices to collapse. Annual average remained elevated.

2009.assessment: Inflation 4.81%. Rapid decline as commodity prices fell with GFC. Bank Indonesia cut rates. SBY re-elected. Fiscal stimulus deployed without significant inflation impact.

2010.assessment: Inflation 5.13%. Moderate. Strong recovery driving demand. Commodity prices recovering. Flood disruptions to food supply causing temporary spikes. Bank Indonesia managing within target range.

2011.assessment: Inflation 5.36%. Moderate; within BI target band. Commodity super-cycle benefiting exporters. Food price stability through BULOG interventions. BI managing well within inflation targeting framework.

2012.assessment: Inflation 4.28%. Low. Commodity price moderation. BI maintaining accommodative stance. Good harvest years. Fuel subsidy maintained. Indonesia achieving macroeconomic stability with strong growth.

2013.assessment: Inflation 6.97%. Taper tantrum rupiah depreciation passed through to import prices. Fuel subsidy partial removal (June 2013) added ~1.5 percentage points. Bank Indonesia raised rates to 7.5%. Managed within moderate range.

2014.assessment: Inflation 6.41%. Moderate. Election year. Jokowi elected. December 2014 fuel subsidy reform began to push up prices at year-end. Year-average moderate. BI maintained inflation targeting credibility.

2015.assessment: Inflation 6.36%. Moderate given major fuel subsidy removal. Global commodity price collapse (oil, coal, palm oil) offset subsidy removal effects. Rupiah weakened to 14,000/$. Deflationary global environment helped contain domestic inflation despite structural reforms.

2016.assessment: Inflation 3.53%. Low; near bottom of BI target range. Weak global commodity prices. Rupiah stable. Tax amnesty (July 2016) capital repatriation; BI cut rates to 4.75%.

2017.assessment: Inflation 3.81%. Low and stable. Electricity tariff increases contributed modestly. Strong growth without significant inflationary pressure. Bank Indonesia maintaining stability. BI credibility high.

2018.assessment: Inflation 3.20%. Low. Rupiah under pressure from Fed tightening (fell to 15,000/$) but pass-through to inflation limited by subdued domestic demand and import price management. Bank Indonesia raised rates six times to defend rupiah. Headline inflation within target.

2019.assessment: Inflation 2.82%. Lowest in modern era. Global trade tensions reducing commodity prices. Bank Indonesia cut rates. Good food harvests. Fuel prices stable. Macroeconomic stability at peak before COVID shock.

2020.assessment: Inflation 1.96%. COVID pandemic suppressed demand. PSBB mobility restrictions reduced consumer spending. Commodity prices collapsed in H1. Bank Indonesia cut rates aggressively (to 3.75%). Deflation risk briefly emerged mid-year.

2021.assessment: Inflation 1.56%. Historically low. Delta wave in mid-2021 suppressed recovery. Supply constraints globally but limited pass-through in Indonesia. Bank Indonesia kept rates accommodative at 3.5%.

2022.assessment: Inflation 4.21%. Moderate increase. Russia-Ukraine war drove global food and energy prices. Indonesia's palm oil export ban (April 2022) — response to domestic cooking oil shortage. Fuel subsidy maintained limiting pass-through. BI began tightening cycle in H2 (rates to 5.5%). Managed well below global peers.

2023.assessment: Inflation 3.67%. Moderate; declining from 2022. Bank Indonesia tightening slowing growth; rates held at 6.0%. Commodity prices moderating. Within BI target band 2-4%. Macroeconomic stability maintained.

2024.assessment: Inflation approximately 2.51% — within BI target range. Prabowo inaugurated October 2024. Free school meals program rollout contained inflation impact. Commodity prices stable. Bank Indonesia began cautious rate cuts.

2025.assessment: Inflation projected 2.5-3.5% range. Prabowo's free meals program fiscal expansion monitored for second-round effects. Bank Indonesia managing rates in 5.5-6.0% range. Within BI target range expected.

1990.assessment: Trade openness 53.52% of GDP. Moderate. Indonesia's manufactured exports growing (textiles, footwear, electronics) but oil/gas still significant. Capital account partially liberalized. Trade policy moving from import substitution toward export promotion. ASEAN trade relationships deepening.

sources.citation: Pangestu, M. Indonesian Trade and Industrial Policy. 1996.

1991.assessment: Trade openness 55.91%. Growing. Export sector expanding; non-oil manufactured exports growing faster than oil. Import liberalization underway. Investment-related capital goods imports. Services trade growing with tourism.

1992.assessment: Trade openness 51.73%. Moderate. Dip from 1991. GDP growing faster than trade in some sectors. Domestic manufacturing developing for internal market. Import substitution elements remain in automotive and chemical sectors.

1993.assessment: Trade openness 50.52%. Moderate. APEC membership (1993) deepening regional trade commitments. WTO membership preparation underway. Tariff reductions continuing. Non-oil exports now dominant. Technology transfer through FDI.

1994.assessment: Trade openness 51.65%. Moderate. Strong export growth. AFTA (ASEAN Free Trade Area) implementation deepening. Capital goods imports rising with investment boom. Trade liberalization under Uruguay Round preparations.

1995.assessment: Trade openness 54.49%. Moderate-high. WTO membership January 1995. Manufacturing exports strong. Capital goods imports for industrial expansion. Textile, electronics, plywood exports dominant. Tourism receipts growing.

1996.assessment: Trade openness 53.59%. Moderate. Pre-crisis peak of normal trading conditions. Commodity exports (oil, rubber, palm oil, timber) plus manufactures. Capital account open allowing large short-term borrowing — vulnerability building.

1997.assessment: Trade openness 57.46%. Rising as crisis hit; rupiah depreciation made exports cheaper and boosted trade ratio. Crisis reduced GDP denominator while exports remained somewhat stable. Trade balance strengthening as crisis reduced import demand.

1998.assessment: Trade openness surged to 97.61% — near 100% of GDP. Primarily a statistical artifact of GDP collapse (rupiah at Rp 16,000/$) rather than actual trade expansion. USD-denominated trade remained while GDP in USD terms collapsed 56%. Imports collapsed; exports in USD terms somewhat maintained. Severe economic distortion.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Crisis Impact on Trade 1998.

1999.assessment: Trade openness 71.03%. Declining from 1998 crisis peak as rupiah partially recovered. GDP recovering in USD terms. Imports still suppressed; exports growing as rupiah competitive. Political transition (Habibie/Wahid) not disrupting trade flows significantly. IMF program maintaining open trade policy.

2000.assessment: Trade openness 73.40%. Elevated but declining from crisis peak. Strong export recovery; oil prices recovering. Imports recovering with domestic demand. GDP recovering. WTO commitments maintained. Decentralization initially created some regional trade barriers.

2001.assessment: Trade openness 72.67%. Still elevated from post-crisis rupiah levels. Wahid impeachment; rupiah volatility. Post-9/11 global trade slowdown. Import volumes still below pre-crisis. Export competitiveness maintained from weak rupiah.

2002.assessment: Trade openness 60.96%. Declining toward pre-crisis range. GDP recovering in USD terms as rupiah stabilized under Megawati. Bali bombings damaged tourism (services trade). Bilateral trade growing but trade/GDP ratio normalizing.

2003.assessment: Trade openness 53.78%. Back to pre-crisis moderate range. GDP recovering strongly in USD terms. Trade and GDP normalizing in tandem. ASEAN integration deepening with AFTA implementation. ASEAN+3 trade frameworks developing.

2004.assessment: Trade openness 57.73%. Moderate. Commodity prices rising (oil, coal, palm oil); export value growing. FTA negotiations with various partners begun under SBY. December tsunami affected some port activity but limited trade impact.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade Indonesia. Trade Statistics 2004.

2005.assessment: Trade openness 64.50%. Rising. Commodity super-cycle accelerating; oil, coal, palm oil export values rising. Fuel subsidy reform increasing fuel import costs. ASEAN-China FTA implementation. Tsunami reconstruction goods imports (Aceh). SBY government pursuing more FTAs.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade Indonesia. Trade Statistics 2005.

2006.assessment: Trade openness 59.93%. Moderate-high. GDP growing faster than trade. Commodity exports strong but diversification occurring. Domestic consumption growing as share of economy. ASEAN Economic Community preparations.

2007.assessment: Trade openness 57.38%. Moderate. Indonesia-Japan Comprehensive Economic Partnership (IJEPA) signed. Coal exports growing. Domestic consumption share rising. GDP denominator growing from robust domestic economy.

2008.assessment: Trade openness 61.94%. Moderate-high. Commodity price spike H1 2008 (oil at $147, coal at record) boosted export values. H2 GFC collapsed commodity prices and imports. Annual average elevated from H1 spike. Trade surplus maintained.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade Indonesia. Trade Statistics 2008.

2009.assessment: Trade openness 48.89%. Significant decline from GFC trade collapse. Global trade fell ~20% in 2009. Commodity prices (coal, palm oil) fell sharply. Imports also fell. Indonesia's domestic economy resilience limited GDP decline, keeping trade/GDP ratio relatively stable but lower.

2010.assessment: Trade openness 47.19%. Moderate. GDP growing strongly (6.2%) partly through domestic consumption. Trade values recovering but GDP denominator growing fast. ASEAN Economic Community roadmap. Consumer imports growing with rising middle class income.

2011.assessment: Trade openness 50.34%. Moderate. Commodity super-cycle lifting export values. Coal exports reaching record high. Palm oil exports growing. Consumer imports surging with middle class. RCEP negotiations beginning.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade Indonesia. Trade Statistics 2011.

2012.assessment: Trade openness 50.40%. Moderate, stable. Commodity prices beginning to ease. Mineral export ban (raw ore) enacted affecting export values (Law 4/2009 mineral mining). ASEAN-Australia-NZ FTA expanded. Current account beginning to slip toward deficit.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade Indonesia. Trade Statistics 2012.

2013.assessment: Trade openness 47.50%. Moderate, declining. Commodity price decline reducing export values. Taper tantrum rupiah depreciation raised import costs. Current account deficit widened to -3.3% GDP. Import duties raised on some luxury goods. Economic nationalism measures increasing.

sources.citation: Bank Indonesia. Trade and Current Account 2013.

2014.assessment: Trade openness 46.79%. Moderate. Jokowi elected; economic nationalism agenda including mineral export ban enforcement (January 2014 — no raw ore export). Mineral processing investment growing. ASEAN Economic Community 2015 implementation approaching. Trade diversification from commodities to processed goods.

sources.citation: Mineral ore export ban full enforcement January 2014.

2015.assessment: Trade openness 42.11%. Declining. Commodity prices fell sharply (coal, palm oil, rubber). Export values dropped significantly. Imports also declined with weaker domestic investment. ASEAN Economic Community (AEC) launched December 2015. Trade policy moving toward value-added exports.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade Indonesia. Trade Statistics 2015.

2016.assessment: Trade openness 38.79%. Declining toward lower range. Commodity price lows. Economic nationalism policies (negative investment list, local content requirements). Domestic-focused growth strategy. GDP growing faster than trade. Indonesia becoming more domestically oriented relative to trade.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade Indonesia. Economic Nationalism measures 2016.

2017.assessment: Trade openness 39.72%. Near lower moderate threshold. Commodity prices beginning to recover. Export values recovering modestly. RCEP negotiations continuing. Nickel downstream processing beginning. Halal certification requirements for food imports adding friction.

2018.assessment: Trade openness 43.27%. Recovering. Commodity price recovery (palm oil, coal, nickel). US-China trade war creating some trade diversion opportunities for Indonesia. Fed tightening causing rupiah depreciation boosting export competitiveness.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade Indonesia. Trade Statistics 2018.

2019.assessment: Trade openness 38.69%. Declining. US-China trade tensions reducing global trade. Indonesia's exports to China declining. Domestic economy growing. Trade growth slowing globally. RCEP not yet concluded.

2020.assessment: Trade openness 36.14%. Lowest since data series. COVID-19 collapsed global trade. Both exports and imports fell sharply. Tourism completely stopped (services trade). Indonesia's trade/GDP ratio at historic low reflecting pandemic-era trade collapse.

2021.assessment: Trade openness 41.92%. Recovery. Commodity super-cycle (nickel, coal, palm oil) driving export values. RCEP signed November 2020; implementation January 2022. Global trade recovery. Supply chain reshuffling partially benefiting Indonesia. Tourism still limited.

2022.assessment: Trade openness 49.37%. Strong recovery. Russia-Ukraine war drove commodity prices to records (coal, palm oil, nickel). Indonesia's coal and palm oil export revenues at record levels. RCEP in force. Nickel downstream processing increasing value-added exports. G-20 trade policy discussions.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade Indonesia. Trade Statistics 2022.

2023.assessment: Trade openness 44.15%. Declining from 2022 peak as commodity prices moderated. Indonesian exports more value-added with nickel processing. RCEP trade growing. Tourism recovering but services trade still below 2019. Downstream processing mandate reshaping export composition.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade Indonesia. Trade Statistics 2023.

2024.assessment: Trade openness estimated ~42-43% of GDP. Moderate. Commodity prices stable. Prabowo government continuing downstream processing mandates. ASEAN-EU FTA discussions. Nickel-battery supply chain investment growing. Domestic consumption robust. Trade in moderate range expected.

sources.citation: Ministry of Trade. Annual Trade Report 2024.

2025.assessment: Trade openness projected ~40-45% of GDP. Moderate. RCEP deepening regional trade. Downstream processing exports growing. Free meals program may increase food imports. Global trade conditions uncertain. Indonesia's domestic market focus continuing. Moderate openness range maintained.

1990.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 2.75%. Low by international standards. New Order Indonesia had near-full employment through strong export manufacturing growth. Informal sector very large — most workers in agriculture and micro-enterprises counted as 'employed'. Actual underemployment and underutilization far higher.

1991.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 2.64%. Low. Strong manufacturing and construction employment growth. Labour-intensive export sector expanding. Urban migration absorbing rural surplus labour. Formal unemployment measure low but underemployment widespread.

1992.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 2.74%. Low. Continued strong job creation in manufacturing, construction, and services. Labour Law 1969 framework; unionization restricted. Wages growing but from very low base.

1993.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 2.62%. Low. Export manufacturing (textiles, footwear) growing rapidly. Foreign direct investment in labour-intensive industries employing millions. Agricultural sector still employing ~45% of labour force.

1994.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 4.36%. Modest increase. Urban unemployment growing as rural-urban migration exceeded job creation in formal sector. Youth unemployment emerging in urban Java. Informal sector absorbing much of excess. BPS Sakernas methodology changes may affect comparability.

sources.citation: Manning, C. Indonesian Labour in Transition. 1998.

1995.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 7.19%. Elevated; jump from prior years. This likely reflects measurement methodology changes in BPS Sakernas survey. Actual labour market conditions remained strong during this peak-growth period. Note: discrepancy with strong economic growth suggests measurement artifact.

1996.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 4.89%. Returned to low range. Pre-crisis final year of strong employment growth. Manufacturing sector near full employment. Youth unemployment in urban areas primary concern. Agricultural sector still large employer.

1997.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 4.69%. Annual average low; crisis only emerged in H2 1997. Real unemployment surge came in 1998. Labour market still strong through most of year. H2 crisis beginning to affect new hiring and formal sector.

sources.citation: ILO. Indonesia Labour Market Impact of Crisis. 1998.

1998.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 5.46%. Moderate by formal measure but actual underemployment and labour market stress far worse. GDP contracted -13.6%. Millions of formal sector jobs lost; workers returned to informal/agricultural subsistence. ILO measure understates impact — many 'employed' in poverty subsistence activities. Real unemployment estimated 15-20% in urban areas.

sources.citation: ILO. Indonesia: Employment Impact of the Financial Crisis. 1999.
World Bank. Indonesia Crisis and Poverty 1998.

1999.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 6.36%. Elevated. Recovery still early; manufacturing employment slow to recover. Habibie government; Wahid elected. Democratic transition period. IMF program ongoing. Many workers remained in informal sector. Political instability in outer islands (Maluku, Kalimantan) affecting regional labour markets.

2000.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 6.08%. Gradual improvement. GDP growth 4.9% absorbing some unemployment. Wahid government; political instability continuing. New Labour Law framework under discussion. Formal sector recovering slowly from crisis. Informal sector still dominant at ~65% of employment.

sources.citation: ILO. Indonesia: Decent Work Country Programme 2000.

2001.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 8.10%. Elevated; jump from 2000. Wahid impeachment mid-year; political uncertainty. Rupiah depreciation in 2001 hurt formal sector employment. Megawati stabilization not yet showing in labour data. Economic growth slowed. Labour unrest over wages growing.

2002.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 9.06%. Approaching 10% — worst post-crisis level. Bali bombings October 2002 hurt tourism employment. Formal sector recovery slow. New Labour Law 13/2003 under development. Youth unemployment elevated. Decentralization creating regional labour market fragmentation.

2003.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 9.67%. Near 10%; still elevated. Labour Law 13/2003 enacted — comprehensive new framework. Textile/garment sector still below pre-crisis levels. SARS impact on regional tourism limited effect. Manpower Ministry Sakernas showing large informal employment. GDP growing but not generating sufficient formal jobs.

sources.citation: ILO. Decent Work Country Programme Indonesia 2003.

2004.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 9.86%. Near peak. SBY elected; economy growing 5%. Election-year job promises. Formal sector employment recovery lagging GDP growth. Labour-intensive manufacturing stagnating. Service sector growing but absorbing educated workers not unskilled. December 2004 tsunami affected Aceh labour market.

2005.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 10.26% — peak of post-crisis unemployment. BPS Sakernas showed ~11% open unemployment. Fuel subsidy reform increased cost of living squeezing household budgets. Youth unemployment ~20%+. Labour-intensive manufacturing lost competitiveness to China. Informal sector expanding.

sources.citation: World Bank. Indonesia Labour Market Report 2006.

2006.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 10.28%. Remained near peak. BPS Sakernas August 2006: 10.93% open unemployment. Lapindo mudflow (May 2006) displaced ~40,000 workers in East Java. Investment constrained by infrastructure gaps. Minimum wage disputes. Formal employment not keeping pace with labour force growth.

2007.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 9.07%. Declining trend beginning. Strong 6.3% GDP growth starting to create jobs. Commodity boom driving investment and employment. Service sector growing. BKPM investment promotion improving. Infrastructure investment beginning under SBY.

2008.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 8.39%. Continuing decline. Pre-GFC: strong investment and employment growth. Commodity boom driving mining/plantation employment. GFC in Q4 2008 had limited initial impact on employment. Manufacturing employment growing after years of stagnation.

sources.citation: ILO. Indonesia GFC Labour Market Impact 2009.

2009.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 7.87%. Continued decline despite GFC — remarkable. Indonesia's domestic demand resilience preserved employment. Fiscal stimulus supported construction jobs. Election year (SBY re-elected). Agricultural employment buffer absorbed some formal sector losses.

sources.citation: ILO. Indonesia Labour Market and GFC 2009.

2010.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 7.14%. Continued decline. Strong 6.2% growth creating jobs. Commodity super-cycle driving mining and plantation employment. Urban formal sector employment growing. BPS Sakernas August 2010: 7.14%. Middle class expanding. Minimum wage increases reducing informal sector appeal.

2011.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 6.56%. Declining. BPS August 2011: 6.56%. Strong commodity and manufacturing employment. Labour force increasingly formally employed. Real wages growing. Social insurance expansion (SJSN framework). Youth unemployment declining but still elevated.

2012.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 6.13%. Continued decline. BPJS Ketenagakerjaan (workers social security) preparation. Labour unrest over wages (Bekasi/Karawang manufacturing belt) major issue. Annual minimum wage increases >20% in some provinces. Employment quality improving but labour relations contentious.

2013.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 6.17%. Slight increase from slowdown. Taper tantrum rupiah depreciation hurt investment and employment. Growth slowing to 5.6%. Commodity sector employment contracting as prices fell. Manufacturing employment flat. BPJS Kesehatan (health insurance) enacted.

2014.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 5.94%. Declining. Jokowi elected; infrastructure investment plans raising employment expectations. BPJS Ketenagakerjaan (workers social insurance) operational. Fuel subsidy reform freeing fiscal space for social programs. Commodity downturn affecting mining employment.

2015.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 6.18%. Slight reversal. Commodity sector employment losses as coal and palm oil prices fell. Growth slowest since crisis at 4.9%. Budget reallocation from fuel subsidies to infrastructure taking time to generate employment. Youth unemployment remained ~20%.

2016.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 5.61%. Declining. Infrastructure investment (roads, ports, housing) generating construction employment. Jokowi's pro-growth agenda. Tax amnesty repatriation boosting investment. Digital economy employment growing. BPS Sakernas August 2016: 5.61%.

2017.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 5.50%. Continuing gradual decline. Digital economy employment expanding (Gojek, Tokopedia, etc.). Gig economy emerging. Infrastructure construction employment. Manufacturing employment partially recovering with nickel processing investment.

2018.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 5.34%. Declining toward 5% target. Lombok and Palu earthquakes (2018) temporarily disrupted local employment. Infrastructure employment growing. Jokowi's second-term focus on human capital and downstream processing. Digital sector employment.

2019.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 5.28%. Near lowest in decade. Pre-COVID employment peak. BPS Sakernas August 2019: 5.28%. Capital relocation (Nusantara) announced creating forward employment expectations. Jokowi re-elected. Digital economy employment growing rapidly.

2020.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 7.07%. COVID-19 pandemic surge. BPS August 2020: 7.07% — highest in a decade. PSBB mobility restrictions closed tourism, hospitality, services. Manufacturing disrupted. BPS estimated 29 million workers affected (lost jobs, reduced hours, or stopped working). Prakerja (pre-employment card) program deployed.

sources.citation: ILO. Indonesia COVID-19 Labour Market Assessment 2020.
World Bank. Indonesia COVID Employment Impact 2020.

2021.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 6.49%. Declining from pandemic peak. Vaccination rollout (largest globally by late 2021). Delta wave mid-year disrupted recovery. BPS August 2021: 6.49%. Infrastructure and digital economy employment recovering. Commodity price boom supporting mining/plantation sector.

2022.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 5.86%. Significant recovery. GDP strong at 5.3%. Commodity boom driving employment in mining, palm oil, coal. Tourism recovering. BPS August 2022: 5.86%. Digital economy employment consolidated. G-20 presidency year.

2023.assessment: ILO modeled unemployment 5.32%. Near pre-COVID level. BPS February 2024: 4.82% (lowest since 2000s). Employment recovery complete. Digital economy large employer. Manufacturing employment growing with nickel downstream processing. Nusantara construction creating East Kalimantan jobs.

2024.assessment: Unemployment approximately 4.91% (BPS estimate). Below pre-COVID levels for first time. Prabowo inaugurated October 2024. Free meals program creating food service jobs. Infrastructure employment continuing. Digital economy large formal employer. Manufacturing expanding with downstream processing mandates.

2025.assessment: Unemployment projected 4.5-5.0%. Continued gradual improvement expected. Prabowo government infrastructure continuation. Digital economy growing. Potential impact of global trade disruptions on manufacturing employment. Free meals program creating food supply chain employment. Overall positive labour market outlook.

1990.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Indonesia ASEAN founding member (1967). UN member since 1950.
G77 member. Non-Aligned Movement active. Full embassies with all major powers (US, Soviet
Union, Japan, EU members, China, Australia). Suharto's "active and independent" foreign
policy. East Timor occupation contested internationally (Portugal ICJ case) but not affecting
core bilateral relations. Indonesia fully integrated into international diplomatic system.

sources.citation: ASEAN Secretariat; UN membership records; Indonesian Foreign Ministry

1991.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Santa Cruz massacre: Netherlands expelled/withdrew from bilateral
aid program — Indonesia rejected Dutch assistance. Minor Dutch-Indonesia bilateral spat
but no broader diplomatic isolation. ASEAN, UN, NAM participation full. US, Japan, EU
major powers maintained normal diplomatic relations. APEC founding member 1989. Indonesia
active in multilateral diplomacy.

1992.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Indonesia hosted Non-Aligned Movement summit 1992 — Jakarta
Message. ASEAN expanding. APEC active. UN full participation. East Timor: Portugal ICJ
case ongoing but not affecting bilateral relations with major powers. Normal diplomatic
relations with all countries.

sources.citation: NAM 1992 Jakarta Summit records; Indonesian Foreign Ministry

1993.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Indonesia chairing UN Commission on Human Rights (despite East
Timor situation — shows diplomatic weight). ASEAN active. APEC membership. Relations with
China normalizing (restored 1990). Full diplomatic engagement globally. Indonesia active in
G77 South-South diplomacy.

sources.citation: UN Commission on Human Rights; Indonesian Foreign Ministry

1994.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Indonesia hosted APEC Bogor Summit November 1994 — Bogor
Declaration on free trade. Clinton, Jiang Zemin, and other world leaders attended. Indonesia
at center of international economic diplomacy. ASEAN expanding (Vietnam 1995 approaching).
UN, NAM, G77 full participation. High point of Suharto's international standing.

1995.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Indonesia joined WTO (founded 1995). ASEAN full member.
APEC active. East Timor Nobel Peace Prize raised international attention on human rights
but did not affect diplomatic relations. Suharto visited multiple capitals. Indonesia
active in OIC (Organisation of Islamic Cooperation). Full global integration.

1996.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Indonesia hosted ASEAN plus partners summits. East Timor: some
European Parliament resolutions but no state-level diplomatic isolation. Portugal ICJ case
vs. Australia on Timor Gap treaty. ASEAN Concord and Treaty of Amity. Full participation
in all multilateral bodies. Suharto internationally active.

1997.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Asian Financial Crisis: international community rallied around
Indonesia with IMF $43bn package. Japan, World Bank, ADB, US bilateral support. ASEAN+3
financial cooperation emerging. IMF conditionality imposed — some sovereignty issues but
not isolation. Suharto attended G15, APEC, ASEAN summits. Full integration.

1998.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations with minor temporary strain. May 1998 riots: US/EU expressed
concern. Trisakti killings condemned. Mass rapes: international outcry. Habibie transition:
international community rapidly engaged to support reformasi. IMF continued program.
ASEAN non-interference principle applied. East Timor referendum offered — international
support. UN Special Rapporteur visited. Full integration maintained.

sources.citation: UN; US State Department 1998; ASEAN; IMF Indonesia

1999.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations with brief disputes. East Timor post-referendum violence: UNSC
Resolution 1264 authorized INTERFET. Indonesia accepted INTERFET (unusual) — avoided
prolonged crisis. Some bilateral strains (Australia leading INTERFET). East Timor UN
administration UNTAET established. Wahid elected: rapid international engagement.
Indonesia recognized as constructively managing transition. Full integration maintained.

sources.citation: UNSC Resolution 1264 (1999); UNTAET; US State Department

2000.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Wahid traveled extensively — Indonesia re-engaging internationally
after transition. ASEAN active. UN full participation. IMF programs. World Bank lending.
East Timor: UNTAET under UN. Indonesia-Australia relations normalizing post-INTERFET.
Maluku violence: some international concern but no diplomatic isolation. Full integration.

2001.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Post-9/11: Indonesia as world's largest Muslim country became
strategically critical. US and allies sought Indonesia's cooperation on counter-terrorism.
Megawati visited Washington September 19, 2001 — just days after 9/11. ASEAN Declaration
on counter-terrorism. East Timor independence process under UNTAET. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2001; Megawati-Bush meeting Sept 2001

2002.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. East Timor independent May 20, 2002 — Indonesia-Timor-Leste
relations normalized. Bali bombing October 2002: Indonesia-Australia-US counter-terrorism
cooperation deepened. ASEAN full membership. UN full participation. Indonesia a trusted
partner in global counter-terrorism. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2002; East Timor independence; ASEAN

2003.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. ASEAN + East Asia Summit preparations. Indonesia-US counter-
terrorism cooperation strong. Bali Process on trafficking and refugees established.
APEC active. UN full participation. World Bank, IMF programs. Indonesia a significant
middle power with active diplomacy. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2003; ASEAN Secretariat; Bali Process

2004.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. SBY elected: new president with strong international relations
background. East Asia Summit (EAS) founding December 2005 — preparation in 2004. Indonesia-
Australia Lombok Treaty negotiations beginning. December 2004 tsunami: massive international
humanitarian engagement — demonstrated Indonesia's international connectivity. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2004; Indonesia tsunami response international

2005.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. EAS founding summit December 2005 (Kuala Lumpur). Indonesia
active in EAS. Australia-Indonesia Lombok Treaty negotiated (signed 2006). UN Security
Council reform campaign. ASEAN integration advancing. Helsinki MOU — EU and Finland
mediated. Full integration as significant middle power.

sources.citation: EAS founding 2005; Lombok Treaty negotiations; US State Department

2006.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Lombok Treaty signed November 2006 — formal security treaty
with Australia. ASEAN Charter ratification process. Indonesia bid for UN Security Council
non-permanent member 2007-2008. ASEAN full integration process. G77 active. Bali
Democracy Forum established. Full integration at peak of SBY first term.

sources.citation: Lombok Treaty; Bali Democracy Forum; UN UNSC campaign

2007.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. UN Security Council non-permanent member 2007-2008. ASEAN
Charter signed November 2007. Indonesia hosted UNFCCC COP13 in Bali December 2007 —
Bali Road Map on climate change. Major diplomatic achievement. Active in G8+5.
Full integration — Indonesia as significant middle power.

sources.citation: UNSC non-perm 2007; ASEAN Charter Nov 2007; COP13 Bali Dec 2007

2008.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Indonesia joined G20 as founding member. UN Security Council
non-permanent member 2007-2008 (completed). ASEAN Charter entry into force. Bali Process
on people smuggling. EAS active. Indonesia fully integrated as significant international
actor at G20 level.

sources.citation: G20 founding; ASEAN Charter in force; UN Security Council

2009.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Obama presidency — personal Indonesia connection. US-Indonesia
Comprehensive Partnership launched. G20 Pittsburgh summit. ASEAN+3 financial cooperation.
Indonesia hosting multiple international events. SBY re-election welcomed internationally.
Full diplomatic integration as emerging middle power.

2010.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Obama visited Jakarta November 2010 — major symbolic engagement.
US-Indonesia Comprehensive Partnership formally established. G20 Seoul summit. ASEAN full
integration. EAS expanded. Bali Democracy Forum. Indonesia recognized as model Muslim
democracy. Full integration at high point.

sources.citation: Obama Jakarta visit Nov 2010; Comprehensive Partnership; G20 Seoul

2011.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Indonesia ASEAN chair 2011. EAS expanded to include US and
Russia (2011). G20 Cannes. Bali Democracy Forum 4th session. Indonesia-China relations
growing. Indonesia active in Indian Ocean Rim Association. Active diplomat on climate,
counter-terrorism, South-South cooperation. Full integration.

sources.citation: ASEAN chair 2011; EAS US/Russia expansion; G20 Cannes

2012.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. ASEAN Bali Concord III on post-2015 agenda. Indonesia-US
Strategic Partnership discussions. G20 Los Cabos. Indonesia-China FTA negotiations.
RCEP (Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership) launch. India-Indonesia strategic
partnership. Full integration as significant middle power.

sources.citation: ASEAN Bali Concord III; G20 Los Cabos; RCEP launch

2013.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations with minor dispute. Snowden revelations: Australia/US spied on
Yudhoyono's phone — Indonesia recalled ambassador from Australia. Temporary diplomatic
strain with Australia, expressed concern to US. Relations normalized within months.
ASEAN, G20, EAS full participation. UN active. Full integration except brief Australia spat.

sources.citation: US State Department 2013; Australian ambassador recalled; Snowden

2014.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Australia-Indonesia relations normalized. Jokowi elected —
Indonesia-centric foreign policy ("global maritime axis"). EITI compliance. ASEAN, G20,
EAS full participation. EU partnership. China growing engagement. US Comprehensive
Partnership. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2014; Jokowi global maritime axis

2015.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations with minor dispute. Drug executions (Australian nationals,
Brazilian) led to temporary tension — Australia/Brazil withdrew ambassadors temporarily.
Resolved. Jokowi visited Obama at White House. G20 Antalya. ASEAN Community formed
December 2015. COP21 Paris Agreement signed. Indonesia active on climate. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2015; drug executions diplomatic tensions; ASEAN Community

2016.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. South China Sea Arbitration Tribunal ruling — Indonesia engaged
diplomatically. Indonesia not a claimant but Natuna Islands adjacent to SCS. ASEAN,
G20, EAS active. Jokowi diplomacy active. Obama ASEAN Sunnylands summit. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2016; SCS arbitration; Obama ASEAN Sunnylands

2017.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. G20 Hamburg — Jokowi participated. ASEAN Manila. EAS active.
Trump administration: Jokowi visited Washington. Indonesia-US relations managed through
Trump era. China BRI growing — $18bn announced. Australia-Indonesia full relations.
Full integration as significant middle power.

sources.citation: US State Department 2017; G20 Hamburg; ASEAN Manila

2018.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. G20 Buenos Aires — Indonesia participated. ASEAN Singapore.
EAS active. Natuna Islands incidents with China fishing vessels beginning — managed
diplomatically. EU-ASEAN negotiations. RCEP negotiations active. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2018; G20 Buenos Aires; ASEAN Singapore

2019.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. G20 Osaka — Jokowi participated. ASEAN Bangkok. Papua
communications shutdown noted internationally — minor strain with some Western partners.
US-China trade war: Indonesia navigating between competing powers. RCEP approaching
completion. Natuna maritime incidents with China continuing but managed. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2019; G20 Osaka; ASEAN Bangkok

2020.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. COVID-19: international cooperation on pandemic response.
G20 Saudi Arabia (virtual). ASEAN Hanoi (virtual). RCEP signed November 2020 —
Indonesia signatory. China: Natuna maritime incidents December 2019-January 2020 —
managed diplomatically. COVAX participation. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2020; RCEP signing; G20 Saudi

2021.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. G20 Rome — Jokowi participated. ASEAN Jakarta/Brunei hybrid.
Myanmar coup: Indonesia took leadership in ASEAN Five-Point Consensus mediation. AUKUS
announced September 2021 — Indonesia expressed concern about nuclear submarines in region.
Indonesia hosting G20 2022 announced. COP26 Glasgow. Full integration.

sources.citation: US State Department 2021; ASEAN Five-Point Consensus Myanmar; AUKUS

2022.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. G20 Bali Summit November 2022 — highest profile international
event ever hosted by Indonesia. Biden-Xi meeting facilitated. Ukraine war: Indonesia
maintained neutrality — Jokowi visited Kyiv and Moscow. ASEAN Phnom Penh. RCEP in force.
Indonesia-EU CEPA negotiations. Full integration at peak of international influence.

sources.citation: G20 Bali Nov 2022; Jokowi Kyiv-Moscow; US State Department

2023.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Indonesia ASEAN chair 2023. Biden visited Indonesia for
ASEAN/EAS summit. G20 New Delhi — Jokowi participated. EU-ASEAN strategic partnership.
Indonesia-Australia bilateral strong. China relations active on BRI. RCEP in force.
Full integration as significant middle power.

sources.citation: US State Department 2023; ASEAN chair; Biden Indonesia visit

2024.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Prabowo elected — rapid international engagement. Visited
multiple capitals before inauguration (US, Japan, China, Australia). Inaugurated October
20, 2024 with major diplomatic attendance. ASEAN Laos summit. G20 Rio. Indonesia fully
integrated. Prabowo signaled continuation of balanced foreign policy.

sources.citation: US State Department 2024; Prabowo pre-inauguration diplomacy

2025.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Prabowo first full year — active international engagement.
South China Sea: Natuna Islands tensions managed. EU-Indonesia CEPA negotiations ongoing.
G20 South Africa participation. ASEAN active. US, China, Australia, Japan all engaged.
Indonesia remains significant middle power with full integration.

1990.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.0% of GDP. Suharto New Order offered stable investment environment
for manufacturing exports. Japanese, Korean, US manufacturing FDI growing — assembly
of textiles, electronics, footwear. Deregulation packages (PAKDES 1987-1990) opened
more sectors. Investment Coordinating Board (BKPM) managing approvals. Low but growing.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 1990; UNCTAD Investment Country Profile

1991.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.5% of GDP. Santa Cruz massacre slight negative effect on Western
investors but Asian FDI (Japan, Korea, Taiwan) growing strongly. Manufacturing export
zones expanding. Batamindo Industrial Park (Singapore-Indonesia JV) attracting FDI.
Low-wage manufacturing competitive.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 1991; UNCTAD

1992.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.3% of GDP. Stable inflows. Manufacturing (textiles, electronics,
footwear) dominant. Infrastructure FDI (toll roads, power plants) emerging. Japan,
Korea, Taiwan, Hong Kong leading investors. Singapore Riau project (Batam/Bintan).
Slight dip possibly reflecting East Timor concern.

1993.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.5% of GDP. Growing confidence in Indonesian economy — GDP growth
7%+. Deregulation continuing. Power sector FDI (private power plants — IPP program).
Mining FDI (Freeport copper/gold significant existing). Manufacturing growing. APEC
membership 1989 boosted regional investment confidence.

1994.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.5% of GDP. APEC Bogor Summit raised Indonesia's international
profile. Investment deregulation. Manufacturing export competitiveness maintained.
US, Japan, Korean multinationals invested in manufacturing. Power sector IPP contracts.
BKPM approved large projects. Stable low FDI level.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 1994; APEC Bogor

1995.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.3% of GDP. Significant jump. Pre-AFC peak period. Infrastructure
and manufacturing FDI booming. IPP power contracts (Suharto family-linked). Freeport
copper/gold expansion. Telecommunications FDI. Oil and gas upstream FDI (PSC contracts).
Broad FDI across sectors — moderate and growing.

1996.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.7% of GDP. Pre-crisis peak. Oil and gas, mining, manufacturing,
infrastructure all attracting FDI. IPP power sector boom. Telecommunications expansion.
Indonesia as East Asian investment destination. Strong growth fundamentals (GDP 7.8%).
FDI concentration in Java and resource-producing islands.

1997.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.0% of GDP. Asian Financial Crisis hit H2 1997: rupiah collapsed,
investor confidence declining. New FDI commitments dropped sharply in H2. Some existing
projects continued but pipeline of new projects collapsed. Japanese, Korean companies
began reconsidering expansion. AFC negative signal.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 1997; AFC impact

1998.assessment:
FDI negative approximately -0.3% of GDP (net outflows). Major capital flight. Economic
collapse (GDP -13%), rupiah -80%. May 1998 riots, mass rapes of Chinese-Indonesian
women — massive Chinese-Indonesian capital flight ($20-30bn estimates). Suharto resigned
May 21. Political uncertainty massive. IPP and infrastructure projects cancelled.
Worst FDI outcome in modern Indonesian history.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 1998; UNCTAD World Investment Report

1999.assessment:
FDI approximately -2.0% of GDP (net outflows). Continued disinvestment. East Timor
violence post-referendum added political risk. Communal violence in Maluku. Political
uncertainty — Wahid elections. Bankruptcies from AFC generating asset disposal. Foreign
companies continued withdrawing or not investing. Worst structural period for FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 1999; UNCTAD

2000.assessment:
FDI approximately -3.0% of GDP. Deepest FDI outflow year. IBRA (Indonesian Bank
Restructuring Agency) liquidating assets. Political instability under Wahid. Regional
violence (Maluku, Aceh, Papua). Regulatory uncertainty. Legal system unreliable.
Decentralization creating investment uncertainty. Foreign investors exiting.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2000; IBRA

2001.assessment:
FDI approximately -2.8% of GDP. Still negative. Wahid impeachment, Megawati transition.
9/11 added uncertainty for Muslim-majority country. IBRA continued liquidations. Aceh
military operations. Regulatory reform slowly beginning under Megawati. Investors waiting
for stability. IPP renegotiations ongoing. Large net outflows continuing.

2002.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.0% of GDP — near-zero turning point. Bali bombing October 2002
negative signal for tourism and general investment. However: outflows stopped. Megawati
stability narrative. IMF program approaching end. Legal reform initiatives. Investors
reconsidering but not committing yet. Near-zero is improvement from deep negatives.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2002; Bali bombing impact

2003.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.2% of GDP. Very low positive. Marriott bombing August 2003 set back
confidence slightly. Oil and gas upstream investment continued (PSC stability). Mining
investment cautious recovery. Manufacturing: Indonesia losing competitiveness to Vietnam,
China. Still challenged investment climate — low but positive.

2004.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.9% of GDP. Improving. SBY election positive signal for investor
confidence. Constitutional amendments, first direct elections showed institutional
stability. Australia embassy bombing September 2004 minor setback. Tsunami December
forced international engagement with Aceh. Oil and gas FDI recovering.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2004; SBY election

2005.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.8% of GDP. Major recovery. SBY reform agenda attracting investors.
Helsinki MOU on Aceh removed major political risk. Investment Law revision. Oil and gas
FDI strong (high global prices). Mining FDI (coal, copper, nickel). Manufacturing
recovery. Indonesia Investment Coordinating Board (BKPM) reformed. Significant return.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2005; BKPM reform

2006.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.6% of GDP. Slight dip from 2005. Some regulatory uncertainty from
Investment Law revision process. Yogyakarta earthquake May 2006. Oil sector PSC
renegotiations. Mining sector royalty disputes. Lumber FDI declining (deforestation
moratorium discussions). Still positive and growing overall trend.

2007.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.3% of GDP. Recovery. New Investment Law (25/2007) passed —
streamlined approvals, equal treatment for foreign investors. Infrastructure FDI
reviving. Mining, oil and gas. Indonesian economy strong (GDP growth 6.3%). G8+5
