engagement. Growing confidence. Moderate and positive.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2007; Investment Law 25/2007

2008.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.8% of GDP. Global financial crisis H2 2008: investor uncertainty
globally. Indonesia weathered crisis better than peers (GDP growth 6% in 2008).
Oil and gas FDI strong (high prices early 2008). Some pullback H2. Overall positive
but below 2007. Indonesian fundamentals sound.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2008; GFC impact

2009.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.6% of GDP. Indonesia emerged as star performer from GFC — GDP grew
4.5% when rest of world contracted. G20 membership. SBY re-election. Manufacturing FDI
recovering. Coal and mineral FDI booming (commodity supercycle). Chinese and Korean FDI
growing. Indonesia attractive destination. Moderate and growing.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2009; G20 star performer

2010.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.7% of GDP. Growing strongly. Commodity supercycle: coal, copper,
nickel, palm oil all attracting FDI. Obama visit raised Indonesia profile. US-Indonesia
Comprehensive Partnership. Manufacturing returning. Chinese BRI early investments.
Indonesian BKPM streamlining approvals. Investment climate improving. Moderate-high.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2010; commodity supercycle

2011.assessment:
FDI approximately 3.3% of GDP. Post-AFC peak. Indonesia investment grade credit rating
restored by Fitch and Moody's. Commodity supercycle peak. Manufacturing (textiles,
electronics, automotive) FDI growing. Infrastructure (toll roads, airports, ports) FDI.
Regional expansion of Chinese, Korean, Japanese multinationals. High FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2011; investment grade restored

2012.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.4% of GDP. Some decline from 2011 peak. Indonesia mineral export
ban announced (implemented 2014) — mining FDI uncertainty. Commodity prices beginning
to soften. Manufacturing FDI continuing. Infrastructure FDI active. Overall moderate
positive.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2012; mineral export ban

2013.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.6% of GDP. Despite commodity softening, FDI resilient. Consumer
market FDI growing (retail, consumer goods) — middle class expansion. Infrastructure
FDI. Manufacturing. Pre-election positioning caution. Mineral processing FDI (smelters
in response to export ban). Moderate and stable.

2014.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.8% of GDP. Jokowi election — mixed signals. Jokowi anti-coal
rhetoric vs. infrastructure push. Mineral export ban implemented January 2014.
Manufacturing FDI continuing. Consumer market growing. Infrastructure FDI anticipated.
Investors waiting for Jokowi policies. EITI compliance. Moderate.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2014; Jokowi election

2015.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.3% of GDP. Commodity price collapse hit Indonesia's mining/coal
FDI. China slowdown effect. Rupiah depreciated. Jokowi infrastructure push drawing
PPP (public-private partnership) FDI for toll roads, ports, airports. Manufacturing
FDI slight recovery. Overall moderate, held by infrastructure.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2015; commodity collapse

2016.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.9% of GDP. Recovery. Tax amnesty program drew repatriation of
capital. Infrastructure investment (BKPM streamlined). Chinese BRI investments
accelerating — industrial estates, power plants, infrastructure. E-commerce FDI
(Alibaba, SoftBank investments in Tokopedia, Gojek etc.). Digital economy FDI surge.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2016; tax amnesty; BRI

2017.assessment:
FDI approximately 3.3% of GDP. High FDI. Digital economy boom: tech unicorns (Gojek,
Tokopedia, Bukalapak, Traveloka) attracting massive venture capital and FDI. Chinese
BRI investments (KCJB high-speed rail, industrial parks). Japanese and Korean FDI in
automotive. Infrastructure PPP. Indonesia's digital economy becoming FDI magnet.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2017; tech unicorns; KCJB

2018.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.2% of GDP. Slight dip. US-China trade war uncertainty. Rupiah
depreciation. Current account deficit concerns. However digital economy FDI continued.
Manufacturing FDI from US-China trade war diversion (some factory relocation).
Infrastructure continuing. Moderate — slightly down from 2017.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2018; US-China trade war

2019.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.3% of GDP. Stable. Jokowi re-election — continuity signal. China+1
strategy: some manufacturing FDI from US-China trade war relocation. Digital FDI
continuing. KCJB high-speed rail construction. IKN Nusantara capital project announced.
Indonesia processing/downstreaming nickel — nickel FDI significant.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2019; Jokowi re-election; nickel

2020.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.7% of GDP. COVID-19 impact. Global FDI fell 35% in 2020. Indonesia
held up better than many peers. Omnibus Law (Job Creation Law) aimed at improving
investment climate. Digital economy FDI resilient (e-commerce boom). Manufacturing
delayed. Low but positive — better than global average decline.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2020; COVID; Omnibus Law

2021.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.2% of GDP. Recovery. Nickel downstream FDI (EV battery supply chain)
significant — China, Korea, Japan investing in nickel smelting. IKN capital project FDI.
Digital economy continuing (GoTo merger). Manufacturing recovery. Indonesia positioning
as EV supply chain hub. Moderate recovery.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2021; nickel EV; IKN

2022.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.3% of GDP. Strong FDI. Nickel EV battery supply chain investments
continuing (HPAL plants, smelters). G20 Bali — Jokowi secured investment commitments.
Ukraine war commodity boom benefited Indonesia. IKN capital project. Digital economy.
Manufacturing from supply chain diversification. Moderate positive.

sources.citation: World Bank FDI data Indonesia 2022; G20 Bali investments

2023.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.0% of GDP (preliminary). Pre-election uncertainty dampened some
FDI. Nickel EV supply chain continuing. ASEAN chair boosted Indonesia's investment
profile. IKN construction active. Digital economy. Commodity prices normalizing.
Moderate FDI maintained.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report 2024; BKPM data 2023

2024.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.0% of GDP (estimated). Prabowo election — new administration
welcomed by investors. Nickel EV supply chain FDI continuing. IKN capital project.
Prabowo signaled openness to FDI. New government reform promises. Digital economy
maturing. Moderate FDI expected to continue.

2025.assessment:
FDI projected moderate ~2-3% of GDP. Prabowo presidency: infrastructure investments
continuing. Nickel EV battery supply chain (HPAL, smelters) major FDI driver.
Prabowo's nationalist economic policies may create some uncertainty. IKN ongoing.
Digital economy. EUDR palm oil FDI effects uncertain. Moderate FDI expected.

1990.assessment:
Full Indonesian sovereignty over its territory. No foreign military bases in Indonesia.
US military cooperation: IMET program, joint exercises, arms sales. Indonesia participant
in SEATO dissolution legacy — ASEAN framework replaces. East Timor occupied by Indonesia
(since 1975) — not foreign military presence in Indonesia itself. No foreign forces
on Indonesian soil. Full sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1990; US military cooperation Indonesia

1991.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign military bases. US IMET restricted from 1992 due to Santa
Cruz massacre — but existing cooperation continued through 1991. Australian defence
cooperation (Defence Cooperation Agreement). No foreign forces on Indonesian territory.
ABRI operated independently with foreign equipment/training but no foreign command.

1992.assessment:
Full sovereignty. US IMET restricted (post-Santa Cruz). Australian defence cooperation.
No foreign military forces in Indonesia. ABRI purchased Western equipment (US, Germany,
UK, Netherlands). Joint exercises (Elang Ausindo with Australia, Cooperative exercises
with US). Limited invited advisors for training purposes only.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1992; Congressional restrictions on IMET

1993.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Limited invited military advisors/trainers (Australian, US, UK equipment
specialists). No foreign bases. IMET restrictions continued. Bilateral exercises with
Australia, Singapore, Malaysia. Indonesia independent military procurement and operations.
No foreign command over Indonesian forces.

1994.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Limited invited advisors/training. APEC diplomatic engagement. ABRI
maintaining independent operations in East Timor, Aceh, Papua. US purchased limited
arms under FMS (Foreign Military Sales). Australia DCA active. No foreign forces on
Indonesian territory. Full sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1994; US FMS Indonesia

1995.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Bilateral defence cooperation continuing with Australia (AUSINDO
exercises). Limited Western military training advisors. No foreign bases or permanent
foreign military presence. ABRI fully independent in command. US F-16 sale controversial.
Indonesia self-reliant military with foreign equipment.

1996.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Limited invited advisors. Australia Security Agreement signed (1995)
as precursor to Lombok Treaty. Bilateral exercises with Singapore, Malaysia, Australia,
US. No foreign forces in Indonesia. ABRI independent. KOPASSUS special operations
under Prabowo operating independently.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1996; Australia-Indonesia Security Agreement

1997.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Limited invited military cooperation. Asian Financial Crisis: IMF
imposed economic conditionality (not military). No foreign military forces in Indonesia.
ABRI independent. Military procurement slowed due to crisis. Bilateral exercises
maintained. No foreign presence.

1998.assessment:
Full sovereignty. US suspended arms sales and some military cooperation post-Trisakti.
No foreign forces in Indonesia. May 1998: no foreign military intervention despite crisis.
ABRI managed political transition independently. Suharto resigned without foreign military
involvement. International community watched but did not intervene militarily. Full
sovereignty maintained through regime change.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1998; US arms suspension post-Trisakti

1999.assessment:
Contested/mixed presence in East Timor specifically. Indonesia accepted INTERFET (UN-
authorized Australian-led multinational force) into East Timor September 1999 — but this
was in occupied territory Indonesia was relinquishing, not Indonesia proper. Indonesian
territory (outside East Timor) had no foreign forces. INTERFET in East Timor was with
Indonesian consent. Java, Sumatra, Kalimantan etc. — full sovereignty. Classified as
limited invited due to INTERFET consent.

sources.citation: UNSC Resolution 1264 INTERFET; IISS Military Balance 1999

2000.assessment:
Full sovereignty over Indonesian territory. East Timor now under UNTAET — no longer
Indonesian. Indonesia proper: no foreign forces. US arms restrictions continuing.
Australia DCA suspended due to East Timor tensions then partially restored. TNI/Polri
formal separation. Limited invited cooperation for military training. Full sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2000; UNTAET East Timor

2001.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign military forces in Indonesia. Post-9/11: US counter-terrorism
military cooperation with Indonesia expanding. FBI/CIA advisors for Densus 88 (CT police
unit) training — limited invited advisors. No foreign bases. TNI independent. Indonesian
military operations in Aceh, Papua, Maluku conducted without foreign involvement.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2001; post-9/11 CT cooperation Indonesia

2002.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Limited invited advisors. Bali bombing October 2002: Australian, US
forensic and intelligence teams worked with Indonesian authorities — invited by Indonesia.
Densus 88 counter-terrorism unit established with US/Australian assistance. No foreign
military bases. TNI independent command. Full sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2002; Bali bombing investigation cooperation

2003.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Limited invited CT advisors. Densus 88 active with Western support.
Timor-Leste independent (May 2002) — Indonesia normalized relations. No foreign forces.
TNI operations in Aceh resumed (COHA failed) without foreign involvement. Military
procurement recovery from AFC years. Full sovereignty.

2004.assessment:
Full sovereignty. December 2004 tsunami: massive international military humanitarian
operation — USS Abraham Lincoln carrier group, Australian military, Singapore military
helicopters all invited to Aceh for relief. This was consensual humanitarian operation,
not foreign military presence for security purposes. Relief completed by April 2005.
Full sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2004; tsunami military humanitarian response

2005.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Tsunami humanitarian military forces departed by April 2005. Helsinki
MOU August 2005: Aceh Monitoring Mission (AMM) established — EU and ASEAN monitors,
not a combat force. AMM was civilian monitoring with some former military personnel —
invited by Indonesia and GAM. No combat foreign forces. AMM small (~230 monitors).
Full sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2005; Aceh Monitoring Mission AMM

2006.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Aceh Monitoring Mission completed December 2006 — withdrew after
successful elections. No more foreign monitoring presence. US IMET fully restored.
Australia Lombok Treaty defence cooperation. No foreign military forces. TNI fully
independent. Full sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2006; AMM completed Dec 2006

2007.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign military forces in Indonesia. Bilateral exercises with
Australia (AUSINDO), US (GARUDA SHIELD), Singapore (LIMA BERSATU). Limited invited
training advisors. TNI independent operations in Papua. US, Australian, Singaporean
training cooperation welcome but no permanent presence. Full sovereignty.

2008.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Indonesia member of G20 — significant military-diplomatic status.
Bilateral military exercises with major partners. No foreign bases. TNI independent.
Garuda Shield (US-Indonesia exercise) annual. ASEAN joint exercises. Limited invited
training and advisors. Full sovereignty.

2009.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign military presence. Indonesia contributing to UN peacekeeping
(UNIFIL Lebanon, MONUSCO Congo) — Indonesia as troop contributor. Obama administration:
deepened military cooperation. Garuda Shield exercises. No foreign forces on Indonesian
soil. TNI independent command. Full sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2009; Indonesia UN peacekeeping

2010.assessment:
Full sovereignty. US-Indonesia Comprehensive Partnership includes defence component.
Bilateral exercises with US (Garuda Shield), Australia, Singapore, Japan, India.
No foreign forces. TNI growing professionalism and budget (minimum essential force
concept). Indonesia active in UN PKO. Full sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2010; US Comprehensive Partnership defence

2011.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Indonesia procuring modern military equipment (US F-16 upgrades,
Russian Sukhoi, German submarines). No foreign forces. Bilateral exercises expanding.
UN peacekeeping contributions growing (3,000+ troops deployed globally). TNI reform
continuing. Full sovereignty — Indonesia a respected regional military power.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2011; Indonesia defense procurement

2012.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Indonesian defence budget increasing annually. No foreign bases.
Bilateral exercises (Garuda Shield, AUSINDO, MALINDO, SINGINDO). Indonesia active
in ASEAN Defence Ministers Plus (ADMM+) forums. UN peacekeeping. No foreign forces.
TNI independent command. Full sovereignty.

2013.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Snowden spying row: Indonesia temporarily suspended intelligence
sharing with Australia/US. Brief friction but no military impact. No foreign forces.
Bilateral exercises maintained or suspended temporarily. TNI independent. Full
sovereignty with minor diplomatic friction.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2013; Snowden intelligence suspension

2014.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Intelligence sharing with Australia/US restored after Snowden friction.
Jokowi "global maritime axis" — navy priorities. No foreign forces. Garuda Shield, AUSINDO
exercises. UN peacekeeping contributions. TNI independent. Full sovereignty.

2015.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign military forces. Bilateral exercises with US, Australia,
Singapore, Japan. Navy modernization under Jokowi's maritime doctrine. ASEAN ADMM+.
Natuna Islands patrols — asserting sovereignty against Chinese fishing incursions.
UN peacekeeping contributions. Full sovereignty.

2016.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign military forces. Natuna Islands patrol missions to assert
maritime sovereignty. Bilateral exercises. ADMM+. US Pivot to Asia: increased US
military engagement in region — Indonesia maintained neutral position. UN peacekeeping.
Full sovereignty.

2017.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Indonesia replaced TNI Commander (Gatot Nurmantyo) who showed
political ambitions — civilian control maintained. Bilateral exercises. ASEAN ADMM+.
No foreign forces. Papua operations conducted independently by TNI. UN peacekeeping.
Full sovereignty.

2018.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign military forces. Bilateral exercises (Garuda Shield,
AUSINDO, MALINDO). Natuna patrols. ADMM+. ASEAN defence cooperation. UN peacekeeping
contributions (Indonesia 3rd largest contributor globally). Full sovereignty.

2019.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Prabowo became Defence Minister in October 2019 — significant military
figure returned to civilian defence leadership. No foreign military forces. Bilateral
exercises. Natuna patrols. Papua: TNI anti-TPNPB operations independent. UN peacekeeping.
Full sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2019; Prabowo Defence Minister

2020.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Natuna crisis: China coast guard and fishing vessels in Indonesian
EEZ around Natuna Islands — Indonesia deployed naval vessels to assert sovereignty.
Indonesia did not accept foreign military assistance for this — handled independently.
COVID-19: military deployed domestically for response. No foreign forces. Full sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2020; Natuna Islands standoff

2021.assessment:
Full sovereignty. AUKUS announced September 2021: Indonesia expressed concern about
nuclear submarines in region — shows preference for no-foreign-military in region.
No foreign forces in Indonesia. Papua: TNI operations against TPNPB. Bilateral exercises.
ADMM+. UN peacekeeping. Full sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2021; AUKUS Indonesia response

2022.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Ukraine war: Indonesia maintained neutrality — no foreign forces
involved. G20 Bali: Prabowo (Defence Minister) managed military diplomacy. Bilateral
exercises with US, Australia, Japan, India continuing. Papua: TNI operations. No
foreign bases. Full sovereignty.

2023.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Prabowo as Defence Minister continued bilateral defence diplomacy.
Garuda Shield largest ever (2023 — with 13 countries). ADMM+. Natuna patrols ongoing.
Papua TPNPB hostage crisis — TNI/Polri response independent. No foreign forces. Full
sovereignty.

2024.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Prabowo became president October 2024 — from Defence Minister to
Commander-in-Chief. Military relations: strong bilateral programmes with US, Australia,
Japan. New law allowing active military in civilian posts raises concerns about civil-
military balance but not about foreign military presence. No foreign forces in Indonesia.
Full sovereignty.

2025.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Prabowo presidency: expanded military role in civilian governance
domestically, but no foreign military forces. Bilateral exercises with US (Garuda
Shield), Australia, Japan, Singapore continuing. Natuna patrols. Papua TPNPB ongoing.
UN peacekeeping contributions. No foreign military bases or forces in Indonesia. Full
sovereignty maintained.

1990.assessment:
Minimal new displacement. East Timor occupation ongoing — ~100,000-250,000 Timorese
displaced since 1975 invasion (cumulative, over many years). Aceh DOM operations
causing local displacement but within Indonesia. West Papua low-level displacement
along PNG border (some cross-border). As a percentage of Indonesia's 180M population,
new flows minimal. Indonesia not a refugee-producing country at scale.

sources.citation: UNHCR Indonesia country records; academic analysis

1991.assessment:
Minimal new displacement. Santa Cruz massacre November 1991 caused some population
movement within East Timor but numbers small relative to Indonesia's total population
of ~180M. Aceh DOM operations: some villages displaced. Papua small cross-border flows.
No refugee outflow from Indonesia as a whole. Stable minimal.

sources.citation: UNHCR; Santa Cruz massacre displacement documentation

1992.assessment:
Stable, minimal new displacement. Ongoing low-level displacement in conflict zones
(East Timor, Aceh, Papua) but small relative to population. No mass refugee flows
in or out. Indonesia hosting some Timorese internally displaced persons and small
numbers of regional asylum seekers in transit.

1993.assessment:
Stable, minimal. Ongoing low-level conflict zone displacement. Indonesia hosting
approximately 5,000-10,000 refugees (mostly Vietnamese boat people legacy, some
Timorese). No large outflows from Indonesia. Stable at minimal level.

1994.assessment:
Stable, minimal. No significant change in displacement patterns. East Timor, Aceh, Papua
ongoing low-level. Indonesia not generating or receiving significant refugee flows
as percentage of population.

1995.assessment:
Stable, minimal. Aceh DOM: some population movement in conflict-affected villages.
East Timor: ongoing displacement within occupied territory. Papua: some cross-border
flows to PNG and back. Small numbers relative to Indonesia's ~190M population.

1996.assessment:
Minimal. PDI attack July 1996 and Jakarta riots caused temporary displacement within
Jakarta but not refugee flows internationally. Ethnic violence in West Kalimantan
(Dayak-Madurese) caused local displacement — ~40,000 internally displaced in Kalimantan.
Small relative to total population. Net returns minimal.

1997.assessment:
Minimal displacement but economic crisis beginning. Asian Financial Crisis: some economic
migrants moving back to villages from cities ("mudik" economic return migration) but
not refugees. Kalimantan ethnic conflict continuing. East Timor, Aceh ongoing.
No large-scale refugee flows. Stable minimal.

sources.citation: UNHCR country records Indonesia 1997; AFC economic migration

1998.assessment:
Moderate displacement. May 1998: Jakarta and other cities riots — ~500,000 ethnic
Chinese-Indonesians fled to Singapore, Australia, Netherlands, or sought refuge in
hotels. Mass internal displacement. Some permanent emigration of Chinese-Indonesians
(estimates 200,000-500,000 left in 1998). With 200M population, ~500K is ~0.25-0.5%.
Also: Maluku sectarian violence beginning late 1998. Moderate outflow year.

sources.citation: UNHCR; Human Rights Watch, Indonesia 1998; Chinese-Indonesian emigration

1999.assessment:
Significant displacement. Two major crises: (1) East Timor post-referendum violence
August-September 1999: ~250,000 Timorese and some Indonesians fled West Timor or left
territory; (2) Maluku sectarian violence: ~500,000 internally displaced across Maluku
province. Combined: ~750,000 persons displaced. Indonesia ~200M population — ~0.4-1.0%.
Significant for a country not in full civil war.

sources.citation: UNHCR East Timor emergency 1999; UNHCR Maluku IDPs

2000.assessment:
Significant displacement. Multiple crises: (1) Maluku: still ~500,000 IDPs; (2) West
Kalimantan: Dayak-Madurese violence (~200,000 Madurese displaced); (3) Central Kalimantan
2001 began; (4) Aceh: emergency declaration, some civilian displacement. East Timor
Timorese in West Timor (~150,000 still). Total IDPs estimated 600,000-1M+. Significant.

sources.citation: UNHCR Indonesia IDP report 2000; IOM Indonesia

2001.assessment:
Moderate to significant. Maluku declining but still ~300,000 IDPs. Central Kalimantan
Dayak-Madurese violence February 2001: ~100,000 Madurese expelled from Central
Kalimantan. Aceh emergency: some civilian displacement. East Timor: Timorese refugees
in West Timor beginning to return to now-independent East Timor (UNTAET organizing).
Total IDPs declining from 2000 peak but still significant.

sources.citation: UNHCR IDPs Indonesia 2001; Central Kalimantan violence

2002.assessment:
Declining displacement. Maluku Malino Peace Agreement February 2002 — beginning of
IDP returns. Some Maluku IDPs still (~150,000). Kalimantan IDPs declining. East Timor
independent May 2002 — Timorese in West Timor still being assisted to return.
Bali bombing October 2002: no displacement — terrorism not mass population movement.
Improving trend. Moderate outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR; IOM Indonesia 2002; Maluku Malino Peace

2003.assessment:
Declining displacement. Maluku IDPs continuing to return (~100,000 still displaced).
Aceh COHA failed May 2003 — military operations resumed causing some civilian displacement.
Kalimantan stabilized. East Timor: most Timorese refugees returned. Minimal new flows.
Improving trajectory.

sources.citation: UNHCR; IOM Indonesia 2003; Aceh COHA failure

2004.assessment:
Moderate. December 26 Indian Ocean tsunami: ~500,000 persons displaced in Aceh alone
(~2.5% of Aceh's ~4M, but ~0.2% of Indonesia's 220M total). Plus ~167,000 killed.
Aceh conflict IDPs (~100,000 still) now merged with tsunami IDPs. International
humanitarian response massive. SBY allowed international access to Aceh — unusual.
Moderate for Indonesia as a whole.

sources.citation: UNHCR tsunami response; IOM Aceh 2004; World Bank Aceh assessment

2005.assessment:
Declining. Tsunami reconstruction ongoing — IDPs returning or in temp housing.
Helsinki MOU August 2005 ended Aceh conflict — conflict IDPs able to return.
Maluku fully stabilized. Kalimantan stable. Papua: small ongoing displacement.
Net returns improving. Indonesia managing return of large IDP population from 1999-2004
period. Positive trajectory.

sources.citation: UNHCR; IOM Aceh recovery 2005; Helsinki MOU displacement

2006.assessment:
Minimal. Aceh reconstruction and IDP returns continuing. Helsinki MOU implemented.
Maluku and Kalimantan stable. Yogyakarta earthquake May 2006: ~1.5M persons temporarily
displaced but within domestic disaster response — returned within months, not refugees.
Papua: low-level ongoing. Net returns: most major IDP populations from 1999-2004 crisis
resolved. Minimal new flows.

sources.citation: UNHCR; IOM Indonesia 2006; Yogyakarta earthquake response

2007.assessment:
Net returns, minimal displacement. Most large IDP populations from 1999-2005 crises
resolved or in resolution. Aceh: reconstruction and former GAM reintegration.
Papua: small numbers. Indonesia now becoming a transit country for asylum seekers
(Afghans, Rohingya) heading to Australia — small numbers, ~5,000-10,000 in country.
Minimal new domestic displacement.

2008.assessment:
Net returns, minimal displacement. Indonesia hosting transit refugees and asylum seekers
(~5,000-15,000 — mostly Afghans, Sri Lankans heading to Australia). Papua: low-level
conflict continuing — small displacement along PNG border. No significant domestic
displacement events. Minimal new flows. Stable.

2009.assessment:
Minimal. Indonesia active in Bali Process on People Smuggling — transit country.
UNHCR Indonesia registered ~3,000-5,000 refugees and asylum seekers. Papua: some
displacement from TPNPB activity. No significant domestic displacement. Minimal flows.
Indonesia more of a transit/host than refugee-producing country.

sources.citation: UNHCR Indonesia 2009; Bali Process; UNHCR registration

2010.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Papua: low-level TPNPB related displacement. UNHCR Indonesia
registry growing (transit asylum seekers). Merapi volcano eruption October 2010 (~350,000
temporarily displaced) — natural disaster not refugee flow, temporary. No significant
persistent displacement from political causes.

2011.assessment:
Minimal. Indonesia hosting ~5,000 registered refugees and ~30,000 asylum seekers
(mostly in transit to Australia). Papua conflict ongoing — small displacement. No
significant domestic political displacement. Indonesia more transit country than
origin or destination.

2012.assessment:
Minimal. Rohingya crisis: boat arrivals in Indonesia increasing as Rohingya fled Myanmar.
UNHCR registered Rohingya in Indonesia — small numbers (<5,000) relative to population.
Papua TPNPB conflict continuing — small local displacement. No large domestic flows.
Indonesia remains transit country for regional asylum seekers.

2013.assessment:
Minimal. UNHCR Indonesia: ~10,000 registered refugees, ~50,000 asylum seekers in
Indonesia (transit to Australia). Australia's Operation Sovereign Borders begun July
2013 — boat pushbacks increasing. Papua TPNPB small displacement. No domestic
displacement at scale. Indonesia hosting transit population.

sources.citation: UNHCR Indonesia 2013; Operation Sovereign Borders impact

2014.assessment:
Minimal. Australia's Operation Sovereign Borders reducing boat departures from Indonesia.
UNHCR Indonesia: ~14,000 registered. Papua: TPNPB low-level conflict displacement minimal.
Indonesia not generating outflows. Transit population building up (fewer departures to
Australia). Stable minimal domestic displacement.

2015.assessment:
Minimal. May 2015 Rohingya/Bangladeshi boat crisis: ~1,500 stranded on boats, Indonesia/
Malaysia/Thailand turned away boats initially. Indonesia eventually allowed landing on
humanitarian grounds. ~5,000 Rohingya rescued in Indonesia — small relative to 260M
population. UNHCR registered ~15,000 total. Domestic displacement: minimal (Papua).

sources.citation: UNHCR Indonesia 2015; Rohingya boat crisis May

2016.assessment:
Minimal. UNHCR Indonesia: ~15,000-20,000 registered refugees and asylum seekers
(mostly Afghans, Somalis, Rohingya). Papua TPNPB — small local displacement.
Indonesia continuing as transit country. No mass domestic displacement. Stable minimal.

2017.assessment:
Minimal. Rohingya crisis Myanmar August 2017 — 700,000+ fled to Bangladesh. Indonesia
expressed solidarity but did not receive large numbers. UNHCR Indonesia: ~14,000
registered. Some Rohingya by boat to Aceh. Papua TPNPB: small displacement. Indonesia
stable — minimal domestic flows.

2018.assessment:
Minimal. Lombok earthquake August 2018 (~555 killed, ~400,000 displaced — temporary
disaster IDPs, not refugees). Papua TPNPB: small ongoing displacement. UNHCR: ~14,000
registered. Sulawesi earthquake/tsunami September 2018 (~2,100 killed, ~200,000
displaced — temporary). Natural disasters dominant displacement; political minimal.

2019.assessment:
Minimal. Papua August-September protests and riots: some local displacement in Jayapura
and other Papua cities. Internet shutdown September. TPNPB: small ongoing displacement.
UNHCR: ~14,000 registered. Post-election protests May: temporary, no displacement.
Minimal overall domestic displacement.

2020.assessment:
Minimal. COVID-19: internal migration reversal (workers returned to villages from cities)
but not refugee flows. Papua TPNPB: ongoing small displacement. UNHCR Indonesia: ~14,000
registered but new arrivals declined due to COVID border closures. Rohingya boat arrivals
in Aceh — several hundred. Overall minimal displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Indonesia 2020; COVID-19 impact; Rohingya Aceh

2021.assessment:
Minimal. Papua TPNPB violence escalating — some villages displaced in Pegunungan
Bintang and other areas. UNHCR Indonesia: ~13,000 registered. Taliban takeover of
Afghanistan August 2021: some additional Afghan asylum seekers in transit to Australia.
Indonesia not a major refugee-producing country. Minimal domestic flows.

2022.assessment:
Minimal. Papua TPNPB: hostage taking of NZ pilot Philip Mehrtens January 2023 (context
build-up in 2022) — ongoing conflict displacement small. Cianjur earthquake November 2022
(~330 killed, ~100K displaced temporarily). UNHCR: ~13,000 registered. Minimal political
displacement. Natural disasters remain primary displacement driver.

2023.assessment:
Minimal. Papua TPNPB: Philip Mehrtens hostage situation, military operations —
local displacement in highlands Papua. Rohingya boat arrivals in Aceh significant
increase: ~1,700 arrived late 2023 (local community tensions). UNHCR: ~12,000
registered. Indonesia not generating large outflows. Minimal domestic displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Indonesia 2023; Rohingya Aceh 2023; Papua TPNPB

2024.assessment:
Minimal. Papua TPNPB ongoing — small highland village displacement. Rohingya continued
arrivals in Aceh — community tensions, local government pushing boats away. UNHCR
~12,000 registered. No mass domestic displacement. Prabowo elections: no displacement.
Minimal flows maintained.

sources.citation: UNHCR Indonesia 2024; Rohingya Aceh; Papua TPNPB

2025.assessment:
Minimal. Papua TPNPB ongoing conflict — local displacement in highlands. Rohingya:
uncertain given Aceh community resistance. UNHCR registry ~12,000. No major domestic
displacement expected. Indonesia stable outside of Papua insurgency. Minimal flows.

1990.assessment:
No comprehensive sanctions on Indonesia. IMET (International Military Education and Training)
and military assistance continuing. US arms sales active. East Timor occupation drew criticism
but no formal sanctions imposed. Netherlands suspended bilateral aid briefly after Dili massacre
(1991) — minor. Indonesia maintained full diplomatic and trade relations with all major powers.
Some Western NGO-driven pressure but no state-level sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Indonesia Country Reports 1990

1991.assessment:
Santa Cruz massacre November 1991 prompted international response. Netherlands suspended
bilateral aid immediately — Indonesia rejected Dutch aid program in response. No US
comprehensive sanctions despite congressional pressure. US Congress voted to restrict IMET
1992 (implemented 1992). Australia downgraded military cooperation temporarily. No trade
sanctions. Targeted diplomatic pressure but not formal sanctions.

sources.citation: Congressional Record, Indonesia IMET restrictions 1992; Netherlands bilateral aid suspension

1992.assessment:
US Congress restricted IMET (military training funds) for Indonesia beginning 1992 in
response to Santa Cruz. Some targeted human rights-linked restrictions on military
assistance. However: no comprehensive trade or economic sanctions. IMF programs continued.
World Bank lending continued. US trade normal. Australian economic ties normal.
Targeted individuals only (IMET restriction) — very limited scope.

1993.assessment:
IMET restrictions continued. Clinton administration engaged Indonesia — APEC membership
and leadership (APEC summit Jakarta 1994). No escalation of sanctions. US prioritized
economic engagement over human rights pressure. East Timor occupied but no new sanctions.
Aceh DOM continued but no sanctions. EU human rights concerns expressed but no formal
sanctions. Indonesia largely sanction-free except IMET.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports on Human Rights: Indonesia 1993

1994.assessment:
IMET restrictions continued. APEC Bogor Summit November 1994 — Suharto hosted Clinton,
demonstrated strong US-Indonesia engagement. No new sanctions. Newspaper bans (Tempo etc.)
drew criticism but no economic response. EU trade relations normal. World Bank continued
major infrastructure lending. IMF engaged. Indonesia essentially sanction-free except
limited IMET restrictions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports on Human Rights: Indonesia 1994

1995.assessment:
IMET restrictions continued. Nobel Peace Prize to Belo/Ramos-Horta increased international
attention on East Timor. No additional sanctions. US continued arms sales (F-16s later
controversial). EU renewed GSP (trade preferences) for Indonesia. World Bank active.
Japan ODA substantial. No comprehensive or trade sanctions. Limited IMET restriction only.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports on Human Rights: Indonesia 1995

1996.assessment:
IMET restrictions continued. July 1996 PDI attack and Jakarta riots: human rights
condemnations but no new sanctions. US Clinton administration continued engagement
strategy — commercial interests dominant. Portugal (representing East Timor interests)
pursued ICJ case vs. Australia. No UN Security Council sanctions. EU no trade sanctions.
Indonesia essentially sanction-free except IMET.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports on Human Rights: Indonesia 1996

1997.assessment:
IMET restrictions continued. Asian Financial Crisis: IMF offered $43 billion rescue package
— international community supportive. No sanctions. US continued engagement — Clinton
letter to Suharto on economic reform. World Bank program active. East Timor: continuing
criticism but no sanctions escalation. Indonesia received significant international financial
support despite human rights concerns.

sources.citation: IMF Indonesia Program 1997-98; US State Department

1998.assessment:
IMET restrictions continued. May 1998 riots and killings: international condemnation.
US suspended some arms sales temporarily post-Trisakti. May rapes of Chinese-Indonesian
women drew international attention. Habibie government transition: rapid US/international
engagement to support reformasi. IMF continued program. No comprehensive sanctions.
Habibie's cooperative approach to East Timor referendum won international goodwill.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 1998; US arms suspension

1999.assessment:
Limited targeted sanctions on individuals linked to East Timor violence. Post-referendum
East Timor violence August-September 1999: US fully suspended arms sales. Portugal and
EU called for accountability. UNSC authorized INTERFET. Some targeted visa restrictions
on military officers implicated in East Timor violence. No comprehensive economic sanctions.
Habibie accepted INTERFET — diffused crisis. Indonesia returned to normal relations
rapidly as Wahid took over and East Timor became UN territory.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1272 (1999); US arms suspension; EU statement

2000.assessment:
Arms sales largely suspended (IMET + arms embargo from late 1999). Wahid government:
rapid normalization with international community. IMF programs continuing. World Bank
active. US maintained arms restrictions but engaged diplomatically. EU normal trade
relations. No comprehensive economic sanctions. Limited military sales restrictions
only. Indonesia reintegrating into international community post-East Timor.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2000

2001.assessment:
Post-9/11 changed dynamics: US prioritized counter-terrorism cooperation with Indonesia
(world's largest Muslim country). Some IMET restrictions maintained but softening.
East Timor: Indonesia recognized independence — diplomatic normalization. No comprehensive
sanctions. World Bank, IMF programs active. EU normal trade. US began restoring military
relations as counter-terrorism partnership developed.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2001; post-9/11 military cooperation

2002.assessment:
Bali bombing October 2002 — JI designated terrorist organization by US and UN. Targeted
sanctions on JI-linked individuals. Counter-terrorism cooperation with Polri massively
expanded. IMET restrictions partially lifted for counter-terrorism training. No comprehensive
economic sanctions on Indonesia. JI-related individual designations by OFAC/UNSC.
Essentially no state-level sanctions — only targeted individual designations.

sources.citation: UNSC Resolution 1452 JI designation; US OFAC individual designations

2003.assessment:
US IMET fully restored by 2005 — 2003 partial restoration. JI-linked individual sanctions
continuing. No comprehensive economic sanctions on Indonesia. Active US-Indonesia
counter-terrorism partnership. World Bank, ADB, IMF programs normal. EU trade relations
normal. Indonesia essentially sanction-free except JI-linked individual designations.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2003

2004.assessment:
IMET fully restored. US-Indonesia Comprehensive Partnership. No state-level sanctions.
JI individual designations by UNSC/OFAC continuing. SBY election: US immediately engaged
new government. December 2004 tsunami: massive international humanitarian engagement.
World Bank, IMF, ADB all active programs. Indonesia reintegrated fully into international
financial system. No sanctions.

2005.assessment:
No sanctions on Indonesia. Helsinki MOU on Aceh — international support. US-Indonesia
Strategic Partnership. World Bank, IMF, ADB active. Millennium Challenge Corporation
(MCC) engagement beginning. Australia Lombok Treaty negotiations. EU trade normal.
Japan ODA substantial. Indonesia fully integrated into international community — no sanctions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2005

2006.assessment:
No sanctions on Indonesia. Full diplomatic and trade normalization. US-Indonesia relations
strong on counter-terrorism, economic. World Bank, IMF, ADB active. Australia Lombok Treaty
signed November 2006. EU trade relations normal. No JI state-level sanctions. Papua:
some international concern but no sanctions. Indonesia a regional leader — ASEAN chair etc.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2006

2007.assessment:
No sanctions. Indonesia hosting UNFCCC COP13 in Bali December 2007 — Bali Road Map.
Full international engagement. US-Indonesia comprehensive economic partnership. World Bank
and MCC active. EU GSP trade preferences active. G20 membership in process. No sanctions
on Indonesian government officials. JI individual designations continuing at UNSC level.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2007

2008.assessment:
No sanctions. Indonesia joined G20 — formal major international economic actor. Full
trade and diplomatic relations globally. World Bank, IMF, ADB active programs. US
increased military and counter-terrorism cooperation. No human rights-linked sanctions
despite Papua concerns. EU normal trade relations. Japan and China active investment.
Indonesia sanction-free since IMET restoration.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2008; G20 Indonesia

2009.assessment:
No sanctions. Obama presidency: engaged Indonesia — Obama lived in Jakarta as child.
November 2010 Obama visited Jakarta. US-Indonesia Comprehensive Partnership elevated.
G20 Pittsburgh — Indonesia active. World Bank and MCC programs. EU no sanctions.
Counter-terrorism cooperation strong. Papua access restrictions noted by US but
no sanctions response. Indonesia fully integrated.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2009

2010.assessment:
No sanctions. Obama visited Jakarta November 2010 — elevated relations. US-Indonesia
Comprehensive Partnership formal launch. USAID programs active. World Bank, MCC, ADB
lending. EU no sanctions. Papua restrictions flagged by US but no sanctions. Indonesia
co-chairing various international forums. Fully integrated into international community.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2010

2011.assessment:
No sanctions. Indonesia hosting various ASEAN and East Asia Summit events. Bali Democracy
Forum active. US-Indonesia relations strong across security, economic, cultural dimensions.
World Bank, IMF, ADB, MCC programs. EU trade normal. No sanctions for Papua or human
rights concerns. Indonesia seen as democratic success story — no sanctions pressure.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2011

2012.assessment:
No sanctions. Indonesia ASEAN chair 2011. US-Indonesia Strategic Partnership upgrade
discussions. World Bank active. EU trade normal. Japan, China, Australia all active
economic partners. Papua: some international NGO concern but no government sanctions.
Indonesia presenting itself as moderate Muslim democracy — no international isolation.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2012

2013.assessment:
No sanctions. Snowden revelations: Australia/US spying on Yudhoyono and wife — Indonesia
recalled ambassador. Temporary diplomatic spat but no sanctions by Indonesia or on Indonesia.
Relations normalized 2014. World Bank, IMF active. EU trade relations normal. Fully
integrated. No human rights-based sanctions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2013; Snowden diplomatic spat

2014.assessment:
No sanctions. US-Australia-Indonesia relations normalized post-Snowden. Jokowi election
welcomed internationally. EITI compliance. World Bank, IMF, ADB, MCC programs. EU trade
normal including GSP preferences. China-Indonesia economic relations growing rapidly
(Belt and Road early). No state-level sanctions on Indonesia. Fully integrated.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2014

2015.assessment:
No sanctions. Indonesia hosting Bandung Conference 60th anniversary. Jokowi visited Obama
at White House. US-Indonesia Strategic Partnership advancing. World Bank, ADB, USAID active.
EU trade normal. Papua access restrictions noted by US annually but no sanctions imposed.
Drug executions (including Australian nationals) led to temporary Australian ambassador
recall but no sanctions. Fully integrated internationally.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2015; drug executions

2016.assessment:
No sanctions. Ahok prosecution: international community expressed concern but no sanctions.
Obama administration engaged Jokowi. World Bank, IMF, ADB programs. EU normal trade including
GSP. Papua: international attention but no sanctions. Indonesia participating in all
major international forums. Fully integrated — no state-level sanctions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2016

2017.assessment:
No sanctions. Trump administration engaged Indonesia — Jokowi visited Washington.
Ahok conviction: some US/EU concern expressed but no formal response. HTI ban: some
religious freedom concerns. ITE law: flagged in State Dept reports but no sanctions.
World Bank, IMF programs. EU trade normal. China FDI and BRI growing significantly.
No state-level sanctions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2017

2018.assessment:
No sanctions. Indonesia fully integrated in international community. G20 Buenos Aires —
Indonesia active. US-Indonesia trade: US concern about trade balance but no sanctions.
EU GSP trade preferences. World Bank, IMF, ADB active. China BRI projects ongoing.
Japan investment. Papua: flagged but no sanctions. Religious freedom: flagged but no
sanctions. Indonesia essentially sanction-free.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2018

2019.assessment:
No sanctions. Post-election: international community recognized Jokowi re-election. Papua
communications shutdown September 2019 — noted internationally but no sanctions. KPK
weakening noted in State Dept report but no action. World Bank, IMF, ADB programs. EU
trade normal. China BRI active. Indonesia fully integrated — no sanctions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2019

2020.assessment:
No sanctions. COVID-19: Indonesia received international support — WHO, COVAX. World Bank
$1bn COVID response. IMF rapid facility. US, EU normal relations. Omnibus Law protests:
flagged internationally but no sanctions. Papua military operations: noted but no sanctions.
Indonesia fully integrated — no state-level sanctions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2020; COVID international support

2021.assessment:
No sanctions. Biden administration: engaged Indonesia on climate, democracy. COVAX vaccines.
World Bank, ADB, IMF programs. Papua communications restrictions noted by US in annual report.
ITE law: noted in press freedom reports. EU deforestation regulation discussions potentially
affecting palm oil trade — not sanctions. No state-level sanctions on Indonesia.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2021

2022.assessment:
No sanctions. Indonesia G20 Presidency — Bali Summit November 2022. Biden-Xi meeting at
G20. Ukraine war: Indonesia did not sanction Russia, maintained neutral position.
Indonesian palm oil: EU deforestation regulation (EUDR) creates trade concerns but not
sanctions. World Bank, IMF, ADB programs. US-Indonesia Bilateral Investment Treaty
discussions. No state-level sanctions on Indonesia.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2022; G20 Bali

2023.assessment:
No sanctions. ASEAN chair (2023). Indonesia-US Strategic Partnership active. Biden visited
Indonesia for ASEAN summit September 2023. World Bank, ADB, MCC programs. EU trade including
ongoing EUDR palm oil discussions. Papua TPNPB hostage crisis: noted internationally but no
sanctions. New Criminal Code concerns raised by US/EU but no sanctions. No state sanctions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2023; ASEAN

2024.assessment:
No sanctions. Prabowo election: US and international community engaged new administration.
EU GSP trade preferences. World Bank, IMF, ADB programs. China Belt and Road projects.
ASEAN engagement. Papua ongoing concerns noted but no sanctions. Military role expansion
noted but no international sanctions response. Indonesia fully integrated.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2024 (estimated)

2025.assessment:
No sanctions. Indonesia continues full international integration. EUDR palm oil trade
concerns ongoing but not sanctions. Papua and ITE law flagged in US State Dept report.
World Bank, IMF, ADB programs active. China, Japan, US, EU all active economic partners.
Military expansion concerns noted. No state-level sanctions on Indonesia.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports Indonesia 2025 (forthcoming)

1990.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 6 (raw) = inverted ~28.6/100. Suharto New Order: severe restrictions
on assembly, expression, and press. ABRI intelligence (BAKIN) monitored activists.
Political parties limited to Golkar, PDI, PPP. Blasphemy law (1965) in force. Labor
unions controlled by SPSI. Freedom of movement restricted for some groups. Media
censorship pervasive — all TV state-owned.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1990-91, Indonesia

1991.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 6 (raw) = inverted ~28.6/100. Santa Cruz massacre November 1991:
TNI killed ~250 East Timorese mourners in Dili. No accountability for killers.
Continued military operations in Aceh under DOM. Journalists and activists imprisoned
for criticism. Press still censored. NGOs monitored. Assembly restricted.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1991-92, Indonesia

1992.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 6 (raw) = inverted ~28.6/100. 1992 elections: monitored opposition
parties only. PDI, PPP participation tightly controlled. Ethnic tensions in several regions.
Media censorship continues. Aceh DOM maintaining military operations. Political prisoners
held without trial. Assembly restricted except for Golkar-organized events.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1992-93, Indonesia

1993.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 6 (raw) = inverted ~28.6/100. Suharto re-elected for sixth term.
Labor rights severely restricted — strikes broken by ABRI. West Papua OPM suppressed.
Student protests at universities contained. Academic freedom limited — some professors
dismissed for critical views. Religious minorities monitored. Press censorship continues.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1993-94, Indonesia

1994.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 6 (raw) = inverted ~28.6/100. Tempo, Editor, Detik newspapers
banned June 1994 — severe press freedom restriction. Labor activist Marsinah murdered
1993 by military-linked forces. NGO activists monitored and arrested. PDI internal
tensions managed by government. Religious conversion from Islam restricted by practice.
Civil liberties severely constrained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1994-95, Indonesia

1995.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 6 (raw) = inverted ~28.6/100. Continued press restrictions,
self-censorship pervasive. Student activism growing at universities. Megawati's PDI
increasingly popular — government began planning her ouster. East Timor: Bishop Belo
and José Ramos-Horta received Nobel Peace Prize 1996 (announced 1995) — highlighting
human rights situation. Religious minorities (Christians in some areas) facing pressure.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995-96, Indonesia

1996.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 6 (raw) = inverted ~28.6/100. July 27, 1996 PDI headquarters
attack and Jakarta riots — ABRI complicit, 5+ killed. PRD (student democracy movement)
leaders arrested and imprisoned. Megawati supporters dispersed by force. Political
speech criminalized. Assembly banned for opposition groups. Press restrictions tight.
Worst year for civil liberties since 1965.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996-97, Indonesia

1997.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 6 (raw) = inverted ~28.6/100. 1997 elections: ethnic and anti-
Chinese riots in campaign period. Kalimantan inter-ethnic violence. Asian Financial Crisis:
economic crisis began to erode public trust. Some early protests tolerated. Student
movement growing on campuses but police response harsh. Aceh DOM continuing. Conditions
remained severe but pre-reform sentiment building.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997-98, Indonesia

1998.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 5 (raw) = inverted ~42.9/100. Regime change year. May 1998:
student protests, TNI killings of students (Trisakti, May 12: 4 killed), massive riots
in Jakarta (~1,200 killed), mass rapes of ethnic Chinese women. Suharto resigned May 21.
Habibie government: immediate liberalization — press restrictions lifted, political
prisoners released, political parties legalized. Major improvement H2 1998 but starting
from very low base. FH upgraded from 6 to 5.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998-99, Indonesia

1999.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Major improvement. Free press
established — hundreds of new newspapers, TV stations licensed. Political parties freely
organized — 48 parties competed in June elections. Freedom of assembly for political
purposes. East Timor: post-referendum TNI/militia violence August-September continued
to implicate military (countering overall improvement). Maluku communal violence began.
Mixed — significant improvement in political rights but ongoing military impunity.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999-2000, Indonesia

2000.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Press freedom greatly expanded.
Civil society active. Political assembly generally permitted. Maluku sectarian violence
killing thousands — government response slow and inadequate. Aceh emergency declared.
Security forces still committing human rights abuses with impunity in conflict zones.
Java broadly free; outer islands — Maluku, Papua, Aceh — severe restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000-01, Indonesia

2001.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Wahid impeachment followed
constitutional process. Press active and critical. Civil society organizations multiplying.
Maluku violence continued though declining. Aceh under martial law. Papua: OPM suppressed
but Papuan civil society more active. Religious minorities (Christians, Ahmadiyah) facing
growing pressure from Islamist groups.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001-02, Indonesia

2002.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Bali bombing October 2002 — JI
terrorist attack killed 202. Government enacted anti-terrorism law. Press free and
investigative. Civil assembly and association permitted. Religious minority tensions
continuing. Aceh: COHA signed Feb 2003. Maluku largely stabilized. Papua ongoing
low-level conflict but civil society more active.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002-03, Indonesia

2003.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Constitutional Court became
operational. JI continued operations (Marriott bombing August 2003). Aceh COHA failed
May 2003, military operations resumed. Press free. Labor rights improving — independent
unions allowed. Religious freedom formally guaranteed but Ahmadiyah discrimination
continuing. Overall partially free with ongoing concerns.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003-04, Indonesia

2004.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Free elections with genuine
competition. Press vigorous and free. Civil society active. Australia embassy bombing
September 2004 (JI). December 2004 tsunami — government allowed widespread humanitarian
access including international. Religious minority tensions including Ahmadiyah.
Aceh ongoing until MOU. Overall partially free with improving trajectory.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004-05, Indonesia

2005.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Helsinki MOU August 2005 ended
Aceh conflict — human rights situation in Aceh dramatically improved. Regional elections
(pilkada) expanded democratic participation. Bali bombings October 2005 (4 killed).
Anti-pornography legislation debate. Ahmadiyah decree issued by MUI calling for ban.
Overall civil liberties stable at partially free level.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005-06, Indonesia

2006.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Aceh post-conflict: civil liberties
improved significantly in province. Ahmadiyah government ban discussions intensifying —
government issued joint ministerial decree 2008. Press free and active. Labor unions
independent. Freedom of assembly generally respected in Java. Papua remains restricted.
Religious pluralism under stress.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006-07, Indonesia

2007.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Anti-Pornography Law passed —
controversial for potential overreach. Ahmadiyah attacks by FPI (Islamic Defenders Front).
Press free. Civil society vigorous. Religious freedom formally guaranteed but minorities
facing social and increasingly legal pressure. Labor rights respected. Papua low-level
conflict but civil society more open. Overall partially free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007-08, Indonesia

2008.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Joint Ministerial Decree against
Ahmadiyah issued June 2008 — allowed to exist but banned from proselytizing. FPI violence
against Ahmadiyah. ITE (Electronic Information and Transactions) Law passed — provisions
allowing criminal defamation of internet content. Press free. Political assembly permitted.
Labor rights improving. Overall partially free with some concerning trends.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008-09, Indonesia

2009.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Free elections — SBY re-elected.
JW Marriott/Ritz-Carlton bombings July 2009 (JI). Press vigorous. Civil society active.
ITE law beginning to be used against critics — first prosecutions. Ahmadiyah minority
situation deteriorating with more local government bans. Papua civil society monitored.
Freedom of assembly generally permitted.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009-10, Indonesia

2010.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. ITE law used against bloggers and
social media critics. Ahmadiyah violence in Cikeusik February 2011 (occurred early 2011).
Press free and active. Civil assembly permitted. Labor unions organizing effectively.
Papua: foreign journalists restricted from accessing Papua. Overall partially free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010-11, Indonesia

2011.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Cikeusik attack February 2011:
Ahmadiyah followers killed by mob — perpetrators given light sentences. GKI Yasmin
(Christian church) dispute in Bogor. Press free. Civil society vibrant. Political
assembly permitted. Papua journalists still restricted. ITE law used against online critics.
Religious pluralism weakening. Overall partially free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011-12, Indonesia

2012.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Press free and investigative.
Civil society active — NGOs engaged in policy debates. Political assembly permitted.
Papua: 400+ Papuans arrested for independence-related activities. Ahmadiyah: more
local government closures. ITE law: multiple bloggers prosecuted. Labor rights generally
respected. Internet access growing. Overall partially free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012-13, Indonesia

2013.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Press free and multiple ownership.
Civil society active. Religious minority situation: Shia minority attacked in Madura
(2012 precedent), Ahmadiyah ongoing restrictions. Papua: journalists still restricted.
Freedom of assembly: FPI harassment of LGBT groups. ITE law use expanding. Labor rights
improving. Overall partially free — religious freedom key weakness.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013-14, Indonesia

2014.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Competitive free elections — Jokowi
won. Press freedom generally maintained. Civil society active. FH rated Indonesia "Free"
(PR+CL combined). Religious intolerance toward minorities continuing. Papua: ongoing
restrictions on journalists and peaceful assembly. EITI compliance. Labor rights improving.
Positive trend overall.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014-15, Indonesia

2015.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Press freedom maintained —
multiple ownership, critical journalism. Civil society vigorous. Religious intolerance
toward Ahmadiyah, Shia, Christians in some regions. Papua: access for journalists and
international observers restricted (Jokowi partially lifted but reimposed restrictions).
Blasphemy law (1965) used occasionally. ITE law expanding use. Overall partially free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015-16, Indonesia

2016.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Ahok (Jakarta governor) blasphemy
prosecution began — politically motivated. 212 Movement protests November-December 2016:
~500,000+ protesters, Islamist identity politics mobilization. Press active. ITE law
used against social media critics. Discrimination against LGBT community increasing —
police raids on LGBT gatherings. Civil society space under pressure from Islamist groups.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016-17, Indonesia

2017.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Ahok convicted of blasphemy May 2017
— 2-year sentence. HTI banned by government decree July 2017 (bypassing courts). ITE law
used: multiple critics of Jokowi, Prabowo, others prosecuted. Press freedom maintained
for mainstream media but online speech increasingly criminalized. LGBT persecution increasing.
Papuan independence activists imprisoned. Religious pluralism declining.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017-18, Indonesia

2018.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. FH downgraded Indonesia to "Partly
Free." ITE law: 200+ cases annually. Blasphemy law increasing use — Meiliana sentenced
for complaining about mosque loudspeakers. Papua: arrests of independence demonstrators.
LGBT community facing increasing persecution. Press freedom: journalists harassed in Papua.
Religious minority situation worsening. Jokowi's accommodation of Islamist groups weakened
civil liberties protections.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018-19, Indonesia

2019.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Free elections April 2019. Post-
election protests: 8 killed. Papua: mass arrests August-September after student protests
in Surabaya (racial slurs). Papua riots — security lockdown, internet shutdown. West Papua
internet shutdown September 2019. ITE law continuing. Student protests September — police
response harsh. KPK weakening legislation. Civil liberties stable at partially free level.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019-20, Indonesia

2020.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. COVID-19: emergency powers used.
Critics of COVID response prosecuted under ITE. Papua: military operations continued;
civilians killed. TPNPB (West Papua National Liberation Army) escalating. Omnibus Law
protests: student/labor protests October 2020 — police tear gas and arrests. Press
freedom maintained for national outlets. Partly free with COVID-related constraints.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020-21, Indonesia

2021.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Papua: military offensive operations
continuing. TPNPB attacks on military and civilians. Church burnings. Government imposed
Papua communications blackouts repeatedly. ITE law: 300+ cases filed in 2021. Online
criticism of Jokowi/government criminalized. Press freedom: some journalists harassed.
LGBT: increasing arrests for same-sex relations. Partly free, deteriorating trend.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021-22, Indonesia

2022.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. New Criminal Code December 2022:
provisions on blasphemy (broadened), cohabitation criminalized, insulting president
criminal offense — concerning for civil liberties. Papua TPNPB hostage-takings and
violence. Kanjuruhan stadium disaster October 2022: police tear gas killed 135 —
accountability questioned. Press freedom: RSF ranked Indonesia 117th. ITE law continuing.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022-23, Indonesia

2023.assessment:
Freedom House CL score 4 (raw) = inverted ~57.1/100. Election campaign period:
press relatively free in covering elections. Constitutional Court MK age manipulation
for Gibran — critics arrested under ITE for comments. Papua: TPNPB hostages, military
operations. New Criminal Code to take effect 2025-2026. RSF press freedom index: Indonesia
score declining. Civil society organizations under pressure. Partly free with concerns.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023-24, Indonesia

2024.assessment:
Freedom House CL score estimated 4-5 (raw) = inverted ~42.9-57.1/100. Prabowo elected
February 2024. Military role in civilian government expanded. ITE law cases continuing.
Papua: military operations continuing with TPNPB. New Criminal Code implementation
timeline discussed — concerns about blasphemy and cohabitation provisions. Press freedom
challenges in Papua. Civil society generally active but space narrowing.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024-25, Indonesia (estimated)

2025.assessment:
Freedom House 2025 report not yet published. Prabowo presidency first full year.
Concerns: TNI returning to dual civilian-military role (new law allowing active military
in civilian posts). ITE law ongoing prosecutions. New Criminal Code in process of
implementation. Papua: ongoing TPNPB conflict with media restrictions. Religious minority
situation (Ahmadiyah, Christians in some regions) unchanged. Estimated CL score 4-5.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025 Indonesia (forthcoming)

1990.assessment:
Suharto New Order. Elite cohesion high — Suharto maintains tight control through ABRI
(military), Golkar (party), and technocratic-bureaucratic elite (Berkeley Mafia economists).
Patronage network disciplined: business cronies (Liem Sioe Liong, Bob Hasan) aligned with
regime. ABRI factions (Red and White vs. Green — secular nationalist vs. Islamic officers)
managed by Suharto personally. No visible elite challenge to Suharto's position.

sources.citation: Schwarz, A. 'A Nation in Waiting: Indonesia in the 1990s'

1991.assessment:
Elite cohesion high. Suharto controls all major power centers. Santa Cruz massacre
generated some international pressure but no elite defection. Military unity maintained —
generals aligned with Suharto. Suharto children (Tommy, Tutut) expanding business
interests with elite acquiescence. Habibie (technology minister) gaining favor as
possible successor but within controlled parameters.

sources.citation: Schwarz, A. 'A Nation in Waiting' analysis of 1991 elite dynamics

1992.assessment:
Elite cohesion high. 1992 elections managed smoothly. Military factions: Murdani (secular
nationalist) sidelined, Prabowo (son-in-law) rising. Suharto family business interests
protected by entire elite structure. Golkar mobilized efficiently. No elite splits
despite some grumbling about Suharto children's business monopolies.

sources.citation: Liddle, R.W. 'A Useful Fiction: Democratic Legitimation in New Order Indonesia'

1993.assessment:
Suharto re-elected for sixth term — elite unanimity. Habibie chosen as Research and
Technology Minister with growing political profile. ABRI (Commander Feisal Tanjung)
fully aligned with Suharto. Golkar elite intact. Signs of elite unease about Suharto
family business monopolization — but no open dissent. Business elite (Chinese-Indonesian
conglomerates) concerned but silent.

sources.citation: Robison, R. and Hadiz, V. 'Reorganising Power in Indonesia'

1994.assessment:
Minor tensions emerging. Suharto promoted ICMI (Indonesian Muslim Intellectuals Association)
with Habibie — shifting elite balance toward Islamic modernist technocrats and away from
secular nationalist-military (ABRI/Murdani wing). Newspaper bans showed Suharto still
controlling elite through media coercion. No open elite fragmentation.

sources.citation: Hefner, R.W. 'Civil Islam: Muslims and Democratization in Indonesia'

1995.assessment:
Minor tensions. Suharto engineering Megawati's ouster from PDI leadership — shows
elite managing opposition. Prabowo (military) vs. Wiranto (military) factions emerging.
Business elite increasingly tied to Suharto children creating internal competition.
ABRI factions: Prabowo (Islamic-nationalist aligned) vs. professional officers. Elite
competition managed within system — no open defection.

sources.citation: Crouch, H. 'The Army and Politics in Indonesia' (revised)

1996.assessment:
Significant elite competition managed. July 1996 PDI HQ attack required military collusion
— shows Suharto still commanding ABRI for political purposes. Prabowo faction gaining
influence (KOPASSUS). Some Golkar politicians uncomfortable with tactics. Elite still
unified around Suharto but tensions growing. Economic elite concerned about political
instability risks. No defections yet.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Indonesia 1996, PDI attack analysis

1997.assessment:
Significant competition, cracks emerging. Asian Financial Crisis H2 1997: elite unity
stressed as economic situation deteriorated. IMF demands threatened Suharto family
businesses directly. Prabowo-Wiranto military rivalry intensifying. Business elite
(Chinese-Indonesian conglomerates) starting capital flight. Golkar 74% in elections showed
Suharto still in control but economic crisis creating instability pressures.

sources.citation: Haggard, S. 'The Political Economy of the Asian Financial Crisis'
ICG Indonesia: From Soeharto to Habibie, 1999

1998.assessment:
Elite fragmentation — regime change. January-April 1998: Suharto re-elected for 7th term
with Habibie as VP but IMF refused to endorse. May 1998: student protests, Trisakti
killings, Jakarta riots. Military elite split: Prabowo (provoked violence to justify
emergency) vs. Wiranto (urged Suharto to resign). Key business elites fled or withdrew
support. DPR members and Golkar leadership abandoned Suharto. May 21: Suharto resigned.
Elite fragmentation caused regime collapse.

sources.citation: ICG Indonesia: From Soeharto to Habibie, Asia Report No.31, 1999
Mietzner, M. 'Military Politics, Islam, and the State in Indonesia'

1999.assessment:
Open factional conflict. Habibie (Golkar/Islamic modernist) vs. Megawati (PDI-P/nationalist)
vs. Wahid (PKB/traditional Islamic) in post-reformasi elite contest. Military split:
Prabowo dismissed, facing tribunal; Wiranto continuing; military factions competing.
June 1999 elections: multiparty results created coalition negotiations. October MPR:
Wahid elected president in complex coalition-building — elite bargaining intense.
No unified elite but democratic process managing competition.

sources.citation: ICG Indonesia: From Soeharto to Habibie; Wahid's Balancing Act, 1999

2000.assessment:
Significant competition. Wahid presidency: DPR-president conflict intensifying. Multiple
parties in unstable coalition — PDI-P, Golkar, PKB. Military reform creating officer
corps factionalism. Wahid fired ministers from rival parties, provoking DPR backlash.
Communal violence in Maluku and Kalimantan: regional elites fragmented. Aceh emergency.
Elite competition intense but managed within democratic framework.

sources.citation: ICG Indonesia: Wahid's Balancing Act, Asia Report 2000

2001.assessment:
Significant competition — managed. MPR impeachment of Wahid July 2001 — Megawati became
president through constitutional process. Elite factions (PDI-P, Golkar, PPP, military)
aligned to remove Wahid. Megawati built broader coalition. Military: Wiranto out of favor,
Sutiyoso (Jakarta governor) and others aligned with Megawati. Elite competition still
intense but managed through democratic institutions.

sources.citation: ICG Indonesia: The Megawati Presidency, Asia Report 2001

2002.assessment:
Significant competition. Pre-election positioning: Golkar, PDI-P, military-backed parties
all preparing for 2004 elections. Bali bombing October 2002 created temporary elite
unity on counter-terrorism. Prabowo rehabilitating politically — Gerindra being formed.
Elite competition within reformed democratic framework. No coup risk. Coalition politics
managing factional differences.

2003.assessment:
Competition intensifying as 2004 elections approach. Megawati popularity declining.
SBY (Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono) resigned from cabinet to run for president — elite
split in Megawati coalition. Prabowo's Gerindra in formation. Democratic Party (SBY)
organizing. Multiple presidential candidates from various elite factions. Competition
managed democratically — no extra-legal challenges.

2004.assessment:
Elite competition resolved democratically. SBY defeated Megawati (Golkar) in first
direct presidential election — significant: first peaceful elite transition through
direct election. Prabowo allied with Megawati in second round. SBY formed coalition
government including former competitors. Military: firmly civilian-subordinate under
SBY (himself former general but now as civilian). Elite competition managed through
democratic institutions. Minor tensions functional.

sources.citation: ICG Indonesia: After the Bali Bombing; SBY election analysis 2004

2005.assessment:
Minor tensions, functional governance. SBY governing with broad coalition (Sekretariat
Gabungan — Setgab). Helsinki MOU on Aceh: SBY-Kalla leadership demonstrated elite
coherence on major strategic issues. Jusuf Kalla (Golkar) as VP provided cross-party
bridge. Minor tensions within coalition — budget disputes. Overall elite cohesion good.

2006.assessment:
Minor tensions, functional. SBY coalition government managing. Golkar under Kalla
supportive. Aceh elections November 2006 — ex-GAM governor Irwandi Yusuf won — peaceful
integration of former insurgents into political elite. Economic team (Sri Mulyani finance
minister) effective. Some coalition tensions on budget and regulatory issues. Prabowo
(Gerindra not yet official) positioning for future.

2007.assessment:
Minor tensions, functional. SBY-Kalla coalition stable. Pre-2009 election positioning
beginning — Kalla considering presidential run on own ticket. Democratic Party (SBY)
building electoral machinery. Prabowo launching Gerindra party. PDI-P rebuilding under
Megawati. Elite competition managed within democratic framework. No coup risk.

2008.assessment:
Minor tensions. SBY-Kalla coalition approaching end of term. Kalla declared presidential
candidacy (against SBY) — coalition tension but managed within democratic norms.
Global financial crisis: elite coordinated economic response (Sri Mulyani, Bank Indonesia).
Century Bank bailout decision later controversial (2010 investigation). DPR active in
oversight. Military firmly subordinate. Elite competition ahead of 2009 elections.

2009.assessment:
Minor tensions — competitive but functional. SBY (with Boediono) vs. Megawati-Prabowo
vs. Kalla-Wiranto: three-way presidential race. SBY won 60.8% first round — clear
result. Losers accepted outcome. KPK-Polri conflict ("Cicak vs Buaya") showed elite
competition using institutions. SBY second term formed broad coalition. Elite competition
through democratic mechanisms — no extra-legal challenges.

2010.assessment:
Significant competition. Century Bank scandal: DPR investigation targeted Sri Mulyani
and Boediono (VP) — elite conflict using parliament. Prabowo-Gerindra growing.
SBY coalition management challenged by intra-coalition disputes. KPK prosecuting
DPR members from multiple parties. Regional heads (bupati/walikota) increasingly
autonomous and some corrupt. No coup risk but elite competition intensifying.

2011.assessment:
Significant competition, managed. SBY second-term coalition managing multiple factions.
Sri Mulyani left Finance Ministry (World Bank). Golkar within coalition but with tensions.
Pre-2014 positioning beginning. Jokowi gaining national profile as Solo mayor. PDI-P
rebuilding. Elite competition within democratic norms — no extra-legal challenges.
Corruption within elite widespread — KPK prosecuting multiple figures.

2012.assessment:
Significant competition. Jokowi won Jakarta governor election — new face in elite politics.
Constitutional Court Chief Justice Akil Mochtar bribery (discovered 2013). Pre-2014
election positioning: Prabowo (Gerindra), Jokowi/Megawati (PDI-P), Golkar (ARB — Aburizal
