Bakrie), Demokrat (weakening). KPK prosecuting multiple DPR members. Elite competition
intense with 2014 approaching.

2013.assessment:
Significant competition. Constitutional Court Chief Justice Akil Mochtar arrested October
2013 — showed deep corruption in highest judicial elite. PKS (Prosperous Justice Party)
chairman Lutfi Hasan Ishaaq convicted. SBY second term weakened by scandals.
PDI-P rallied behind Jokowi. Prabowo-Gerindra strong. Golkar/ARB weak. Democratic Party
(SBY) collapsing. Elite competition fragmented ahead of 2014.

2014.assessment:
Active power struggle — resolved democratically. Jokowi (PDI-P/progressive) vs. Prabowo
(Gerindra/nationalist) — two-candidate race, closely contested (53-47). Prabowo refused
initial result, filed Constitutional Court challenge — court upheld Jokowi. Prabowo's
coalition (KMP) won DPR majority initially but Jokowi built counter-coalition (KIH).
Constitutional crisis threatened but resolved. Elite split deep but managed constitutionally.

sources.citation: ICG Indonesia: Indonesia's 2014 Elections, Asia Report 2014

2015.assessment:
Significant competition. Jokowi managing DPR opposition (Prabowo coalition majority).
Gradual defections from Prabowo's KMP to Jokowi coalition through patronage. Golkar
split — two competing chairmen. KPK-Polri conflict 2015: police arrested KPK leaders
on dubious charges — elite institutional warfare. Jokowi mediated KPK-Polri crisis.
Military cohesive under civilian control. Elite competition managed.

2016.assessment:
Significant competition. Ahok blasphemy prosecution: opposition (Prabowo + Islamic
parties + PKS) mobilized Islamist mass movements. Jokowi coalition managing pressure.
212 Movement December 2016 — largest demonstrations since reformasi. Prabowo and
Gerindra aligned with Islamist populism against Jokowi. DPR: Jokowi coalition now
majority through defections. Elite competition managed through democratic and legal channels.

2017.assessment:
Significant competition. Ahok convicted May 2017 — Prabowo coalition won political battle.
HTI banned July 2017. Jokowi consolidated position through coalition management. Prabowo
positioning for 2019 presidential race. Military: Gatot Nurmantyo (TNI Commander) showing
political ambitions — Jokowi replaced him with Hadi Tjahjanto December 2017.
Elite competition within constitutional limits.

2018.assessment:
Significant competition. Jokowi chose Ma'ruf Amin (MUI head) as VP — accommodation to
Islamist pressure, weakening civil liberties. Prabowo chose Sandiaga Uno as running mate.
Prabowo-Jokowi rematch confirmed. Novanto (DPR Speaker) convicted by KPK. Regional elites
aligned with national factions. Competition intense but within constitutional framework.

2019.assessment:
Active power struggles — resolved constitutionally. Jokowi won 55.5% vs. Prabowo 44.5%.
Prabowo refused initial result, declared victory fraudulently. Post-election violence May
21-22 (8 killed). Constitutional Court dismissed Prabowo's challenge. Jokowi invited
Prabowo into cabinet as Defense Minister — ended factional war by co-optation. Parliament:
massive coalition including Gerindra. Elite competition managed through co-optation.

sources.citation: ICG Indonesia 2019 election analysis; Jokowi-Prabowo cabinet

2020.assessment:
Significant competition, managed. Jokowi-Prabowo coalition broad — most parties
included. Omnibus Law controversy: massive protests October 2020 but elite mostly
aligned on economic reform. COVID response: elite coordination improved. KPK
weakened with elite acquiescence. Prabowo (Defense Ministry) well integrated.
Luhut Binsar (Jokowi's closest advisor) coordinating elite extensively.

2021.assessment:
Minor tensions, functional. Broad Jokowi super-coalition stable. Prabowo positioned as
2024 frontrunner within governing coalition. MK ruled Omnibus Law unconstitutional but
government legislated fix. Luhut Binsar and Prabowo: two main power centers under Jokowi.
Some speculation about third term (ruled unconstitutional). Elite competition muted
within broad coalition structure.

2022.assessment:
Minor tensions, functional. G20 Presidency — Jokowi government cohesive on international
stage. Prabowo positioned for 2024, still in coalition. Gibran (Jokowi's son) elected
Solo mayor 2021. Jokowi's IKN (Nusantara capital) project supported by coalition.
Early election extension discussion (Jokowi denied). Elite competition for 2024 positioning
but managed within coalition framework.

2023.assessment:
Significant competition. 2024 election triangular competition: Prabowo-Gibran (backed by
Jokowi), Anies Baswedan (backed by PKS/opposition), Ganjar Pranowo (PDI-P/Megawati).
MK Chief Justice age manipulation to allow Gibran — shows Jokowi using institutions.
Jokowi split from PDI-P by backing Prabowo-Gibran against PDI-P's Ganjar. Family dynasty
politics. Elite fragmentation within previously unified Jokowi coalition.

2024.assessment:
Active power struggles — resolved. Prabowo-Gibran won 58.6% in first round — decisive.
Anies and Ganjar filed challenges; dismissed. Prabowo inaugurated October 20, 2024 with
Jokowi retiring. Jokowi family influence: Gibran as VP. Cabinet included broad coalition.
Megawati-PDI-P excluded initially. Post-election: Prabowo building own coalition.
Tensions: Jokowi legacy vs. Prabowo's own agenda. Managed through coalition politics.

2025.assessment:
Minor tensions functional. Prabowo presidency consolidating. Luhut Binsar advisory role
continuing. Gibran as VP. PDI-P gradually reconciling with Prabowo government. Cabinet
broad coalition — Golkar, PKB, Gerindra, Nasdem. Military role in civilian government
expanding under new law. Jokowi's influence through family network ongoing but managed.
Elite competition within governing coalition — manageable.

1990.assessment:
Suharto New Order. Highly centralized authoritarian state. Parliament (DPR/MPR) rubber-stamp
dominated by Golkar. Judiciary not independent — courts follow executive direction. Military
(ABRI) holds dual function (dwifungsi): military and civilian governance roles. Bureaucracy
functional but deeply politicized. Elections held but not competitive. Institutions formally
exist but serve authoritarian consolidation rather than democratic governance.

1991.assessment:
New Order institutions unchanged. Santa Cruz massacre in East Timor revealed gap between
formal legal institutions and actual state practice — security forces killed ~250 civilians
with no accountability. Courts remained subservient. DPR continued as rubber stamp.
Bureaucracy functioned to maintain Suharto's patronage network rather than public service.

1992.assessment:
1992 general elections held — Golkar won 68% as expected. PDI and PPP allowed nominal
participation as vetted opposition. Legislative institutions managed tightly by Suharto.
Judiciary non-independent. Constitutional Court did not exist. State institutions function
but only to maintain authoritarian stability, not democratic governance.

1993.assessment:
Suharto re-elected by MPR for sixth term. Habibie promoted as vice presidential candidate,
indicating succession planning within authoritarian framework. Institutions stable but
entirely serving Suharto family business interests and military patronage. Judiciary
continued to follow executive direction in politically sensitive cases.

1994.assessment:
Three newspapers (Tempo, Editor, Detik) banned — demonstrating government control over
information flows and lack of press freedom. Institutions continued to function in
authoritarian mode. Labor protests increasing but suppressed through ABRI and Golkar-
controlled unions (SPSI). Bureaucracy deeply corruptly tied to Suharto family interests.

1995.assessment:
New Order institutions stable but increasingly brittle. Suharto family businesses (Bakrie,
Suharto children) operating throughout economy. Student activism growing. PDI under Megawati
increasingly popular, leading Suharto to engineer her ouster from party leadership.
Institutions hollowed out by cronyism and corruption at all levels.

1996.assessment:
Suharto-backed Soerjadi faction stormed PDI headquarters July 27, 1996 — security forces
complicit in attacks on Megawati supporters. Jakarta riots followed with at least 5 killed.
PRD activists arrested. ABRI intervention in civilian politics explicit and undisguised.
Courts used to persecute political opponents. Institutions severely degraded and weaponized.

1997.assessment:
1997 general elections: Golkar won 74% — most controlled result in New Order history.
Ethnic and anti-Chinese riots across Kalimantan and Java during campaign. Asian Financial
Crisis hit rupiah hard in H2 1997. IMF negotiations began. Institutions functionally
intact but deeply corrupted and increasingly unable to manage economic crisis. State
capacity declining as economic situation deteriorated.

1998.assessment:
Regime change year. Economic collapse (rupiah -80%), May riots killed ~1,200, Suharto
resigned May 21. Habibie presidency: institutions transitioning. Parliament MPR began
constitutional reform process. DPR-RI initiated legislative changes. Key reforms:
Golkar hold weakened, political parties legalized. ABRI dwifungsi under pressure.
Institutions severely degraded but reform process initiated. Transitional justice absent.
Corruption investigations limited — Habibie protected Suharto interests.

sources.citation: ICG Indonesia: From Soeharto to Habibie, Asia Report 1999

1999.assessment:
June 1999 legislative elections — first free elections since 1955. PDI-P won 34%, Golkar
19%, PKB 13%. October 1999 MPR elected Abdurrahman Wahid (Gus Dur) as president.
East Timor referendum August 30: 78.5% for independence, followed by TNI/militia violence.
Massive institutional reform underway: multiparty system, regional autonomy laws,
press freedom restored. ABRI renamed TNI, beginning of separation from police (Polri).
Institutions partially functional — reforming but chaotic. Rule of law fragile.

sources.citation: ICG Indonesia: Keeping the Military Under Control, 1999

2000.assessment:
Wahid presidency struggling. DPR increasingly assertive — issued censures against president.
Decentralization laws (22/1999 and 25/1999) being implemented, creating regional governance
challenges. TNI/Polri formally separated April 2000. Aceh emergency declaration. Communal
violence in Maluku, Kalimantan. Constitutional court still absent; Mahkamah Agung (Supreme
Court) beginning to assert independence. Institutions partially functional but unstable.

2001.assessment:
MPR impeached President Wahid July 2001 — Megawati Sukarnoputri became president.
Constitutional process followed proper procedure — demonstrates institutional functioning.
KPK (anti-corruption commission) proposed. Mahkamah Konstitusi (Constitutional Court)
established in 2001 amendments (became operational 2003). Press freedom maintained.
Multiparty system functioning. Military reform progressing but incomplete.

2002.assessment:
Direct presidential elections approved (constitutional amendment 2002) — major institutional
reform. Bali bombing October 12 killed 202 — government response showed improving counter-
terrorism institutions. Helsinki MOU negotiations on Aceh not yet complete. KPK not yet
operational. Judicial reform advancing slowly. Institutions improving but fragile.

2003.assessment:
Mahkamah Konstitusi (Constitutional Court) became operational 2003 — key institutional
milestone. Aceh COHA signed February; broke down May, military operations resumed.
TNI reform continuing under civilian oversight pressure. DPR active in oversight role.
KPK law passed December 2002, KPK becoming operational. Institutions strengthening.

2004.assessment:
First direct presidential election April/July 2004 — Yudhoyono (SBY) won 60.6%.
Institutional landmark: peaceful transfer of power through competitive direct election.
KPK fully operational. Constitutional Court adjudicating electoral disputes. Legislative
elections March 2004 also competitive. December 26: Indian Ocean tsunami killed ~167,000
in Aceh — government response tested institutional capacity.

2005.assessment:
Helsinki Memorandum of Understanding August 15, 2005 ended Aceh conflict — major success
for Indonesian institutional diplomacy and democratic negotiation. KPK began anti-corruption
prosecutions. Regional elections (pilkada) held across provinces — direct democratic
governance extending to subnational level. Institutions consolidating democracy.
Constitutional Court active. TNI reform continuing.

2006.assessment:
Democratic consolidation continuing. KPK prosecuting high-profile corruption cases.
Aceh governance elections November 2006 — Irwandi Yusuf (former GAM) won governor.
Constitutional Court active. DPR legislative function strengthened. Military reform
progressed — TNI commercial businesses ordered divested. Institutions functional
with ongoing improvements. Corruption remains institutional challenge.

2007.assessment:
Institutions consolidating. KPK increasingly effective — multiple bribery convictions.
Mahkamah Konstitusi playing active role in electoral dispute resolution. Anti-pornography
bill controversy showed DPR legislative activism. SBY presidency focused on stability
and growth. TNI fully subordinate to civilian control for first time in history.
Bureaucratic reform progressing slowly.

2008.assessment:
Global financial crisis limited effect on Indonesia (GDP growth 6%). Institutions
handling economic stress better than 1997-98 crisis — Bank Indonesia, Finance Ministry
more capable. KPK continued high-profile prosecutions. Constitutional Court upheld
election results. Press freedom maintained. Institutions functional with ongoing
corruption challenges.

2009.assessment:
April 2009 legislative elections, July 2009 presidential election — SBY re-elected with
60.8%. Second consecutive peaceful competitive election. KPK-Polri conflict emerged —
police attempted to frame KPK leaders ("Cicak vs Buaya"). Constitutional Court
adjudicated disputes. Legislature active. Institutional functioning solid.

2010.assessment:
Century Bank scandal exposed corruption in Bank Indonesia and Finance Ministry.
DPR investigation into Bank Indonesia bailout. KPK under pressure but continuing.
Regional governments (post-decentralization) becoming corruption hotspots with 200+
regional heads prosecuted over decade. Institutions functional but systemic corruption
remains major challenge.

2011.assessment:
Indonesia ranked "Partly Free" improving toward "Free" by Freedom House. Constitutional
Court active. KPK prosecuted DPR members (legislative branch corruption). Papua autonomy
debates. Administrative court (PTUN) handling government disputes. Judicial reform
progressing. Some concerns about TNI drift back toward political involvement.

2012.assessment:
Constitutional Court Chief Justice Akil Mochtar arrested by KPK for bribery in 2013
(discovered 2013). MK integrity damaged. Jokowi elected Jakarta governor 2012 —
demonstrated competitive subnational elections. KPK prosecuted multiple high-profile
officials. Parliament passing legislation. Institutions functional with corruption
challenges continuing.

2013.assessment:
KPK Chief Justice Akil Mochtar arrested October 2013 — major blow to Constitutional
Court independence. Multiple DPR members convicted. PKS chairman Lutfi Hasan Ishaaq
corruption conviction. SBY administration weakened by scandals. Democratic Policing
Program progressing. Institutions functional but increasingly stressed by corruption.

2014.assessment:
April 2014 legislative elections; July 2014 presidential election — Jokowi narrowly
defeated Prabowo (53% to 47%). Constitutional Court validated Jokowi's victory after
Prabowo challenge. Third peaceful competitive presidential transfer. EITI candidacy
pursued. Legislature retained power despite Prabowo coalition majority in DPR initially.
Institutions demonstrating resilience. Freedom House rated Indonesia "Free" in 2014.

2015.assessment:
Jokowi reformist agenda. KPK-Polri conflict escalated — police arrested KPK leaders on
questionable charges. Jokowi mediated KPK-Polri crisis. Indonesia granted EITI compliant
status 2014 (effective). Bureaucratic reform: merit-based hiring tested. DPR actively
engaged in oversight. Constitutional Court functioning. Some civil society restrictions.

2016.assessment:
Ahok (Jakarta governor) criminally charged for blasphemy — large demonstrations mobilizing
Islamist identity politics. 212 Movement protests December 2016. Concerns about rule of
law in blasphemy prosecution. KPK continued anti-corruption prosecutions. Constitutional
Court active. Overall institutional functioning maintained though political pressures grew.

2017.assessment:
Ahok convicted of blasphemy May 2017 — politically motivated prosecution concerning for
rule of law. HTI (Hizbut Tahrir Indonesia) banned July 2017 using government decree
rather than court order. ITE law increasingly used against critics. KPK prosecuted
e-KTP (identity card) scandal implicating hundreds of DPR members. Institutions
functional but rule-of-law quality declining.

2018.assessment:
Regional elections June 2018 — 171 simultaneous pilkada. Jokowi chose Ma'ruf Amin
(conservative cleric) as VP running mate — accommodation to Islamist pressure. KPK
prosecuted House Speaker Setya Novanto (e-KTP). Constitutional Court resolving
election disputes. Institutions functioning but civil society space narrowing.
Freedom House dropped Indonesia to "Partly Free."

2019.assessment:
April 2019 simultaneous legislative and presidential elections — Jokowi re-elected 55.5%.
Post-election protests May 21-22 (8 killed) after Prabowo refused result. Constitutional
Court validated election. DPR passed KPK revision bill weakening anti-corruption body —
KPK supervisory board made executive-appointed. Student protests in September. ITE law
used against critics. Institutions functional but KPK weakening is concerning for rule of law.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 pandemic: government emergency powers but no formal state of emergency.
Omnibus Law (Job Creation Law) passed October 2020 — criticized for bypassing normal
legislative process. Constitutional Court later found Omnibus Law "conditionally
unconstitutional" 2021. KPK further weakened: investigator firings, re-assignment of
cases. December 2020 pilkada held despite pandemic. Institutions under strain.

2021.assessment:
Constitutional Court ruled Omnibus Law "conditionally unconstitutional" November 2021 —
ordered revision within 2 years. KPK leadership continued to politicize institution.
ITE law revision discussion but no reform. Papua autonomy extended via legislation.
COVID-19 response improving. Jokowi-Prabowo coalition broadened (Prabowo became
Defense Minister). Institutions under ongoing stress from anti-reform coalition.

2022.assessment:
New Criminal Code passed December 2022 — controversial provisions on blasphemy, cohabitation,
insult of president. Election campaign period began. KPK continued but weakened.
DPR Akil Mochtar KPK-scandal legacy. Constitutional Court active on electoral matters.
G20 Presidency 2022 — Indonesia performed well in international diplomacy. Capital
relocation project (Nusantara) legislation passed January 2022.

2023.assessment:
Election campaign year for February 2024 elections. Constitutional Court controversy:
Chief Justice Anwar Usman (Jokowi's brother-in-law) changed age requirement ruling
to allow Gibran Rakabuming (Jokowi's son) as VP candidate — subsequently found to
violate ethics code. Constitutional Court ethics body removed Anwar Usman as chair.
Shows institutional checks functioning partially. KPK corruption conviction rate high.

2024.assessment:
February 14, 2024 elections: Prabowo-Gibran won 58.6%. Constitutional Court dismissed
challenges from Anies and Ganjar campaigns. Jokowi term ended October 20, 2024 —
Prabowo inaugurated. Institutions managed transfer of power. Concerns: Jokowi influence
continuing through son (Gibran) as VP, family network in government. DPR legislative
function active. KPK under continued pressure. Military reform questions.

2025.assessment:
Prabowo presidency first full year. Cabinet includes Jokowi loyalists and military figures.
Prabowo expanded military role in civilian affairs — concerns about TNI dwifungsi return.
New law allowing active military in civilian government positions passed 2024-2025.
KPK under political pressure. ITE law restricts critics. Overall institutions functional
but democratic quality declining. Civil society space narrowing.

1990.assessment:
Suharto New Order. Active armed conflicts: (1) East Timor — Falintil resistance vs TNI,
hundreds of deaths annually; (2) Aceh — GAM insurgency under DOM (military operations zone);
(3) West Papua — OPM low-level. State violence against civilians documented. Annual conflict
deaths estimated in hundreds across all theaters. Moderate political violence.

1991.assessment:
Santa Cruz massacre November 12, 1991 — TNI soldiers killed approximately 250 East
Timorese mourners at funeral procession in Dili. Major escalation documented internationally.
Aceh DOM operations continuing. UCDP estimates hundreds of battle deaths. Moderate violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 1991; Amnesty International Santa Cruz report

1992.assessment:
Falintil leader Xanana Gusmao captured November 1992; major blow to East Timor resistance.
Aceh DOM operations continuing. Overall violence decreasing slightly in East Timor post-capture
but GAM Aceh active. Hundreds of deaths annually across all theaters. Moderate violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 1992; Human Rights Watch

1993.assessment:
Aceh DOM operations reducing insurgency but generating civilian casualties. East Timor
post-Xanana capture: resistance weakened but continuing. West Papua OPM low-level.
Hundreds of deaths across all three conflict zones. Moderate violence level.

1994.assessment:
Aceh: GAM rebuilding after DOM crackdown. East Timor: continued resistance. Some intercommunal
tensions in outer islands. Annual deaths estimated in hundreds. Moderate violence level.

1995.assessment:
East Timor: Falintil continuing resistance despite leadership imprisonment. Aceh:
GAM operations ongoing. West Papua: OPM low-level. Hundreds of deaths annually.
Moderate political violence level throughout Suharto New Order.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 1996; July 1996 PDI attack

1997.assessment:
Asian Financial Crisis beginning July 1997. Intercommunal violence beginning in Kalimantan
and Maluku. Active insurgencies in Aceh, East Timor, Papua. Student protests intensifying.
Hundreds of deaths across conflict zones. Moderate violence increasing.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 1997; Asian Financial Crisis context

1998.assessment:
Regime change year. May 1998 riots: ~1,200 killed in Jakarta and other cities; widespread
sexual violence against ethnic Chinese women documented. Suharto resigned May 21. Intercommunal
violence spreading (Kalimantan, Maluku). East Timor referendum process beginning. Active
insurgencies continuing. UCDP estimates >1,000 deaths in 1998. High political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 1998; Human Rights Watch Jakarta riots

1999.assessment:
Most violent post-Suharto year. East Timor: pro-Indonesian militias/TNI killed ~1,400 in
post-referendum violence (September 1999); INTERFET intervention. Maluku intercommunal
religious conflict began (thousands killed 1999-2002). Aceh emergency escalation.
UCDP estimates >2,000 deaths in 1999. Severe violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 1999; East Timor post-referendum violence

2000.assessment:
Maluku intercommunal conflict peak (estimated 2,000-5,000 killed 1999-2002 total).
Aceh emergency operation (DOM 2 effectively). East Timor now independent/UNTAET.
Papua tensions. Multiple active conflict zones. UCDP estimates 1,000-2,000 deaths.
Moderate to high political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2000; Maluku conflict documentation

2001.assessment:
Maluku intercommunal conflict continuing. Aceh emergency military operations. Poso
conflict (Sulawesi) active. Wahid ousted, Megawati became president July 2001.
Multiple conflict zones active. UCDP estimates hundreds to low thousands of deaths.
Moderate violence across multiple theaters.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2001; Maluku; Aceh; Poso

2002.assessment:
Maluku Malino II peace agreement February 2002 reduced intercommunal violence. Aceh:
Cessation of Hostilities Agreement (COHA) signed December 2002. Poso violence continuing.
Bali bombings October 2002 (202 killed — major jihadist terrorism). Papua tensions.
Overall violence moderate but Bali bombing a major event. Hundreds of deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2002; Bali bombings October 2002

2003.assessment:
Aceh: COHA broke down May 2003; martial law declared; TNI offensive. Aceh deaths
increased significantly (estimated 400-600 killed in 2003). JW Marriott Jakarta bombing
August 2003 (12 killed). Poso continuing. Hundreds of deaths nationally. Moderate violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2003; Aceh martial law; Marriott bombing

2004.assessment:
Aceh: martial law downgraded to civil emergency. Australian Embassy bombing Jakarta
September 2004 (11 killed). December 26 tsunami killed ~170,000 in Aceh — separate
from conflict but affected all operations. SBY won first direct presidential election.
Conflict deaths (non-tsunami) hundreds. Low-moderate violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2004; Embassy bombing; tsunami December 2004

2005.assessment:
Helsinki MOU signed August 15, 2005 ending Aceh conflict. Major de-escalation. Poso
conflict winding down (Malino II agreements implemented). West Papua OPM very low level.
Annual conflict deaths dropping significantly. Minimal to low political violence.
Key turning point: Aceh peace achieved.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2005; Helsinki MOU August 2005

2006.assessment:
Aceh Law of Governing Aceh passed; ex-GAM participating in democracy. Poso: last
major violence incidents. West Papua OPM very low level. Sporadic JI terrorism.
Annual deaths declining sharply. Minimal to low political violence. Democratic consolidation.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2006; Aceh peace consolidation

2007.assessment:
Aceh peaceful with ex-GAM party participation. Low-level violence continuing in West Papua
(OPM incidents). Sporadic JI terrorism. Annual conflict deaths below 100. Minimal violence.

2008.assessment:
Papua OPM low-level. JI network dismantled but residual cells. No major domestic
terrorism incidents. Annual conflict deaths below 100. Minimal political violence.
Indonesia consolidated as stable democracy by this period.

2009.assessment:
Ritz-Carlton/Marriott bombings Jakarta July 2009 (9 killed). JI residual cell activity.
Papua OPM low-level. SBY reelected October 2009. Annual deaths below 100 ex-terrorist incidents.
Minimal to low political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2009; Marriott/Ritz bombing July 2009

2010.assessment:
Papua/West Papua: OPM incidents increasing slightly. Sporadic JI splinter group activity.
No major national conflict. Annual deaths below 100. Minimal political violence overall.

2011.assessment:
Papua conflict intensifying slightly with OPM/TPNPB incidents. Sectarian attacks
on Christian churches in some areas. Annual deaths below 100. Minimal political violence.

2012.assessment:
Papua/West Papua ongoing OPM/TPNPB incidents. Sporadic sectarian violence. Annual
deaths below 100 nationally. Minimal political violence.

2013.assessment:
Papua/West Papua TPNPB incidents. Isolated JI-linked incidents. Annual deaths below
100 nationally. Minimal political violence overall.

2014.assessment:
Jokowi elected July 2014. Papua/West Papua TPNPB incidents continuing. Post-election
tensions in some areas. Annual deaths below 100. Minimal political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2014; Jokowi election

2015.assessment:
Papua/West Papua TPNPB incidents. ISIS-linked radicalization concerns emerging.
Annual deaths below 100. Minimal political violence.

2016.assessment:
January 2016 Jakarta attack (ISIS-linked; 8 killed including 4 attackers). Papua TPNPB
ongoing. Political polarization over Jakarta gubernatorial race (Ahok blasphemy case
emerging). Sectarian political mobilization. Annual deaths below 100. Minimal violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2016; Jakarta attack January 2016

2017.assessment:
Papua TPNPB incidents. Sporadic terrorist incidents. Ahok blasphemy trial and conviction.
Anti-Ahok protests and sectarian political tensions. Annual deaths below 100.
Minimal political violence despite political polarization.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2017; Ahok case; Papua TPNPB

2018.assessment:
May 2018 Surabaya church bombings (ISIS-linked; 28 killed). Papua TPNPB escalation.
New anti-terrorism law passed. Annual deaths low-moderate (terrorism + Papua).
Low political violence overall.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2018; Surabaya bombings May 2018
UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2019; Papua TPNPB escalation; post-election protests

2020.assessment:
Papua/West Papua TPNPB continuing. COVID-19 reduced some protest/conflict activity.
Annual deaths in Papua reaching 100+. Low political violence nationally.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2020; Papua TPNPB; COVID

2021.assessment:
Papua/West Papua TPNPB escalating — attacks on security forces, workers; dozens killed.
March 2021 Makassar cathedral bombing (4 killed). NII-linked attack on police in South
Sulawesi. Annual deaths in Papua conflict 50-100+. Low political violence nationally.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2021; Papua TPNPB; Makassar bombing

2022.assessment:
Papua/West Papua TPNPB escalation continuing — New Zealand pilot held hostage; ongoing
attacks on security forces and workers. Annual Papua deaths estimated 50-150. October
2022 Kanjuruhan stadium stampede (135+ killed) — not political violence but major tragedy.
Low political violence nationally.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2022; Papua TPNPB; NZ pilot hostage

2023.assessment:
Papua/West Papua TPNPB continuing with attacks on security forces and workers. NZ pilot
freed after 1 year. Pre-election tensions. Annual Papua deaths low. Low political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2023; Papua TPNPB; pre-election

2024.assessment:
February 2024 elections; Prabowo won presidency. Papua TPNPB ongoing. Post-election
protests from some opposition. Annual deaths low nationally. Minimal political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2024; Prabowo election; Papua TPNPB

2025.assessment:
Prabowo government. Papua/West Papua TPNPB ongoing. No major national political violence
expected. Minimal political violence nationally; ongoing Papua insurgency minor.

sources.citation: UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset, Indonesia 2025; Papua TPNPB; Prabowo government

1990.assessment: Substantial territorial control with active insurgencies. New Order Indonesia controlled most territory but faced: (1) East Timor (occupied, ongoing armed resistance by Falintil); (2) Aceh (GPK/GAM insurgency — DOM military operations); (3) West Papua (OPM low-level insurgency). TNI maintained control of main population centers. Territorial control estimated ~90-95%.

sources.citation: Schulze, K. The Free Aceh Movement (GAM). 2004.

1991.assessment: Continued active insurgencies in East Timor, Aceh, and West Papua. Santa Cruz massacre in East Timor (November 12, 1991) — TNI killed ~250 mourners; major international incident. DOM (Military Operations Zone) in Aceh intensifying. GAM rebuilding after 1989 crackdown. TNI maintaining control of all provincial capitals.

sources.citation: Amnesty International. East Timor Santa Cruz Massacre 1991.
Schulze, K. The Free Aceh Movement. 2004.

1992.assessment: Insurgencies continuing at similar levels. Aceh DOM operations reduced insurgency but widespread human rights abuses. East Timor: Falintil continuing resistance. West Papua: OPM low-level. Xanana Gusmao captured November 1992 — major blow to East Timor resistance. Indonesia maintaining control.

1993.assessment: Territorial control improving slightly. Aceh DOM reducing active GAM capability. East Timor resistance weakened by Xanana's capture/imprisonment. West Papua OPM very low level. Some reconciliation efforts in East Timor. DOM Aceh generating ongoing abuses but suppressing armed insurgency.

1994.assessment: Territorial control broadly maintained. GAM at low capability level. East Timor: Falintil regrouping. West Papua: OPM activities in border areas. Indonesia still occupying East Timor (internationally recognized as occupied under UNGA 3485 and UNSC). Domestic political tensions rising (PDI-Megawati).

1995.assessment: Territorial control broadly maintained. DOM Aceh ended officially (1998 would see resurgence). GAM rebuilt from overseas (Malaysia base). East Timor: resistance continuing at low level. West Papua: mining conflict near Freeport. Domestically: Suharto's grip strong but succession question growing.

sources.citation: Freeport McMoRan. Grasberg mine security incidents 1995.

1996.assessment: Territorial control slightly reduced as tensions rose. July 1996 PDI storming (Megawati's Jakarta office) — urban unrest but not insurgency. East Timor resistance continuing. GAM rebuilding after DOM. West Papua mine incidents. Nationwide political tensions highest in years. Suharto suppressing political opposition.

1997.assessment: Territorial control declining as economic crisis created political space for resistance. GAM active in Aceh. East Timor: Falintil maintained operations. West Papua: OPM incidents. Kalimantan communal violence (Dayak-Madurese) emerging. Crisis beginning to erode security apparatus cohesion.

sources.citation: ICG. Indonesia's Crisis: Chronic but not Acute. 1998.

1998.assessment: Territorial control significantly reduced. Post-Suharto crisis: Suharto resigned May 1998. Habibie released political prisoners. East Timor: offered autonomy option (eventually referendum). Aceh: DOM lifted but GAM expanded dramatically. West Papua: OPM intensified. May 1998 riots killed ~1,000 in Jakarta/Solo. Multiple crisis zones emerging. Kalimantan communal violence.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. Indonesia: The Transition to Civilian Government. 1999.

1999.assessment: Significant territorial challenges. East Timor: referendum August 30, 1999 (78.5% for independence); post-referendum TNI/militia violence destroyed 70% of infrastructure before INTERFET arrival. Aceh: GAM controlling rural areas; post-DOM vacuum. Maluku: sectarian violence erupting (Jan 1999). West Papua: independence push growing. Indonesia losing effective control of multiple regions.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. Indonesia: Human Rights and Pro-Independence Actions. 1999.
OHCHR. East Timor Human Rights Violations 1999.

2000.assessment: Territorial control reduced but not as severe as 1999. East Timor departed (UNTAET administration). Aceh: GAM controlling rural areas; cease-fire negotiations failed; MOU attempts. Maluku: serious sectarian violence ongoing. West Papua: independence pressure. Kalimantan: Dayak-Madurese violence. Wahid government struggling to manage multiple fronts.

2001.assessment: Territorial challenges persisting. Aceh: peace talks (Geneva) collapsed; martial law approaching. Maluku: violence declining with Malino I peace accord signed. West Papua: special autonomy law enacted (Otsus 2001). Kalimantan: Dayak-Madurese violence, Sampit. Megawati presidential succession mid-year. Some improvement in outer island control.

sources.citation: ICG. Indonesia: Overcoming Papua's Special Autonomy. 2001.
Malino I peace agreement Maluku December 2001.

2002.assessment: Improving control. Maluku: Malino I effect; sectarian violence declining. Poso (Sulawesi): Malino II peace accord. Aceh: ceasefire CoHA (Cessation of Hostilities Agreement) signed December 2002. West Papua: Theys Eluay (Papua independence leader) assassinated by Kopassus November 2001. Bali bombing not territorial challenge.

2003.assessment: Deterioration with Aceh martial law. CoHA collapsed May 2003; government declared martial law in Aceh. Large-scale military offensive — 40,000+ troops. TNI asserting control but heavy civilian casualties. West Papua: OPM low-level. Maluku: declining but not zero. Jakarta Marriott bombing (not territorial). Overall control contested in Aceh.

sources.citation: ICG. Aceh: How Not to Win Hearts and Minds. 2003.

2004.assessment: Improving control. Aceh martial law — civil emergency (less intensive); military operations continuing. SBY elected; sent peace signals for Aceh. December 26 tsunami devastated Aceh — transformed political dynamics, opening space for peace negotiations. West Papua: Special autonomy partially implemented. Maluku: normalizing.

2005.assessment: Substantial improvement. Helsinki MoU (August 15, 2005) — comprehensive peace agreement ending 30-year Aceh conflict. GAM decommissioning weapons; TNI withdrawing non-organic troops. West Papua: low-level OPM continuing. Maluku, Poso: largely peaceful. Jemaah Islamiyah cells being dismantled (post-Bali investigations). Territorial control significantly improved.

sources.citation: CMI (Crisis Management Initiative). Aceh process documentation.

2006.assessment: Good territorial control. Aceh peace process advancing; Law on Governing Aceh (LOGA) enacted July 2006. Gubernatorial elections Aceh December 2006 — Irwandi Yusuf (ex-GAM) won. West Papua: OPM low-level activities. No major territorial challenges. JI network disrupted. Poso and Maluku normalizing.

2007.assessment: Good control. Aceh fully peaceful under new elected government. West Papua: periodic OPM incidents and Freeport protests. Poso: sporadic violence but declining. Makassar/Sulawesi: ethnic tensions manageable. Central Kalimantan: stable. JI network increasingly dismantled.

2008.assessment: Good territorial control. Aceh peaceful. West Papua: OPM low-level; Freeport protests periodic. No major armed challenges to state authority. Counter-terrorism operations disrupting JI cells. Poso attacks occasional. Indonesia achieving territorial consolidation from 1999-2005 crisis period.

2009.assessment: Strong territorial control. Aceh fully peaceful; successful gubernatorial and parliamentary elections. West Papua: low-level OPM. Counter-terrorism: JI leadership heavily degraded; Noordin Top killed September 2009. No significant territorial challenges. SBY re-elected; stability consolidated.

sources.citation: ICG. Indonesia's Jemaah Islamiyah network assessment 2009.

2010.assessment: Strong territorial control. Low-level insurgent activity confined to West Papua (OPM). Aceh peaceful. Counter-terrorism effective (Densus 88). Domestic terror incidents declining. No significant territorial challenges to national authority. Indonesia internationally recognized as stable democracy.

2011.assessment: Strong territorial control. West Papua: increased OPM shootings (Puncak Jaya — 'highland insurgency'). Aceh: peaceful, Irwandi government. Central Sulawesi: residual Poso networks. Counter-terrorism: Densus 88 active. No separatist movement with significant territory outside central control.

sources.citation: ICG. Papua: The Dangers of Shutting Down the Debate. 2011.

2012.assessment: Strong territorial control. West Papua: Puncak Jaya violence continuing. Aceh: new elections; PNA (Partai Aceh) won decisively. Sulawesi: residual jihadist networks. Counter-terrorism dismantling new networks (Abu Bakar Ba'asyir networks). Overall minimal territorial challenge.

2013.assessment: Strong territorial control. West Papua: OPM ('TPNPB' wing) continuing Puncak Jaya operations. Indonesian security forces reinforced in Papua. ISIS (proto-state emerging in Syria/Iraq) beginning to attract Indonesian recruits. Aceh and Maluku stable. Densus 88 active nationally.

sources.citation: ICG. Papua: Priorities for the New Government. 2014.

2014.assessment: Mostly strong control. West Papua: Puncak Jaya security operations intensive. ISIS declared; Indonesian foreign fighters joining. Jokowi elected; different approach to Papua (development-focused). Domestic terror network monitoring intensifying. Sulawesi: residual networks.

2015.assessment: Mostly strong control. West Papua: ongoing low-level violence and protests. ISIS Indonesian network (JAD — Jamaah Ansharut Daulah) forming. Jokowi opened Papua to foreign journalists (historic). Domestic jihadist networks preparing domestic attacks. Aceh: fully peaceful.

2016.assessment: Strong but challenged control. West Papua: protests intensifying with international connections (MSG observer status). January 2016 Jakarta attack by ISIS-affiliated JAD — first domestic attack in years (8 killed including attackers). Counter-terrorism tightened. Densus 88 active. Papua highland violence continued.

sources.citation: ICG. Indonesia: Terror, Security and Stabilization. 2016.

2017.assessment: Strong territorial control. West Papua: significant protests around TPNPB (armed wing OPM). JAD network expanded; multiple thwarted attacks. Bandung and other cities: isolated incidents. Jakarta gubernatorial controversy (Ahok trial) generated massive street mobilization but not territorial challenge. Revisi UU Ormas restricting foreign-linked organizations.

2018.assessment: Strong control with significant challenges. Surabaya church bombings (May 2018) — family suicide bombers (JAD) — 13 killed. Mako Brimob prison riot (JAD/JI prisoners). West Papua: TPNPB attack on Freeport construction workers killed 31 (December 2018). New Papua province divisions proposed. Counter-terrorism law strengthened.

2019.assessment: Strong control with ongoing Papua challenge. West Papua: August 2019 riots across Papua/West Papua provinces following racial insults against Papuan students in Surabaya — largest protests in decades. TPNPB attacks on security forces. Papua road construction workers attacked. Mass protests internationally covered. JAD network continuing.

2020.assessment: Strong control; COVID-19 reduced protest activity. West Papua: TPNPB attacks continuing; Intan Jaya and Pegunungan Bintang security operations. 31 Papuan civilians killed in security operations (KontraS). JAD: network disrupted by Densus 88. COVID military/police deployments for health compliance. Papua conflict intensifying.

sources.citation: ICG. Indonesia: Responding to the Papuan Crisis. 2020.

2021.assessment: Strong overall control; Papua escalating. TPNPB killed Brig. Gen. Ignjatios Loyubar (April 2021) — highest-ranking officer killed in Papua. Multiple attacks on security forces and workers. Government split Papua province into new provinces (West Papua Central, South Papua, etc.) from 2022 — 6 total provinces from 2. Makassar church suicide bombing (March 2021). JAD network ongoing.

sources.citation: ICG. Indonesia: The Papua Conflict Gets Deadlier. 2021.

2022.assessment: Strong overall; Papua escalating. New Papua provinces created (Law 14-17/2022) — 6 provinces. TPNPB expanded operations. New Zealand pilot Philip Mehrtens taken hostage February 2023 (year-end event). Multiple civilians killed in crossfire. Counter-terrorism effective elsewhere. G-20 year; Indonesia managing international image.

2023.assessment: Strong overall; Papua conflict most significant challenge. Philip Mehrtens (NZ pilot) still held hostage by TPNPB throughout 2023. TPNPB attacks on security forces and infrastructure continuing. Papua military operations (Damai Cartenz). Aceh, Maluku fully stable. Counter-terrorism monitoring JAD remnants. Pre-election security posture.

sources.citation: ICG. Papua: Resolving the Mehrtens Hostage Crisis. 2023.

2024.assessment: Strong overall. Philip Mehrtens hostage situation resolved September 2024 (released after 19 months). Papua TPNPB attacks continuing. Prabowo government taking harder line on Papua security. Counter-terrorism continuing. Elections February 2024 — Prabowo won; peaceful transition. Overall territorial integrity maintained.

2025.assessment: Strong overall territorial control. Papua conflict continuing at low-intensity level. TPNPB attacks on security forces periodic. Government development + security dual approach in Papua. Nusantara new capital construction proceeding in East Kalimantan. Aceh, Maluku, Sulawesi stable. Counter-terrorism operational. Estimated ~89% effective control.

1990.assessment:
Suharto New Order. Budget entirely controlled by Department of Finance and Bappenas
(planning agency) without meaningful public access or parliamentary scrutiny. State
budget (RAPBN) submitted to parliament (DPR) but DPR functioned as rubber stamp —
no independent analysis. No pre-budget statement. No audit institution independence
(BPK nominally existed but subservient). Estimated OBI equivalent ~10-15/100.

sources.citation: IBP pre-2006 qualitative; academic analysis of Suharto era budget process

1991.assessment:
Same as 1990. Budget process opaque. Suharto family business interests embedded in
state budget through BULOG, Pertamina, and other SOEs. No FOI law. Media restrictions
prevent investigative journalism on budget. Budget documents not publicly accessible.
Estimated ~10-15/100.

sources.citation: IBP qualitative; Suharto era budget analysis

1992.assessment:
Suharto New Order budget process: opaque, highly centralized. RAPBN presented to DPR
but parliament plays ceremonial role only. BPK (Supreme Audit Institution) reports not
public. No independent budget analysis institutions. Military (ABRI) budget off-budget
through own enterprises. Estimated ~10-15/100.

sources.citation: IBP qualitative; academic analysis Suharto budget

1993.assessment:
Unchanged. Suharto New Order budget opaque. Repelita (Five-Year Plans) provide broad
framework but actual budget execution opaque. Decentralization not yet implemented.
Central budget dominated by Jakarta with limited provincial transparency. Estimated ~10-15/100.

sources.citation: IBP qualitative; Repelita era budget analysis

1994.assessment:
Suharto era budget: scant transparency. Newspaper bans (Tempo, Editor, Detik) in 1994
further restricted public access to budget information through journalism. APEC Bogor
commitments included some transparency improvements but budget process unchanged.
Estimated ~10-15/100.

sources.citation: IBP qualitative; newspaper bans impact on budget journalism

1995.assessment:
Scant. Budget process opaque. WTO membership (1995) brought some commitment to trade
policy transparency but not budget transparency per se. IMF Article IV consultations
provided some external budget analysis but not public. Estimated ~10-15/100.

sources.citation: IBP qualitative; WTO 1995; IMF Article IV

1996.assessment:
Scant. Unchanged Suharto budget opacity. Political tensions (PDI attack, PRD arrests)
not related to budget transparency. Cronyism in budget allocations (Suharto children)
opaque. Off-budget military enterprises growing. Estimated ~10-15/100.

1997.assessment:
Scant but AFC begins requiring more transparency. Asian Financial Crisis: IMF demanded
budget transparency as condition of $43bn package. Suharto initially resisted publishing
full budget details. IMF Letter of Intent demanded disclosures of Suharto family
business-linked subsidies (BULOG, IPTN, etc.). Estimated ~10-15/100 but under pressure.

sources.citation: IMF Indonesia program 1997; Letter of Intent transparency demands

1998.assessment:
Improving from scant. Suharto resigned May 21 — Habibie government began transparency
reforms. IMF continued program with transparency conditions. Budget reform initiated:
budget documents becoming more accessible. BPK (Supreme Audit Body) reform started.
Habibie era: significant improvement in budget transparency as part of reformasi.
Estimated ~15-20/100 by year end.

sources.citation: IBP qualitative; IMF Indonesia; Habibie reformasi budget

1999.assessment:
Improving. Budget reform accelerating under reformasi. New DPR more assertive in budget
oversight. Law 25/1999 on fiscal balance between center and regions — decentralization
fiscal framework. IMF program: budget transparency requirements. Media now free to
investigate budget. Estimated ~20-25/100. Still low but major improvement from Suharto era.

sources.citation: IBP qualitative; Law 25/1999 fiscal balance; IMF Indonesia

2000.assessment:
Improving. Budget reform laws enacted: Law 17/2003 in preparation. BPK independence
increasing. DPR budget committee (Panitia Anggaran) more active in review. World Bank
PRSC (Poverty Reduction Support Credit) with governance conditions. Decentralization
creating new fiscal reporting requirements. Estimated ~25-30/100.

sources.citation: IBP qualitative; World Bank PRSC; BPK independence

2001.assessment:
Improving. Wahid impeachment shows DPR asserting budget oversight power. Megawati
government: budget reform continuing. Regional fiscal balance implemented. IBRA
(Bank Restructuring) public reporting requirements. Estimated ~25-30/100.

sources.citation: IBP qualitative; IBRA reporting; regional fiscal balance

2002.assessment:
Improving. Law 17/2003 on State Finance under preparation — major reform. Law 1/2004
on State Treasury being developed. IMF program ending but standards maintained.
BPK gaining more independence. Budget transparency improving steadily. Media investigative
journalism on budget growing (post-Suharto press freedom). Estimated ~30-35/100.

2003.assessment:
Improving. Law 17/2003 on State Finance enacted — major reform establishing performance-
based budgeting, required public budget documentation. Law 1/2004 on Treasury passed.
BPK audit reports beginning to be published. DPR budget approval process more transparent.
Pre-OBI period but significant reform. Estimated ~35-40/100.

sources.citation: Law 17/2003 State Finance; Law 1/2004 Treasury; BPK reports

2004.assessment:
Improving. SBY election brought reform-oriented government. Law 25/2004 on National
Development Planning — RPJM framework. Budget documents increasingly published online.
BPK audit reports public. DPR budget committee more assertive. Mid-term expenditure
framework. Pre-first OBI survey. Estimated ~40-45/100.

sources.citation: Law 25/2004 planning; BPK reports; SBY reform

2005.assessment:
Approaching moderate. Budget reform deepening. MTEF (Medium-Term Expenditure Framework)
being piloted. State Audit Institution (BPK) reform law. Central government financial
reporting improving. Pre-first OBS survey estimate ~45-50/100 based on trajectory
toward 2006 OBI score of 51.

sources.citation: IBP qualitative; MTEF pilot; BPK reform law 2004

2006.assessment:
OBI 51/100 — limited transparency. First OBS survey. Indonesia ranked middle tier.
Budget documents available to public but incomplete. Pre-budget statement weak. In-year
reports partial. BPK audit reports published with delay. Citizens budget absent.
Significant improvement from Suharto era but still below global standard. Limited.

2007.assessment:
OBI estimated ~51-54/100 (interpolated between 2006 score 51 and 2008 score 54).
Budget reform continuing under SBY. Performance-based budgeting piloting. APBN
(National Budget) documentation improving. Mid-year reports being developed. Gradual
improvement trajectory.

2008.assessment:
OBI 54/100 — limited transparency. Improvement from 2006. Pre-budget statement improved.
BPK annual report more timely. In-year reports developing. State finance management
modernization (FMIS). Citizens budget still limited. Mid-year review improving. Gradual
progress. Limited transparency.

2009.assessment:
OBI estimated ~52-53/100 (interpolated between 2008:54 and 2010:51). Budget reform
under SBY second term. Some setbacks in documentation completeness. Gradual reform
with some gaps. Limited transparency range.

2010.assessment:
OBI 51/100 — limited transparency. Slight dip from 2008. Century Bank scandal: DPR
investigation showed some opacity in Bank Indonesia and Finance Ministry. Some regression
in documentation completeness. Citizens budget lacking. Mid-year reporting gaps. Overall
limited — progress stalled.

2011.assessment:
OBI estimated ~55-58/100 (interpolated between 2010:51 and 2012:62). Budget reform
accelerating. FMIS national implementation. BPK reports becoming more timely and
complete. Pre-budget statement improving. KPK involvement in procurement transparency.
Upward trajectory. Limited approaching substantial.

2012.assessment:
OBI 62/100 — substantial transparency. Major improvement. Indonesia now in "substantial"
category. Budget documents widely published online. BPK audit reports timely. Pre-budget
statement published. In-year reports complete. Citizens budget emerging. DPR oversight
active. Significant reform achievement. Substantial.

2013.assessment:
OBI estimated ~62-61/100 (interpolated, slight decline toward 2015 score of 59).
Budget transparency maintained at substantial level. Some gaps in budget documentation.
KPK prosecutions of budget-related corruption. Decentralization creating sub-national
budget transparency challenges. Substantial.

2014.assessment:
OBI estimated ~60/100. Jokowi election. EITI compliance achieved — natural resource
budget transparency improving. Budget documents widely accessible. DPR assertive.
BPK reports published. Sub-national budget transparency growing with pilkada elections.
Substantial level maintained.

2015.assessment:
OBI 59/100 — limited (just below substantial threshold). Slight dip from 2012. Jokowi
government budget transparency: village fund (dana desa) transparency requirements.
Some setbacks in national documentation completeness. Citizens budget improving.
BPK audit reports timely. Pre-budget statement. Overall limited approaching substantial.

2016.assessment:
OBI estimated ~61/100 (interpolated between 2015:59 and 2017:64). Improving. Village
fund (dana desa) transparency requirements. Budget documents widely online. Tax amnesty
tax transparency. EITI compliance. Jokowi reform agenda. Improving trajectory.

2017.assessment:
OBI 64/100 — substantial transparency. Improvement. Indonesia consistently in substantial
category now. Budget documents complete and timely. BPK audit reports. Pre-budget
statement published. Citizens budget available. Mid-year reports. In-year budget
execution reports accessible online. Strong performance. Substantial.

2018.assessment:
OBI estimated ~65-66/100 (interpolated between 2017:64 and 2019:67). Improving. Budget
transparency stable in substantial range. Government Finance Statistics (GFS) improving.
EITI continued compliance. Sub-national fiscal transparency improving with pilkada cycle.

2019.assessment:
OBI 67/100 — substantial transparency. Continued improvement. Jokowi second term.
Budget documents comprehensive. BPK reports timely. Pre-budget statement complete.
In-year execution reports publicly accessible. Citizens budget published. EITI
compliant. Sub-national budget improving. KPK prosecuted procurement violations.
Substantial and improving.

2020.assessment:
OBI estimated ~67/100 (stable, approaching 2021 score of 67). COVID-19: government
reallocated budget through Presidential Regulation 54/2020 — some concerns about
oversight. COVID spending transparency: some documentation lags. Omnibus Law. Overall
maintained at substantial level.

sources.citation: IBP between surveys; COVID budget transparency concerns

2021.assessment:
OBI 67/100 — substantial transparency. Maintained level. COVID-19 budget management:
government published COVID spending reports. EITI compliant. Budget documents available.
BPK audit reports. Substantial transparency maintained despite emergency spending.
Slight concern about speed vs. transparency in COVID response.

2022.assessment:
OBI estimated ~68-70/100 (interpolated toward 2023 score of 72). Post-COVID fiscal
normalization. G20 Bali: Indonesia showcased fiscal management. IKN capital project
budget transparency concerns. EITI compliance continued. Improving trajectory.

2023.assessment:
OBI 72/100 — substantial transparency. Indonesia's highest ever score. Major improvement.
Budget documents comprehensive. BPK audit reports timely and complete. Pre-budget
statement complete. Citizens budget fully published. In-year reports accessible.
Mid-year review comprehensive. EITI compliant. Strong performance — approaching
extensive category.

2024.assessment:
OBI estimated ~72/100 (carry-forward from 2023 — OBS 2025 not yet published).
Prabowo elected — no expected major change in budget transparency framework.
EITI compliance continued. Budget institutions established. Substantial transparency
expected to continue. OBS 2025 results will confirm.

2025.assessment:
OBS 2025 not yet published. Qualitative estimate: ~70-75/100. Prabowo presidency first
year. Budget transparency framework inherited from Jokowi. EITI compliance continued.
BPK independent. Budget documents should remain accessible. Some concerns about IKN
and defense budget opacity. Substantial level expected.

1990.assessment:
Suharto New Order. Pertamina state oil company completely opaque — revenues
channeled directly to presidential discretionary funds. Production Sharing Contracts
(PSCs) with IOCs (Exxon, Caltex, Shell, Total) classified secret. No public
disclosure of oil/gas revenues. Mining contracts similarly opaque under mining law
(UU 11/1967). Coal boom beginning — contracts not disclosed. No EITI (EITI
founded 2002). Estimated revenue disclosure: near zero.

sources.citation: Global Witness Indonesia extractives reporting; Pertamina Annual Report (limited)
V-Dem Indonesia Dataset; Freedom House Indonesia 1990

1991.assessment:
Suharto New Order. No change. Oil/gas and mining revenues completely opaque.
No EITI. Pertamina and mining companies not disclosing revenues.

1992.assessment: Suharto New Order. No change in extractive transparency. Total opacity continues.

1993.assessment: Suharto New Order. No change. Extractive revenues opaque.

1994.assessment:
Suharto New Order. No change. Major nickel and coal contracts not disclosed.
Freeport McMoRan Papua gold/copper mine operating under secret Contract of Work.

sources.citation: Global Witness Indonesia; Freeport McMoRan COW documentation

1995.assessment: Suharto New Order. No change. Total opacity on extractive revenues.

1996.assessment:
Suharto New Order. Bre-X gold mining scandal in Kalimantan exposes opacity of
mining contracts — fraudulent claims enabled by lack of regulatory disclosure.
Total extractive opacity continues.

sources.citation: Bre-X scandal documentation; Global Witness Indonesia

1997.assessment:
AFC crisis year. IMF conditionality includes some demand for financial disclosure
but extractive sector specifically remains opaque. Oil revenues used for emergency
support but channels undisclosed. Bre-X fraud fully exposed March 1997.

sources.citation: IMF Indonesia Program 1997; Global Witness Indonesia

1998.assessment:
Regime change. Suharto falls May 1998. Habibie transition. IMF conditionality
demands transparency reforms. Pertamina restructuring begins discussion.
Extractive contracts still not disclosed but momentum for reform building.
Oil/gas law reform in discussion. Still no meaningful extractive disclosure.

sources.citation: IMF Indonesia LOI 1998; Global Witness Indonesia extractives

1999.assessment:
Reformasi period. New oil/gas law being drafted. Regional autonomy laws (UU 22/1999,
UU 25/1999) mandate revenue sharing for resource regions — creates partial disclosure
requirement. However PSCs and contracts still largely secret. No EITI membership.
Partial progress toward disclosure but still largely opaque.

sources.citation: UU 22/1999 and 25/1999 regional autonomy; Global Witness Indonesia

2000.assessment:
Oil/gas law reform continuing. Revenue sharing for oil/gas producing regions partially
implemented. Pertamina restructuring debated. No EITI. Contracts still secret.
Minimal disclosure improving from Suharto era but still largely opaque.

sources.citation: Global Witness Indonesia extractives; World Bank Indonesia 2000

2001.assessment:
New Oil and Gas Law (UU 22/2001) passed — separates regulatory from commercial
functions; BP Migas (upstream regulator) and BPH Migas (downstream) established
replacing Pertamina's monopoly. BP Migas begins publishing some production data.
Partial disclosure improvement but contract terms still not public. No EITI.

sources.citation: UU 22/2001 Oil and Gas Law; BP Migas establishment

2002.assessment:
EITI launched globally (2002 Johannesburg World Summit). Indonesia not yet member.
BP Migas operational; some production data published. Coal mining regulation reforming.
PSC terms still largely confidential. Pertamina restructured as state-owned company.
Partial but minimal disclosure compared to EITI standards.

sources.citation: EITI global launch 2002; BP Migas Annual Report 2002; Global Witness

2003.assessment:
EITI expanding globally. Indonesia watches but does not join. Coal exports rising
rapidly. Mining contracts under KK (Contracts of Work) system still undisclosed.
BP Migas publishing aggregate production data but individual company PSC terms secret.

sources.citation: EITI annual report 2003; BP Migas Indonesia

2004.assessment:
SBY elected. Revenue Watch Institute active in Indonesia. EITI advocacy begins
domestically. Some PSC data leaking but not official. Mining sector expanding.
Freeport Papua revenues substantial but disclosed only in US SEC filings (Freeport
listed NYSE), not to Indonesian public directly. No EITI membership.

sources.citation: Revenue Watch Institute Indonesia; Global Witness Indonesia; Freeport SEC

2005.assessment:
Global EITI expanding. Indonesia civil society pushes for EITI candidacy.
Some government interest. Coal production and exports accelerating.
BP Migas publishing annual reports with aggregate production. Still no EITI.

sources.citation: Revenue Watch Institute Indonesia 2005; EITI global expansion

2006.assessment:
Indonesia government begins formal consideration of EITI. Presidential working group
on transparency. Mining law reform (UU 4/2009) under drafting. Revenue sharing
for regions partially operational. Still no formal EITI candidacy.

2007.assessment:
Indonesia takes steps toward EITI candidacy. Presidential Regulation (Perpres)
on EITI multi-stakeholder group in discussion. BP Migas disclosure improving.
New Mining Law (UU 4/2009) finalized by parliament. Civil society engagement
on extractive transparency growing.

2008.assessment:
Presidential Regulation Perpres 26/2010 (anticipating) — EITI Indonesia multi-
stakeholder group established in preparation. Indonesia commits to EITI process.
Mining Law UU 4/2009 passed — replaces colonial-era 1967 mining law; introduces
IUP (mining business license) system replacing KK contracts (partially more transparent).
Still no formal EITI candidacy granted.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia candidacy preparation; UU 4/2009 Mining Law

2009.assessment:
UU 4/2009 Mining Law takes effect. Perpres 26/2010 on EITI being drafted.
Indonesia prepares formal EITI application. Civil society MSG (Multi-Stakeholder
Group) being established. Some IUP licensing data begins to be published.
Still technically no EITI membership yet.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia 2010 candidacy docs; UU 4/2009 implementation

2010.assessment:
Presidential Regulation Perpres 26/2010 establishes EITI Indonesia governance
(Tim Transparansi). Indonesia formally becomes EITI Candidate (2010).
Multi-stakeholder group operational. First EITI reconciliation report being
prepared. IUP (mining licenses) database being consolidated. Major step forward
in extractive transparency, but still candidate status (not compliant).

2011.assessment:
EITI candidate. First EITI Indonesia report prepared (covering 2009-2010 fiscal
data). MSG active. IUP clean-and-clear process launched. Oil/gas PSC data
partially disclosed through reconciliation. Still candidate — full compliance
validation pending.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia First Report 2011; EITI Board progress review

2012.assessment:
EITI candidate. Second EITI Indonesia reconciliation report published. Oil, gas,
and mining (coal, minerals) revenues reconciled. IUP database published online.
Revenue Watch Resource Governance Index 2013 rates Indonesia 'Partial' governance.
Candidate status maintained; compliance validation ongoing process.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia Second Report 2012; Revenue Watch RGI 2013

2013.assessment:
EITI candidate. Third EITI Indonesia report published. Validation process begins
preparation. Oil, gas, coal, minerals covered in reconciliation. IUP clean-and-
clear list publicly available. Approaching compliance validation.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia Third Report 2013; EITI validation preparation

2014.assessment:
Indonesia granted EITI Compliant status October 2014. EITI Board validation
confirms Indonesia meets all EITI Requirements. Multi-stakeholder group
(MSG/Tim Transparansi) functional. Oil/gas revenues (Pertamina, SKK Migas post-2012
BP Migas dissolution) and mining revenues fully reconciled annually.
Significant milestone in extractive transparency.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia Compliance Decision October 2014; EITI Board

2015.assessment:
EITI compliant. Annual EITI reports covering oil/gas and mining revenues. Revenue
Watch Resource Governance Index 2015 notes improvement. SKK Migas data published.
IUP database maintained. Government commitment to EITI strengthened under Jokowi.
Beneficial ownership disclosure being discussed as next step.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia 2015 Report; Revenue Watch RGI 2015

2016.assessment:
EITI compliant. Oil/gas and mining revenues fully reconciled. SKK Migas and mining
company reports published. Beneficial ownership roadmap in development. Preparing
for 2017 EITI Validation. Revenue Governance Index improving.

2017.assessment:
EITI Indonesia 2017 Validation — Indonesia rated 'meaningful progress' on most
requirements. Compliant status reaffirmed. Some corrective actions on beneficial
ownership disclosure timeline and state enterprise data completeness.
Overall strong compliance maintained.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia Validation Report 2017; EITI Board Decision

2018.assessment:
EITI compliant. Annual reconciliation reports published. Beneficial ownership
disclosure progress. Oil/gas revenues declining (production decline) but coal
revenues significant. EITI reports cover oil, gas, coal, minerals comprehensively.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia 2018 Report; SKK Migas Annual Report 2018

2019.assessment:
EITI compliant. Indonesia maintains strong compliance. Annual EITI reports published.
Beneficial ownership register development ongoing (delayed). EITI 2019 global standard
on beneficial ownership — Indonesia working toward compliance. Resource Governance
Index 2017 rated Indonesia 'Satisfactory' in oil/gas.

2020.assessment:
EITI compliant. COVID-19 impacts reporting timelines (minor delay). Annual EITI
Indonesia report published. Omnibus Law Cipta Kerja 2020 modifies some mining
regulations — simplification raises some transparency concerns. Overall EITI
compliance maintained.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia 2020 Report; EITI Secretariat COVID adaptations

2021.assessment:
EITI Indonesia 2021 Validation — rated 'meaningful progress' on most requirements,
'satisfactory progress' on some. Compliant status maintained. Corrective actions
on beneficial ownership beneficial owner disclosure (Perpres on BO register still
pending). Strong overall compliance.

2022.assessment:
EITI compliant. Coal revenues very high (commodity supercycle). Annual reconciliation
published. Beneficial ownership: Perpres 13/2018 on beneficial ownership register
partially implementing. EITI reports comprehensive for oil/gas/coal/minerals.

sources.citation: EITI Indonesia 2022 Report; Perpres 13/2018 BO implementation

2023.assessment:
EITI compliant. Annual reports published. Nickel sector increasingly important
(EV battery supply chain). EITI coverage expanding to nickel/cobalt. Beneficial
ownership register partially operational. Strong extractive transparency by
regional standards.

2024.assessment:
EITI compliant (maintained). Prabowo administration takes over October 2024.
EITI Indonesia continues operations. Annual reconciliation ongoing. Nickel sector
a major focus. BO register expanding. Strong compliance trajectory maintained.

2025.assessment:
Estimated EITI compliant status maintained into 2025. Prabowo administration
has not signaled EITI withdrawal. Indonesia's resource sector (nickel, coal, oil/gas)
continues EITI reporting. Extractive transparency significantly improved from
Suharto-era total opacity.

1990.assessment:
Suharto New Order authoritarian rule. Laws and regulations exist on paper but are
implemented arbitrarily at executive discretion. Judiciary completely subservient to
executive — judges appointed and pressured by the state. No freedom of information
law. State secrecy law widely applied. Presidential decrees and ministerial regulations
routinely override formal statutory law. Legal system characterized by KKN (corruption,
collusion, nepotism). Business regulations opaque; permits require unofficial payments.
Estimated WJP equivalent ~25-30/100.

1991.assessment:
Suharto New Order authoritarian rule continues. No change in legal transparency.
Arbitrary enforcement; judiciary subservient; no FOI framework. Estimated WJP ~25-30/100.

1992.assessment:
Suharto New Order. No substantive change. Golkar election victory reinforces status quo.
Legal framework opaque and arbitrarily enforced. Estimated WJP ~25-30/100.

1993.assessment: Suharto New Order. No change. Estimated WJP ~25-30/100.

1994.assessment:
Suharto New Order. Tempo, Editor, DeTik magazines banned — reinforces information
opacity. Government regulatory issuances often unpublished or selectively available.
Estimated WJP ~25-30/100.

1995.assessment:
Suharto New Order. No change in legal transparency. Arbitrary enforcement continues.
Estimated WJP ~25-30/100.

1996.assessment:
Suharto New Order. July 1996 PDI-P raid shows growing instability but no change in
legal transparency regime. Estimated WJP ~25-30/100.

1997.assessment:
Asian Financial Crisis begins mid-1997. Suharto governance collapsing; legal framework
increasingly irrelevant as emergency rule-by-presidential-decree accelerates. Business
regulatory environment chaotic; IMF conditionality imposed. Estimated WJP ~22-28/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 1997; IMF Indonesia Article IV 1997

1998.assessment:
Regime collapse May 1998. Suharto resigns; Habibie presidency begins transition.
Political and legal system in flux. Press freedom laws begin liberalizing (PP 20/1998
press deregulation). Initial rule-of-law improvements: some political prisoners
released, basic civil liberties recognized. But institutional capacity very weak;
judiciary unreformed. Estimated WJP ~28-33/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 1998; ICG Indonesia Rule of Law 1999

1999.assessment:
Reformasi period. June 1999 first free elections. Constitutional amendment process
begins. Press freedom law enacted (UU 40/1999). New political parties legalize.
However judiciary remains unreformed and corrupt. Regional autonomy laws passed
(UU 22/1999, UU 25/1999) — regulatory framework fragmenting across provinces.
No freedom of information law yet. Estimated WJP ~30-35/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 1999; USAID Rule of Law Program Indonesia

2000.assessment:
Wahid presidency. Constitutional amendments 2000 (3rd amendment). Anti-corruption
commission (KPK) proposed but not yet operational. Decentralization creates regulatory
complexity — thousands of regional regulations (perda) inconsistent with national law.
Judiciary still corrupt. Estimated WJP ~32-36/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 2000; World Bank Indonesia Country Assessment

2001.assessment:
Wahid impeached July 2001; Megawati takes over. Constitutional 4th amendment.
Legal system in reform phase but progress slow. Anti-corruption court legislation
debated. World Bank Doing Business starts tracking regulatory environment.
Estimated WJP ~33-37/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 2001; World Bank Doing Business 2004 (baseline)

2002.assessment:
Megawati presidency. Constitutional 4th amendment completed 2002 — direct
presidential elections mandated. KPK legislation (UU 30/2002) passed. Bali bombing
October 2002 leads to counter-terrorism law but limited impact on legal transparency.
Regulatory environment improving slowly. Estimated WJP ~34-38/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 2002; KPK founding law UU 30/2002

2003.assessment:
KPK operational from December 2003. Constitutional Court established 2003.
Anti-corruption court (Tipikor) legislated. First direct elections approaching.
Legal framework modernizing but enforcement capacity limited; corruption pervasive
in judiciary (Mahkamah Agung). Estimated WJP ~35-39/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 2003; KPK Annual Report 2004

2004.assessment:
First direct presidential election (SBY wins). KPK begins anti-corruption operations.
Constitutional Court active. Laws more systematically published. National Secretariat
(Setneg) publishes legislation. Administrative court (PTUN) functional but limited.
No FOI law yet. Estimated WJP ~36-40/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 2004; World Bank Doing Business 2005

2005.assessment:
SBY government pursues legal reform. Helsinki peace agreement August 2005 for Aceh.
KPK prosecutions of senior officials begin. Judiciary reform commissions active.
Legislation increasingly accessible online but FOI framework absent. Estimated WJP ~37-41/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 2005; World Bank Doing Business 2006

2006.assessment:
SBY era. FOI law (UU KIP) under drafting — eventually passed 2008. KPK prosecutions
ongoing (high-profile Bulog/Pertamina cases). Regulatory database improving.
Administrative procedures law absent. Estimated WJP ~38-42/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 2006; KPK Annual Report 2006

2007.assessment:
SBY era. FOI law drafting continues. JDIH (Jaringan Dokumentasi dan Informasi Hukum)
national legal database expanding. KPK convicts multiple senior officials.
Estimated WJP ~39-43/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 2007; World Bank Doing Business 2008

2008.assessment:
FOI law (UU 14/2008 on Public Information Disclosure / KIP) passed April 2008 —
major milestone. Law to take effect 2010. KPK law strengthened. Constitutional Court
active. Legal framework improving significantly but implementation capacity lagging.
Estimated WJP ~40-44/100.

sources.citation: UU 14/2008 Keterbukaan Informasi Publik (KIP); Freedom House 2008

2009.assessment:
SBY reelected. KPK weakened temporarily by 'Cicak vs Buaya' crisis (KPK vs National
Police). FOI law implementation preparation. KIP (Information Commission) being
established. Estimated WJP ~40-44/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Annual Report Indonesia 2009; KPK Annual Report 2009

2010.assessment:
FOI law (KIP) takes effect May 2010. Information Commission (Komisi Informasi Pusat)
operational. WJP Rule of Law Index begins tracking Indonesia (pilot/early data).
Legal framework significantly improved from Suharto era; JDIH national legal database
operational; regulations published. Still significant implementation gaps in judiciary.
Estimated WJP Factor 3 ~43-46/100.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Indonesia 2010 (pilot); UU 14/2008 KIP implementation

2011.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index 2011: Indonesia Factor 3 (Open Government) ~0.46/1.00 (46/100).
FOI law operational. KPK active. Laws searchable via JDIH. Information Commission
handling public information requests. Ongoing challenges: judiciary corruption,
inconsistent regulation implementation, limited court decision publication.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2011, Indonesia Country Report

2012.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.46/1.00 (46/100). Stable from 2011. Constitutional Court active.
KPK prosecutions high-profile. Regulatory environment improving (e-government
initiatives). Jakarta governor election (Jokowi). Estimated WJP ~0.46.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2012, Indonesia

2013.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.47/1.00 (47/100). Modest improvement. Open government initiatives
(OGP — Open Government Partnership: Indonesia joined 2011). E-government and open
data portals expanding. KPK active. Still: judiciary corruption, inconsistent
regional regulation.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2013; OGP Indonesia Action Plan 2013

2014.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.47/1.00 (47/100). Jokowi elected president. Open government
commitment strengthened. KPK chair criminalized (attempt to weaken it). Constitutional
Court chair arrested for corruption — ironic challenge. Ongoing legal reform.
Estimated WJP ~0.47.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2014; Freedom House Indonesia 2014

2015.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.48/1.00 (48/100). Jokowi administration open government agenda.
One-Stop Integrated Services (PTSP) expanding. National ID system (e-KTP) scaling.
Open data portal (data.go.id) active. KPK prosecutes high-profile cases. Legal
framework accessible; court decisions increasingly published.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2015, Indonesia

2016.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.48/1.00 (48/100). Jokowi e-government push. Omnibus law concept
emerging. Tax amnesty program 2016 increases fiscal disclosure. Regional elections
increasing. Islamist mobilization (212 movement) challenging pluralist legal norms.
Estimated WJP ~0.48.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2016; Freedom House Indonesia 2016

2017.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.49/1.00 (49/100). Improving trend. Presidential Regulation 95/2018
on e-government in preparation. Court decisions increasingly published online.
SIPP (court information system) expanding. KPK prosecutions ongoing. OGP active.
Ahok Jakarta conviction (blasphemy) raises rule-of-law concerns.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2017-2018, Indonesia, Factor 3

2018.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.49/1.00 (49/100). E-government regulation Perpres 95/2018 issued.
Open data initiatives. KPK active. Court information systems expanding. Pre-2019
election period.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2017-2018; Perpres 95/2018 e-government

2019.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.49/1.00 (49/100). Jokowi second term. Omnibus law on job creation
in preparation. KPK weakened by new law (UU 19/2019) — supervisory board makes
KPK part of executive branch, raising corruption concerns. Legal transparency
remains partial with significant gaps.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019, Indonesia, Factor 3

2020.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.50/1.00 (50/100). COVID-19 pandemic emergency regulations.
