Omnibus Law on Job Creation (UU 11/2020 Cipta Kerja) passed October 2020 — massive
regulatory simplification but process criticized for opacity. Constitutional Court
later rules it conditionally unconstitutional. Open data and e-government improving.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2020, Indonesia

2021.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.50/1.00 (50/100). Cipta Kerja implementation continues.
Constitutional Court rules Cipta Kerja conditionally unconstitutional (Nov 2021).
E-government systems (SPBE) expanding. Court information systems improving.
KPK reduced effectiveness post-2019 reform. Overall stable.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2021, Indonesia

2022.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.50/1.00 (50/100). Revised Cipta Kerja (Perppu 2/2022) issued.
G20 Bali Summit. Open Government Partnership commitments. Digital government
expanding. Stable legal transparency with partial gaps remaining.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2022, Indonesia

2023.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.51/1.00 (51/100). Pre-2024 election period. ASEAN chair 2023.
Digital government (GovTech) framework. Open data portal active. Court decisions
more systematically published. KPK still operational despite reduced autonomy.
Laws published in JDIH and Setneg systems.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2023, Indonesia

2024.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.51/1.00 (51/100). Prabowo elected president (February 2024).
Legal transparency framework established; open government systems operational.
KPK autonomy concerns persist. FOI law (KIP) functional — Information Commission
active. Some rollback concerns with new administration.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2024, Indonesia

2025.assessment:
Estimated WJP Factor 3 ~0.51/1.00 (51/100) based on 2024 data (2025 WJP not yet
published). Prabowo administration first year. Digital government systems operational.
FOI framework functional. Legal transparency improved significantly from Suharto era
but gaps in judicial independence and consistent enforcement remain.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2024; Freedom House Indonesia 2025

1990.assessment:
Not free. Suharto New Order: all TV channels state-owned (TVRI monopoly). Newspapers
required SIUPP (publication license) from Department of Information. Minister of
Information Ali Moertopo controlled press content. Self-censorship pervasive. Critical
reporting on Suharto family, military, or government illegal. Foreign media restricted.
Estimated press freedom score ~10-15/100.

sources.citation: Freedom House Not Free designation; academic analysis of New Order press

1991.assessment:
Not free. Santa Cruz massacre: journalists at site faced detention, film confiscated.
Some footage smuggled out by British journalist Max Stahl and broadcast internationally —
government could not suppress entirely. Retaliatory restrictions on foreign media.
Domestic press unable to report fully on massacre. Estimated ~10-15/100.

sources.citation: RSF historical; Santa Cruz media suppression; Max Stahl footage

1992.assessment:
Not free. Private TV channels (RCTI, SCTV) allowed after 1990 — owned by Suharto son
Bambang and relatives. Private media but not independent — owned by regime cronies.
Print media SIUPP required. All media practices self-censorship. Journalists arrested
for sensitive coverage. Estimated ~12-15/100.

sources.citation: RSF historical; Freedom House 1992; private TV ownership

1993.assessment:
Not free. Media landscape unchanged. Tempo magazine growing investigative capacity but
still self-censoring on core political issues. State information ministry controlling
content. Radio RRI state monopoly. No independent television. Estimated ~12-15/100.

1994.assessment:
Not free. June 1994: Tempo, Editor, Detik banned — government revoked SIUPP licenses.
Worst press freedom event of the decade. Journalists formed AJI (Alliance of Independent
Journalists) in protest — AJI declared illegal. CPJ condemned bans. Major regression.
Estimated ~8-10/100 — worst press freedom point.

sources.citation: RSF; CPJ Indonesia 1994; Tempo/Editor/Detik banned; AJI formed

1995.assessment:
Not free. Post-ban environment. Journalists more cautious. AJI operating underground.
State information ministry controlling content. Nobel Prize to Belo/Ramos-Horta covered
minimally domestically. International press reporting freely on Indonesia from outside.
Estimated ~10-12/100.

1996.assessment:
Not free. PDI HQ attack and Jakarta riots: journalists beaten, cameras confiscated.
PRD activists arrested — their publications banned. Foreign media's coverage of riots
more complete than domestic. No independent domestic reporting on sensitive events.
Estimated ~10-12/100.

sources.citation: RSF historical; CPJ 1996; journalists beaten at PDI attack

1997.assessment:
Not free. AFC: some loosening of economic reporting as crisis forced more disclosure.
Journalists reporting on economic problems with less censorship than political issues.
Student publications on campuses growing more critical. State still controlled mainstream
media. Estimated ~12-15/100.

1998.assessment:
Transforming — major improvement. H1 1998: not free (Suharto). May: reporters covering
Trisakti shootings, Jakarta riots at risk — some beaten, some covered courageously.
H2 1998 (Habibie): Ministry of Information restrictions lifted. SIUPP requirement
abolished. AJI legalized. Hundreds of new publications allowed. Major transformation.
Estimated ~15-25/100 average across year; ~25-30 by year end.

sources.citation: RSF historical; CPJ 1998; Habibie press liberalization; SIUPP abolished

1999.assessment:
Problematic improving. Press freedom massively expanded. Hundreds of new newspapers,
TV stations, radio stations launched. SIUPP abolished. No government licensing required.
Investigative journalism flourishing. East Timor: journalists still facing risks in
field. Estimated RSF raw ~75-78, inverted ~22-25/100. Problematic but vastly improved.

sources.citation: RSF historical; Freedom House 1999; media explosion post-Suharto

2000.assessment:
Difficult. Journalists reporting on Maluku, Aceh, Kalimantan violence at risk — some
killed or threatened. Inflammatory tabloid journalism fueling communal tensions.
Government press freedom maintained. Press law guaranteeing press freedom passed.
Some harassment of journalists in conflict zones. Estimated ~25-30/100.

sources.citation: RSF historical; Freedom House 2000; journalists killed conflict zones

2001.assessment:
Difficult. Press Law 40/1999 fully operational. Press Council (Dewan Pers) established.
Journalists still facing risks in Aceh, Maluku, Papua. Some threats from criminal elements.
Overall press freedom significantly improved from Suharto era. Multiple competing media.
Estimated ~28-32/100.

sources.citation: RSF historical; Press Law 40/1999; Press Council

2002.assessment:
RSF raw ~78, inverted ~22/100. Difficult. First systematic RSF ranking. Indonesia ranked
relatively low despite major improvement from Suharto era — reflects ongoing field risks.
Bali bombing October 2002 increased security pressures. Journalists in Papua, Aceh
restricted. Foreign journalists face access issues. Overall difficult.

2003.assessment:
RSF raw ~73, inverted ~27/100. Improving. Post-Bali counter-terrorism cooperation
opened some access. Aceh COHA and then military operations limited field access.
Press freedom improving in Jakarta and Java. Conflict zones remained challenging.
Overall difficult — improving.

2004.assessment:
RSF raw ~72, inverted ~28/100. Difficult. Free elections covered by press. SBY election
coverage competitive and diverse. Papua: foreign journalist access restricted.
ITE precursor laws concerning. Aceh conflict area restricted. Overall difficult with
positive trend. Strong domestic press in Java, restrictions in outer islands.

2005.assessment:
RSF raw ~68, inverted ~32/100. Improving. Helsinki MOU on Aceh: Aceh became accessible
to journalists. Major improvement in press access in Aceh region. National press vigorous.
Foreign journalist access to Papua still restricted. Overall improving. Difficult range.

2006.assessment:
RSF raw ~62, inverted ~38/100. Problematic. Significant improvement. Post-Helsinki
Aceh accessible. Free press in most of Indonesia. Papua still restricted. Some defamation
laws used against journalists. Criminal defamation provisions concern. Overall problematic
but improving toward satisfactory.

2007.assessment:
RSF raw ~56, inverted ~44/100. Problematic. Best press freedom result under SBY.
National press vigorous and investigative. Multiple competing media ownership. Online
media growing. Foreign journalists' Papua access improved somewhat. Some defamation
concerns. ITE Law under development (2008). Overall problematic — best result yet.

2008.assessment:
RSF raw ~53, inverted ~47/100. Problematic. ITE Law passed (Electronic Information
and Transactions) — criminal defamation provisions online. Press still relatively free
offline. Vigorous media landscape. Papua: some access. Aceh post-Helsinki free.
ITE law concerns for online journalists. Problematic.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2008; ITE Law

2009.assessment:
RSF raw ~52, inverted ~48/100. Problematic. ITE Law first prosecutions of bloggers.
Election coverage free and competitive. National press vigorous. Online expression
concerns growing. Papua access restrictions. Criminal defamation laws concern.
Overall problematic — reaching upper range.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2009; ITE first prosecutions

2010.assessment:
RSF raw ~55, inverted ~45/100. Problematic. Slight regression. ITE Law use increasing.
Papua: foreign journalists still restricted. Century Bank scandal: some media investigated
freely. KPK-Polri conflict covered openly. National press vigorous. Online speech
concerns. Overall problematic.

2011.assessment:
RSF raw ~57, inverted ~43/100. Problematic. ITE Law growing use against critics.
Papua: reporter access still restricted — journalists escorted or denied. Cikeusik
attack: media covered but faced some threats. National press vigorous. Concentration
of media ownership concern (Bakrie, MNC groups). Problematic.

2012.assessment:
RSF raw ~59, inverted ~41/100. Problematic. ITE Law expanding. Media concentration:
Jokowi vs. Fauzi (Jakarta election) — media bias from ownership. Papua access still
restricted. Some journalists threatened. National press competitive despite concentration.
Problematic range.

2013.assessment:
RSF raw ~60, inverted ~40/100. Problematic. Media concentration concern: Bakrie (TVOne)
vs. Surya Paloh (Metro TV) — media as political tools for party interests. Papua access
restricted. ITE law prosecutions. AJI condemning government harassment. Pre-2014
election media environment politicized. Problematic.

2014.assessment:
RSF raw ~57, inverted ~43/100. Problematic. Jokowi election: competitive coverage.
Media concentration (Surya Paloh's Metro TV pro-Jokowi, Bakrie TVOne pro-Prabowo).
Despite bias, diversity of views. Papua: foreign journalists partially allowed under
Jokowi (announced but not consistently implemented). ITE use continuing. Problematic.

2015.assessment:
RSF raw ~58, inverted ~42/100. Problematic. Jokowi partially opened Papua to foreign
journalists (announcement February 2015) but access still inconsistent. ITE law use.
Media ownership concentration. Drug executions covered freely. AJI active. Problematic.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2015; Jokowi Papua journalists

2016.assessment:
RSF raw ~57, inverted ~43/100. Problematic. Ahok blasphemy trial: media covered trial
relatively freely but self-censorship on 212 Movement criticism. ITE law: social media
critics prosecuted. Papua: access still limited despite Jokowi announcement. LGBT news
coverage suppressed. Problematic.

2017.assessment:
RSF raw ~58, inverted ~42/100. Problematic. ITE law expanding rapidly — hundreds of
cases annually. Ahok conviction covered. HTI ban criticism suppressed. Papua: continued
restrictions with some improvements. Online journalists most at risk. National media
vigorous but online speech increasingly criminal. Problematic.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2017; ITE hundreds of cases

2018.assessment:
RSF raw ~56, inverted ~44/100. Problematic. ITE law: 200+ cases per year. Papua:
journalists harassed, restricted. Meiliana blasphemy (mosque loudspeakers) case:
chilling effect on reporting religious tensions. Pre-election media vigorously reporting
campaigns. Overall problematic.

2019.assessment:
RSF raw ~60, inverted ~40/100. Problematic. Slight regression. Election coverage
free overall but social media fake news rampant. Papua internet shutdown September
2019 — worst internet freedom incident. ITE law use for political critics.
Post-election protests covered. Papua TPNPB conflict media restricted. Problematic.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2019; Papua internet shutdown

2020.assessment:
RSF raw ~56, inverted ~44/100. Problematic. COVID-19: journalists prosecuted under
ITE for criticizing COVID response. Papua: military operations, media restricted.
TPNPB violence covered limitedly. Omnibus Law protests: journalists at police lines
beaten or detained. Press coverage otherwise vigorous. Problematic.

2021.assessment:
RSF raw ~57, inverted ~43/100. Problematic. ITE law: 300+ cases. Papua: communications
blackouts, journalist access restricted. TPNPB conflict minimal coverage. COVID: some
ITE prosecutions for criticism. National press competitive. Online speech criminalized.
Overall problematic with ITE concerns.

2022.assessment:
RSF raw ~62, inverted ~38/100. Problematic. Regression. New Criminal Code December 2022:
insulting the president criminal offense — chilling effect on reporting. ITE law expanding.
Papua: TPNPB hostage, Phillip Mehrtens context — military information control. Kanjuruhan
disaster: police accountability limited despite reporting. National media competitive but
self-censorship expanding.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2022; New Criminal Code

2023.assessment:
RSF raw ~64, inverted ~36/100. Problematic. Indonesia ranked 108/180 globally.
New Criminal Code concerns (insulting president). ITE law expanding. Papua: Mehrtens
hostage — TPNPB military operations media restricted. Pre-election period: some
journalists prosecuted. AJI reported increase in attacks on journalists. Problematic.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2023, Indonesia rank 108

2024.assessment:
RSF raw ~64, inverted ~36/100. Problematic. Election year: press covered election
campaign relatively freely but access limitations. Prabowo-Gibran relationship with
media: some media self-censorship on Jokowi dynasty criticism. New Criminal Code
partially in effect. ITE law. Papua TPNPB restrictions. Problematic.

2025.assessment:
RSF 2025 not yet published. Estimate: ~60-66 raw, inverted ~34-40/100. Prabowo
presidency: expanded military role in civilian government — concerns for military
information control. ITE law ongoing. New Criminal Code implementation. Papua TPNPB.
National media competitive. Overall estimated problematic.

1990.assessment:
Pre-SCI era. BPS (Biro Pusat Statistik) operational. 1990 Population Census. SUSENAS
(National Socio-Economic Survey) annual. SAKERNAS (Labour Force Survey). GDP statistics
produced. CPI timely. Suharto era: politically influenced data (GDP growth possibly
overstated, poverty rates understated). Estimated SCI ~55-60/100.

sources.citation: BPS Indonesia historical statistics; World Bank Indonesia data archive

1991.assessment:
BPS producing regular surveys — SUSENAS annual, SAKERNAS, GDP quarterly developing.
National accounts improving. Trade statistics through Customs. Political constraints:
poverty figures and conflict-zone data (East Timor, Aceh) unreliable. Estimated ~55-60/100.

1992.assessment:
Moderate statistical capacity. Agriculture census 1993 preparation. BPS capacity
building with World Bank. East Timor and Papua statistics problematic. SUSENAS
improving. Estimated ~55-60/100.

sources.citation: BPS Indonesia; World Bank statistical assistance

1993.assessment:
Moderate. SAKERNAS quarterly developing. Regional BPS offices expanding. GDP by
sector improving. East Timor, Aceh data problematic in conflict zones. Labour market
statistics improving. Estimated ~57-62/100.

1994.assessment:
Improving. APEC Bogor commitments included data transparency. SNA 93 adoption process
began. BPS methodology international alignment. SUSENAS expanded. SAKERNAS improved
sampling. Estimated ~58-63/100.

sources.citation: BPS Indonesia; APEC data commitments; SNA 93 preparation

1995.assessment:
Good. WTO membership: trade statistics commitments. IMF SDDS subscription beginning.
GDP quarterly timely. CPI basket updated. BPS improving international comparability.
Estimated ~60-65/100.

1996.assessment:
Good statistical capacity. SUPAS (inter-census population survey). Trade statistics
through DJBC. National accounts improving. Social statistics comprehensive. Some
political constraints on poverty and inequality data. Estimated ~60-65/100.

sources.citation: BPS Indonesia; SUPAS; DJBC trade data

1997.assessment:
Good. BPS formally autonomous 1997 (via Law 16/1997). AFC: IMF requiring accurate
statistics as part of $43bn program. GDP data showed actual -13% contraction in 1998.
Some concerns about pre-crisis data manipulation. BPS independence increasing.
Estimated ~60-65/100.

sources.citation: BPS Law 16/1997; IMF AFC statistical requirements

1998.assessment:
Good. AFC: BPS producing crisis statistics. GDP -13.1% in 1998 accurately measured.
Habibie: BPS more independent from political interference. IMF program statistical
requirements improving data quality. Poverty measurement improved. Estimated ~60-65/100.

sources.citation: BPS Indonesia; AFC GDP -13.1%; IMF; Habibie independence

1999.assessment:
Good and improving. BPS more independent post-Suharto. 2000 census preparation.
National accounts revision. IMF SDDS improving compliance. Decentralization creating
regional statistical challenges. SUSENAS improving. Estimated ~63-68/100.

2000.assessment:
Good. 2000 Population Census conducted successfully — ~200M population. GDP recovery
statistics. BPS provincial network operational. Conflict zones (Maluku, Aceh) data
collection challenging. Estimated ~65-70/100.

2001.assessment:
Good. 2000 census data processing. National accounts revisions. Poverty data improving.
Labour market surveys improving. BPS Law 16/1997 operational. Estimated ~65-70/100.

2002.assessment:
Good. BPS improving international standards alignment. Agriculture census preparation.
Trade statistics improving. Poverty mapping expanding. Gender statistics developing.
Estimated ~67-72/100.

2003.assessment:
Good. Agriculture census 2003. SAKERNAS quarterly operational. SUSENAS annual.
Approaching WB SCI 2004 baseline of 73.3. GDP deflator improved. Estimated ~70-73/100.

sources.citation: BPS Indonesia 2003; agriculture census; approaching 2004 SCI

2004.assessment:
WB SCI 73.3/100 — good. BPS producing comprehensive data. GDP, CPI, labour, trade,
agriculture, population, poverty statistics all operational. SNA 93 implemented.
December 2004 tsunami: some disruption in Aceh data collection. Good capacity.

2005.assessment:
WB SCI 73.3/100 — good. Stable. Tsunami reconstruction data tracking. Aceh data
restored post-Helsinki. BPS surveys continuing. Poverty statistics improving. Good.

2006.assessment:
WB SCI 75.6/100 — good. Improving. BPS methodology improvements. SUSENAS expanding.
SAKERNAS quarterly. Agriculture census data integrated. BPS publishing online. Good.

2007.assessment:
WB SCI 75.6/100 — good. Stable. GDP quarterly improving. CPI basket update.
Trade statistics through DJBC. Business registry improving. BPS publications comprehensive. Good.

2008.assessment:
WB SCI 76.7/100 — good. GFC: Indonesia's rapid GDP statistics reassured markets.
G20 membership raising data transparency standards. BPS institutional strengthening. Good.

2009.assessment:
WB SCI 76.7/100 — good. Stable. GDP +4.5% during global recession — credibly measured.
Social statistics comprehensive. BPS online data. Good statistical capacity.

2010.assessment:
WB SCI 78.9/100 — good. 2010 Population Census. BPS national operations large-scale.
National accounts revisions. Obama Comprehensive Partnership data transparency. Good.

2011.assessment:
WB SCI 80.0/100 — excellent. First year above 80. 2010 census data integrated. GDP
revisions comprehensive. SDDS compliant. Excellent statistical capacity.

2012.assessment:
WB SCI 82.2/100 — excellent. Continuing improvement. BPS online access expanding.
Agriculture census 2013 preparation. Comprehensive data across all domains. Excellent.

2013.assessment:
WB SCI 83.3/100 — excellent. Agriculture census 2013. New base year national accounts.
Indonesia Open Data initiative. Excellent statistical capacity.

2014.assessment:
WB SCI 83.3/100 — excellent. Stable at peak. EITI improved natural resource data.
Flash GDP quarterly. Labour force comprehensive. BPS open data portal. Excellent.

2015.assessment:
WB SCI 82.2/100 — excellent. Slight dip but maintained excellent. 2010 base year
national accounts. SIPD village fund tracking. SUSENAS improved. SAKERNAS quarterly. Excellent.

2016.assessment:
WB SCI 82.2/100 — excellent. Stable. Tax amnesty financial data improvements. EITI.
Regional BPS improving. SDDS compliant. Excellent.

2017.assessment:
WB SCI 84.4/100 — excellent. New peak. Big data applications piloted. Agriculture
census 2018 preparation. SDDS. Excellent statistical capacity.

WB SCI 84.4/100 — excellent. Agriculture census 2018 conducted. BPS data portal
comprehensive. Quarterly GDP flash. Poverty improved measurement. Excellent — among
best in Southeast Asia.

2019.assessment:
WB SCI 82.2/100 — excellent. Geospatial statistics project. SUSENAS/SAKERNAS
continuing. Agriculture data integrated. BPS access improving. Excellent.

2020.assessment:
WB SCI 83.3/100 — excellent. 2020 Population Census conducted during COVID — remarkable.
BPS adapted survey methods (telephone/online). GDP -2.1% accurately measured. Excellent.

2021.assessment:
WB SCI 84.4/100 — excellent. Final year of WB SCI series. 2020 census results published.
Post-COVID surveys restored. National accounts revisions. SDDS. Excellent — among best
developing country performers globally.

2022.assessment:
WB SCI discontinued after 2021. Estimated ~84-86/100. BPS operational at high level.
G20 Bali data credible. Agriculture census 2023 preparation. Digital expansion.
Excellent statistical capacity expected to continue.

WB SCI discontinued. Estimated ~84-86/100. Agriculture census 2023 conducted.

WB SCI discontinued. Estimated ~84-86/100. Prabowo election: no expected BPS change.

2025.assessment:
WB SCI discontinued. Estimated ~84-86/100. BPS operational and independent.
